The only public declaration in VTCompressionProperties.h for the "NumberOf" family is `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfPendingFrames` (read-only, CFNumber int, available since macOS 10.8/iOS 8.0). Apple developer documentation and WWDC sessions contain no reference to this symbol.

**Sub-claim 2: "never called in any searched open-source code" — CONFIRMED.**
Exhaustive Sourcegraph keyword search across millions of indexed OSS repos returns zero call sites where this symbol is passed to `VTSessionSetProperty`, `VTSessionSetProperties`, or any other VideoToolbox API. The only appearances are in `.tbd` linker stub export tables and SDK diff tracking repos.

**The symbol itself, however, is real and correctly spelled — CONFIRMED.**
`_kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSubFrameSections` is a defined global (`S` in nm output — a data-segment CFStringRef constant), exported from `VideoToolbox.framework` on both macOS and iOS. Verified via:
- `nm /Applications/Xcode.app/.../MacOSX.sdk/.../VideoToolbox.tbd` returning `0000000000000000 S _kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSubFrameSections`
- Present in every SDK TBD from iOS 13.0 (first appearance) through iOS 26.5 / macOS SDK (Xcode 26.5). Absent in iOS 12.4 and earlier.
- Confirmed across multiple independent SDK mirror repos: xybp888/iOS-SDKs, theos/sdks, chrisharper22/sdks, okanon/iPhoneOS.sdk.

It was added alongside `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfTemporalLayers` (SVC temporal layers — also private) in iOS 13.0, and sits in the export table immediately next to `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSlices` (parallel spatial slicing). The naming pattern strongly suggests an intra-frame parallelism control (sub-frame sections = wavefront parallel processing tiles or HEVC CTU rows), but its actual semantics and whether it produces any observable effect requires binary reverse-engineering to confirm — which is beyond the scope of public source evidence.

**The core claim that it "has any observable effect" is UNCERTAIN, not refuted.** There is zero public evidence it works, but it cannot be dismissed as nonexistent or definitively inert: it is a shipped, exported, stable symbol present through 6+ years of OS releases (iOS 13 through 26). A real no-op API would typically be removed or made SPI-invisible.
sources: https://github.com/blacktop/ipsw-diffs/blob/main/18_0_22A5316k__vs_18_0_22A5326f/DYLIBS/GameCenterUICore.md#L1129 (ipsw diff showing symbol in iOS 18.0 beta)

Local Xcode 26.5 (Build 17F42): nm output from /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/VideoToolbox.tbd confirming 'S _kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSubFrameSections'
Local Xcode 26.5: VTCompressionProperties.h (macOS SDK) — zero occurrences of NumberOfSubFrameSections
Local Xcode 26.5: VTCompressionProperties.h (iOS SDK) — zero occurrences of NumberOfSubFrameSections
verdict.correction: The claim is imprecise in a load-bearing way. The accurate, corrected statement is: "kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSubFrameSections is a real, exported CFStringRef symbol in VideoToolbox.framework (present from iOS 13.0 / macOS through iOS 26.5 / macOS Xcode 26.5), but it is completely absent from all public headers and Apple documentation, and has zero known open-source call sites. Whether it has any observable effect when set via VTSessionSetProperty is unknown without binary reverse-engineering — it cannot be dismissed as inert on the basis that it is undocumented. Its placement in the export table alongside kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfSlices and kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfTemporalLayers suggests it is a private API for intra-frame parallel section control (possibly HEVC WPP / CTU rows or H.264 sub-frame slices), but this is inferential."

verifications.claim: kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceAttachments and kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceDataLength actually appear as keys in the CMSampleBuffer attachment dictionary returned by VTCompressionOutputCallback — confirmed as exported symbols but actual runtime population is unverified

verdict.claim: kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceAttachments and kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceDataLength actually appear as keys in the CMSampleBuffer attachment dictionary returned by VTCompressionOutputCallback — confirmed as exported symbols but actual runtime population is unverified
verdict.evidence: Both _kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceAttachments and _kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceDataLength are confirmed as exported symbols in the VideoToolbox binary. The macOS 26.5 SDK TBD file (current-version 3320.8.5) lists both in the exports section targeting x86_64-macos, arm64e-macos, x86_64-maccatalyst, and arm64e-maccatalyst. The iOS 26.5 SDK TBD also exports both for arm64e-ios. Live runtime dyld_info confirms both symbols are present at their respective addresses, and ctypes dereference of the live /System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/Versions/A/VideoToolbox resolves them to CFStringRef values "SliceAttachments" and "SliceDataLength" respectively. HOWEVER, a complete scan of all VideoToolbox public headers in the macOS 26.5 SDK (VTCompressionProperties.h, VTCompressionSession.h, VTSession.h, and all other VT*.h files) returns zero results for either constant — they are PRIVATE/UNDOCUMENTED API, not declared in any public header. The only kVTSampleAttachmentKey_* symbols in public headers are kVTSampleAttachmentKey_QualityMetrics (macOS 14.4+) and kVTSampleAttachmentKey_RequireLTRAcknowledgementToken (macOS 12.0+). Apple developer documentation returns 404 for both slice constants. No WWDC session, developer forum post, or open-source VideoToolbox consumer (Moonlight, Sunshine, VLC) is known to use or document runtime population of these keys in the CMSampleBuffer attachment dictionary.
sources: Live runtime dylib: /System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/Versions/A/VideoToolbox (verified via dyld_info and ctypes CFStringRef dereference)
VTCompressionProperties.h (macOS 26.5 SDK public headers — absence confirmed)
Apple developer docs for VideoToolbox sample attachment keys — returns 404 for both constants
verdict.correction: kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceAttachments and kVTSampleAttachmentKey_SliceDataLength are confirmed as PRIVATE (header-undeclared) exported symbols in the VideoToolbox binary on macOS and iOS. Their CFStringRef values resolve at runtime to the strings "SliceAttachments" and "SliceDataLength" respectively. Neither constant is declared in any public VideoToolbox header in the macOS or iOS 26.5 SDKs — they are undocumented private API. Whether VTCompressionOutputCallback actually populates these keys in the CMSampleBuffer per-sample attachment dictionary at runtime is unverified; no public documentation, WWDC session, or open-source code evidence exists to confirm it. The original claim was accurate about the "exported but unverified at runtime" characterization but critically omitted that these are private/undocumented API absent from all public headers.

rawCorpus.summary:
Network.framework (NWConnection/NWParameters) sits atop Apple's user-space networking stack — the same stack used by URLSession — and delivers approximately 30% less CPU overhead on the receive side versus BSD socket-based UDP, per the WWDC 2018 Session 715 live demo. This gain comes from eliminating the kernel→userspace data copy and context switches: the transport stack (TCP, UDP) runs in-process. For a LAN-only screen-sharing pipeline, this translates to lower variance rather than a dramatically different floor latency, because on a wired Ethernet LAN the one-way packet-delivery time is already sub-millisecond and the framework's per-call overhead is the binding constraint.

**ServiceClass / QoS plumbing (confirmed from XNU source darwin-xnu/bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c + socket.h):** NWParameters.serviceClass sets the socket-level SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE option. The full mapping is: `.background` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_BK → DSCP AF11 (10) / Wi-Fi 802.11e AC BK; `.bestEffort` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_BE → DSCP 0 (Default Forwarding); `.responsiveData` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RD → DSCP AF21 (18); `.signaling` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_SIG → DSCP CS3 (24); `.interactiveVideo` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI → DSCP AF41 (34) / Wi-Fi AC VI; `.interactiveVoice` → NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO → DSCP EF (46) / Wi-Fi AC VO. For a video stream, `.interactiveVideo` (DSCP 34, Wi-Fi AC VI, mapped to SO_TC_VI in the kernel) is the correct choice. However, a critical caveat from Apple DTS (Apple Developer Forums thread 109915) and WWDC 2016 documentation: on modern macOS, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE primarily sets Wi-Fi 802.11e User Priority (hardware QoS queue), NOT the IP-header DSCP byte. DSCP marking in the IP header applies only in specific trusted-app/Cisco Fastlane network contexts and requires MDM for generic apps on iOS. On a controlled LAN with managed switches this can still improve switch-level prioritization via 802.1p/DSCP if the AP/switch is configured to honor it. Per-packet overrides are possible via NWProtocolIP.Metadata.serviceClass, letting you mark video payload differently from a control channel on the same connection.

**Receive callback bottleneck:** NWConnection.receiveMessage delivers one datagram per callback invocation. The legacy NWUDPSession delivered arrays of datagrams per callback. Quinn "The Eskimo!" confirmed in 2019 (Apple Developer Forums thread 116500) that no batch-receive exists in NWConnection, and the batch() closure helps only the send path. In practice this means for high-packet-rate video (hundreds of ~1300-byte UDP datagrams/sec at 100 Mbps), each callback involves a GCD dispatch. The recommended mitigation is a dedicated high-priority serial DispatchQueue. For the dispatch queue QoS class, Quinn recommends `.utility` for packet-processing queues (thread 107904), not `.userInteractive` (reserved for UI events). However, for a screen-share send loop, using `.userInteractive` on the send-side queue is more defensible given the real-time constraint; receive-side is less sensitive since decoding is pipelined.

**QUIC datagrams (NWProtocolQUIC with isDatagram=true):** Available since iOS 16/macOS Ventura (added June 2022, WWDC22). Options: `isDatagram = true`, `maxDatagramFrameSize = 65535`. Advantages over plain UDP on LAN: built-in congestion-aware pacing avoids self-inflicted bufferbloat, and L4S/ECN is automatically integrated when enabled. Disadvantage: mandatory TLS 1.3 encryption adds CPU overhead (encryption + QUIC ACK processing is user-space). For a LAN application where the security boundary is physical, plain UDP with `.interactiveVideo` serviceClass adds near zero encryption overhead. QUIC datagrams make more sense if the LAN path is Wi-Fi where congestion can occur, and if L4S is active on the network. On wired GbE LAN, plain UDP likely offers marginally lower and more consistent one-way latency because it has no congestion controller to interfere.

**L4S integration:** Automatic for HTTP/3 and QUIC in Network.framework (no code changes). For custom UDP: mark packets with NWProtocolIP.Metadata.ecn = .ect1 (ECT(1) is the L4S codepoint per RFC 9331) and implement a scalable congestion control algorithm that responds to CE marks. Enable on test devices with `defaults write -g network_enable_l4s -bool true`. iOS 17/macOS Sonoma started a gradual production rollout. WWDC23 demo showed tail RTT reduction from 45ms to under 25ms (50% improvement) under load, with near-zero packet loss. For a private LAN, the network bottleneck must itself support L4S AQM — a standard Wi-Fi AP without L4S AQM firmware will not provide L4S benefits even if endpoints are configured.

techniques.name: serviceClass = .interactiveVideo on NWParameters
techniques.how: Set `parameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo` before creating NWConnection or NWListener. Maps to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI → SO_TC_VI in kernel (XNU in_tclass.c), DSCP AF41 (34) in Fastlane/Cisco networks, Wi-Fi 802.11e AC VI queue (access category for video). The kernel uses this to select the correct egress queue on the local interface.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — on Wi-Fi it places packets in the AC_VI queue (ahead of AC_BE/AC_BK), reducing queuing delay at the radio scheduler; on wired LAN it sets DSCP 34 which managed switches can use for priority forwarding. No impact on raw propagation latency. Critical for multi-client Wi-Fi scenarios.

techniques.name: Per-packet serviceClass override via NWProtocolIP.Metadata
techniques.how: Create `NWProtocolIP.Metadata()`, set `.serviceClass = .signaling` or `.interactiveVideo`, wrap in `NWConnection.ContentContext(identifier:metadata:)`, pass to `connection.send(content:contentContext:isComplete:completion:)`. Lets you mark control/IDR request packets as `.signaling` (NET_SERVICE_TYPE_SIG → CS3/CS5) and video datagrams as `.interactiveVideo` (AF41) on the same UDP flow.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — avoids head-of-line queuing at the interface between IDR requests (latency-critical, small) and large video frames (throughput-oriented)

techniques.name: connection.batch {} for multi-datagram sends
techniques.how: Wrap multiple `connection.send()` calls inside `connection.batch { ... }`. The framework coalesces them into a single kernel context switch, reducing syscall overhead when sending several small packets (e.g., one video slice chunk + one FEC packet + one timing packet) in one logical frame delivery.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — eliminates N-1 extra context switches per batch; WWDC18 quoted 30% CPU overhead reduction for user-space UDP vs kernel sockets overall, batch send is a primary contributor on the send path

techniques.name: Dedicated serial DispatchQueue at .userInteractive QoS for send path
techniques.how: Pass `DispatchQueue(label: "video.send", qos: .userInteractive)` to `connection.start(queue:)`. The queue QoS governs thread scheduling priority for callbacks and send completions. Apple DTS recommends `.utility` for NetworkExtension packet processing, but `.userInteractive` is appropriate for the actual real-time video encode→send loop where frame deadline is sub-millisecond.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — ensures the send callback thread is not preempted by background tasks; on Apple Silicon with performance and efficiency cores, a `.userInteractive` thread stays on P-cores

techniques.name: allowFastOpen = true with idempotent initial send
techniques.how: Set `parameters.allowFastOpen = true` then call `connection.send(content: initialData, contentContext: .defaultMessage, isComplete: false, completion: .idempotent)` before `connection.start(queue:)`. For UDP, 'fast open' means the connection is considered established immediately (no handshake); for the reconnection scenario it eliminates 0 RTT overhead. `.idempotent` tells the stack the data can be safely replayed.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal for UDP on LAN (UDP has no handshake anyway); more relevant if switching to QUIC datagrams where it saves the 1-RTT TLS handshake on reconnect via 0-RTT session resumption

techniques.name: requiredInterfaceType / prohibitedInterfaceTypes to lock to LAN interface
techniques.how: Use `parameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wiredEthernet` for Ethernet-only, or `parameters.prohibitedInterfaceTypes = [.cellular, .loopback]` to restrict to Wi-Fi+Ethernet. This prevents the connection from falling back to a higher-latency path and eliminates Happy Eyeballs probing on non-LAN interfaces.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — prevents accidental cellular fallback (RTT ~30-80ms vs <1ms wired); also eliminates the path evaluation delay (~50-200ms) that can occur when multiple interfaces are candidates

techniques.name: multipathServiceType = .disabled (no MPTCP) on LAN
techniques.how: Do NOT set multipathServiceType to .handover or .interactive on UDP parameters (it applies only to TCP anyway). For QUIC: `parameters.multipathServiceType = .disabled` ensures a single path is used, avoiding multipath probing overhead on LAN where a single interface is optimal.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — eliminates path-probing packets and scheduler complexity on LAN; on a single-interface LAN this is the default behavior

techniques.name: QUIC datagrams (NWProtocolQUIC.Options with isDatagram=true) for congestion-aware unreliable transport
techniques.how: Create `let options = NWProtocolQUIC.Options(alpn: ["myapp-v1"]); options.isDatagram = true; options.maxDatagramFrameSize = 1200; let params = NWParameters(quic: options); params.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo`. Peer must also advertise datagram support. Check `NWProtocolQUIC.Metadata.usableDatagramFrameSize` at runtime to confirm path MTU. Available iOS 16+/macOS 13+.
techniques.latency_impact: high on congested Wi-Fi LAN — QUIC's congestion control prevents self-inflicted bufferbloat that can add 10-100ms queuing delay. On uncongested wired LAN, adds ~0.1-0.5ms overhead vs plain UDP due to encryption + user-space ACK processing. L4S integration is automatic when enabled.

techniques.name: L4S / ECN opt-in for custom UDP via NWProtocolIP.Metadata.ecn
techniques.how: Set `ipMetadata.ecn = .ect1` on outgoing video datagrams (ECT(1) = L4S codepoint per RFC 9331). Implement scalable congestion control (e.g., Prague/CUBIC-v2 variant) that responds to incoming CE marks. Check received `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.ecn` to detect congestion signals. Enable L4S on device: `defaults write -g network_enable_l4s -bool true`. For QUIC, L4S is automatic — no code change needed.
techniques.latency_impact: high under load — WWDC23 demo showed tail RTT drop from 45ms to <25ms (50% reduction) with near-zero packet loss under congestion. On an uncongested LAN, benefit is minimal. Requires L4S-capable AQM at the network bottleneck (typically the Wi-Fi AP).

techniques.name: Use receiveMessage() in a persistent chain (not a loop) on a serial queue
techniques.how: In the completion handler of `connection.receiveMessage(completion:)`, process the datagram then immediately call `connection.receiveMessage(completion:)` again. This chains callbacks without queuing unbounded async reads. Run on a dedicated serial queue to serialize processing and avoid GCD thread explosion at high packet rates. Do NOT call receiveMessage in a tight loop before the previous one completes.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — incorrect usage (calling receiveMessage in a loop) creates unbounded queued reads that add unpredictable latency; correct chaining minimizes the callback→decode pipeline depth to 1 datagram

techniques.name: Query maximumDatagramSize and stay below path MTU
techniques.how: After connection reaches `.ready` state, read `connection.maximumDatagramSize`. On a typical Ethernet LAN this returns 1472 bytes (1500 MTU - 20 IP - 8 UDP). Fragment video slices at the application layer to fit within this limit. Sending larger datagrams triggers IP fragmentation, which doubles packet loss probability per fragment and adds reassembly latency.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — IP fragmentation can add 1-5ms of reassembly jitter and doubles loss probability, both catastrophic for low-latency video

techniques.name: NWProtocolUDP.Options preferNoChecksum = true for trusted LAN
techniques.how: Create `let udpOptions = NWProtocolUDP.Options(); udpOptions.preferNoChecksum = true; let params = NWParameters(dtls: nil, udp: udpOptions)`. Disables UDP checksum computation when the platform supports it. On a wired LAN, checksum offload is typically handled by the NIC already; this option hints the stack to skip the software fallback.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — saves ~1-5 microseconds of CPU per packet in software checksum path; on modern Apple Silicon with hardware checksum offload this is effectively zero benefit

techniques.name: shouldCalculateReceiveTime for hardware-timestamped latency measurement
techniques.how: Set `parameters.shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true` (or equivalent on the connection). Access `NWConnection.ContentContext.receiveTime` (a DispatchTime) when processing received packets. This is the Network.framework equivalent of SO_TIMESTAMP and provides hardware-stamped receive times for accurate one-way delay measurement without adding latency.
techniques.latency_impact: zero — measurement-only feature; provides accurate kernel-level timestamps needed to characterize actual glass-to-glass pipeline latency

techniques.name: BSD socket fallback (sendmsg/recvmsg) for maximum throughput UDP
techniques.how: For highest-throughput scenarios, use POSIX sockets with `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)` + `setsockopt(SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI)` + `sendmsg()`/`recvmsg()`. Enables `recvmmsg()`-style batching (multiple datagrams per syscall) not available via NWConnection. Apple TN3151 explicitly endorses BSD sockets as the fallback when Network.framework does not support a use case.
techniques.latency_impact: high for receive throughput — eliminates the single-datagram-per-callback limitation of NWConnection.receiveMessage (reported 2x slower vs NWUDPSession); for latency-per-packet the difference is smaller but receive batching improves CPU efficiency and reduces scheduling jitter

key_settings: parameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo  // NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI → DSCP AF41(34), Wi-Fi AC_VI queue
parameters.allowFastOpen = true
parameters.multipathServiceType = .disabled  // no MPTCP/multipath on LAN
parameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wiredEthernet  // or use prohibitedInterfaceTypes to exclude cellular
parameters.includePeerToPeer = false  // default; AWDL introduces 30-50ms periodic jitter
udpOptions.preferNoChecksum = true  // NWProtocolUDP.Options
quicOptions.isDatagram = true; quicOptions.maxDatagramFrameSize = 1200  // NWProtocolQUIC.Options for QUIC datagram mode
NWProtocolIP.Metadata().serviceClass = .interactiveVideo  // per-packet override for video datagrams
NWProtocolIP.Metadata().ecn = .ect1  // L4S ECT(1) codepoint for scalable CC integration
defaults write -g network_enable_l4s -bool true  // device-level L4S enable (also available in Developer Settings)
DispatchQueue(label: "video.send", qos: .userInteractive)  // high-priority send queue
connection.shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true  // hardware timestamps for latency measurement
connection.maximumDatagramSize  // query path MTU; stay below (typically 1472 on Ethernet)
latency_numbers: 30% less CPU overhead on receive side, Network.framework user-space UDP vs BSD socket UDP — WWDC 2018 Session 715 live demo
20-80ms device-to-device variability reported for NWConnection TCP on real iOS devices (Wi-Fi) vs 0-3ms on macOS simulator — Apple Developer Forums thread/736689
45ms tail RTT reduced to <25ms (50% improvement) with L4S under congestion — WWDC23 session 10004 screen-sharing demo
~50% request completion time reduction for HTTP/3 vs HTTP/1 — WWDC22 session 10078
2x slower throughput NWConnection.receiveMessage (single datagram/callback) vs NWUDPSession (array/callback) — Apple Developer Forums thread/116500 (DTS confirmed 2019)
1472 bytes typical maximumDatagramSize on Ethernet LAN (1500 MTU - 20 IP header - 8 UDP header) — Apple Developer Forums thread/118609
QUIC datagram support added: iOS 16 / macOS 13 Ventura (June 2022 beta) — Apple Developer Forums thread/685976
claims_to_verify: NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo maps to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI in the current Network.framework implementation — confirmed from Swift interface (xybp888/iOS-SDKs armv7.swiftinterface) showing the enum, but the exact NET_SERVICE_TYPE integer mapping is inferred from XNU socket.h + in_tclass.c (darwin-xnu main branch); the private Network.framework source is not public so the mapping could have changed

SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE sets Wi-Fi 802.11e UP but NOT IP-header DSCP on modern macOS — stated by Quinn (Apple DTS) in thread/109915 and referenced in WWDC 2016; verify this still holds on macOS 14+ Sonoma because Apple may have changed policy for local/LAN connections

QUIC datagram TLS encryption overhead on LAN is ~0.1-0.5ms — this is an estimate synthesized from general QUIC user-space overhead data; no Apple Silicon-specific LAN measurement was found. Verify with local benchmarking.

NWConnection.receiveMessage delivers only one datagram per callback (2x slower than NWUDPSession) — confirmed by DTS engineer Quinn in 2019 (thread/116500), but this is 6+ years old; verify whether batch receive was added in iOS 16+/macOS 13+ updates to Network.framework
L4S ECT(1) is the correct codepoint for scalable CC — confirmed by RFC 9331; but verify that Apple's Network.framework L4S implementation specifically uses ECT(1) and not ECT(0) as its SCE marker

NWProtocolUDP.Options.preferNoChecksum is the only available UDP option (confirmed from Swift interface showing only one property); verify no additional options were added in iOS 16-18 / macOS 13-15 SDK updates
open_questions: Does the new WWDC 2025 NetworkConnection API (iOS/macOS 26) expose batch-receive for UDP datagrams, addressing the longstanding 1-datagram-per-callback limitation of NWConnection?

On a private LAN (wired Ethernet, no internet), does enabling L4S via `network_enable_l4s` provide any benefit without an L4S-capable AQM at the switch/AP? (Apple's docs focus on internet/Wi-Fi scenarios)
Is there an undocumented way to get multiple datagrams per receive callback in current Network.framework, short of reverting to NWUDPSession (deprecated) or BSD sockets?

What is the actual processing latency added by NWConnection vs a raw `recvmsg()` syscall for a single 1200-byte UDP datagram on Apple Silicon (M-series)? No public benchmark was found; needs direct measurement.

For the single-window sharing use case (non-AWDL, infrastructure Wi-Fi or Ethernet LAN), does setting `includePeerToPeer = false` (default) provide any measurable latency benefit vs explicitly setting it true?

Does NWProtocolQUIC enforce a minimum RTT probe before sending data (similar to TCP slow start) that would add latency on first connect on LAN? Relevant because QUIC's handshake on LAN could be <1ms but congestion controller might still impose a ramp-up delay.

verifications.claim: NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo maps to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI in the current Network.framework implementation — confirmed from Swift interface (xybp888/iOS-SDKs armv7.swiftinterface) showing the enum, but the exact NET_SERVICE_TYPE integer mapping is inferred from XNU socket.h + in_tclass.c (darwin-xnu main branch); the private Network.framework source is not public so the mapping could have changed

verdict.claim: NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo maps to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI in the current Network.framework implementation — confirmed from Swift interface (xybp888/iOS-SDKs armv7.swiftinterface) showing the enum, but the exact NET_SERVICE_TYPE integer mapping is inferred from XNU socket.h + in_tclass.c (darwin-xnu main branch); the private Network.framework source is not public so the mapping could have changed
verdict.evidence:
Three independent findings weaken or partially refute specific sub-claims, while the core NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI connection is structurally plausible but unverifiable from public sources:

1. NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI = 3 is confirmed. Local macOS 26.5 SDK header at /Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/SDKs/MacOSX26.5.sdk/usr/include/sys/socket.h (line 280) and darwin-xnu main branch (bsd/sys/socket.h line 439) both define NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI = 3 /* Interactive Video */. darwin-xnu/bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c confirms sotc_by_netservicetype[NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI] = SO_TC_VI and DSCP mapping to _DSCP_AF41.

2. The Swift interface does NOT confirm the integer mapping. The xybp888/iOS-SDKs armv7.swiftinterface (and arm64e variant, iOS 13.0) exposes only named enum cases (bestEffort, background, interactiveVideo, interactiveVoice, responsiveData, signaling) with no raw integer values. It cannot be used to confirm what NET_SERVICE_TYPE integer the enum maps to at the socket level.

3. A distinct, separate enum exists at the C layer. The public Network.framework header parameters.h (MacOSX26.5.sdk, line 789) defines nw_service_class_t as its own enum: nw_service_class_best_effort=0, nw_service_class_background=1, nw_service_class_interactive_video=2, nw_service_class_interactive_voice=3, nw_service_class_responsive_data=4, nw_service_class_signaling=5. This is a 0–5 enum completely distinct from the NET_SERVICE_TYPE_* values (0–8). The integer 2 in nw_service_class_t is NOT the same as NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI=3. The private translation layer between them is not in any public header.

4. The claim's embedded QoS description is wrong. The claim parenthetically attributes to interactiveVideo the characteristics "inelastic flow, and constant packet rate" — but that is the description of NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO (Interactive Voice, value 4) per socket.h. NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI's actual description is "elastic flow, constant packet interval, variable rate & size. E.g. video telephony."

5. The core inference (that .interactiveVideo ultimately sets SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE=NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI=3 on the socket) is structurally plausible given matching names, but the translation from nw_service_class_interactive_video=2 to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI=3 happens in private Network.framework code that is not publicly available. No public header or open-source file documents this translation table.
verdict.correction: The claim has two inaccuracies and one overstatement of its citation. (1) The Swift swiftinterface does NOT confirm the NET_SERVICE_TYPE integer mapping — it only shows named enum cases with no raw values. (2) The embedded description "inelastic flow, and constant packet rate" is NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO (voice, value 4), not NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI (video, value 3); the correct NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI description is "elastic flow, constant packet interval, variable rate and size." (3) There is a separate public C-level enum nw_service_class_t in parameters.h where nw_service_class_interactive_video = 2 (not 3) — this is NOT NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI; the translation from nw_service_class_t to NET_SERVICE_TYPE_* lives in private Network.framework code and is not publicly documented. A corrected statement: "NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo is named 'Interactive Video' consistent with NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI (= 3) in socket.h, and the mapping is structurally plausible, but cannot be confirmed from public sources. The public C-level identifier nw_service_class_interactive_video has raw value 2 in Network.framework's own nw_service_class_t enum — a completely separate namespace from NET_SERVICE_TYPE_*. No public header or open-source file documents the translation table from nw_service_class_t to SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE. The swiftinterface exposes only named enum cases and cannot be used to infer the socket-level integer. NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI's actual characteristics are elastic flow / constant packet interval / variable rate and size (video telephony), not inelastic flow / constant packet rate (which is NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO / voice)."

verifications.claim: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE sets Wi-Fi 802.11e UP but NOT IP-header DSCP on modern macOS — stated by Quinn (Apple DTS) in thread/109915 and referenced in WWDC 2016; verify this still holds on macOS 14+ Sonoma because Apple may have changed policy for local/LAN connections

verdict.claim: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE sets Wi-Fi 802.11e UP but NOT IP-header DSCP on modern macOS — stated by Quinn (Apple DTS) in thread/109915 and referenced in WWDC 2016; verify this still holds on macOS 14+ Sonoma because Apple may have changed policy for local/LAN connections
verdict.evidence:
Multiple independent authoritative sources confirm the claim, with no evidence of a policy change for macOS 14+ Sonoma on LAN/local connections.

**1. Quinn "The Eskimo!" (Apple DTS), forums thread/109915 (Oct 2018):** Explicitly stated that QoS requirements from SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE are "reflected at the Wi-Fi level rather than via DSCP" on macOS, referencing WWDC 2016 "Networking for the Modern Internet." The thread also notes that DSCP-not-appearing in tcpdump on macOS 10.14 is "expected."

**2. WWDC 2016 "Networking for the Modern Internet":** Documented that Wi-Fi-level QoS marking (802.11 User Priority / WMM) is supported on all Apple devices, while IP-layer DSCP QoS marking only applies to iOS and only within specific Cisco Fastlane networks.

**3. XNU kernel source (darwin-xnu, main branch — the most recent public release):**
- `bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c`: `set_packet_qos()` writes DSCP to the IP header ONLY when the outgoing interface has `IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED` set AND `if_qosmarking_mode` is `IFRTYPE_QOSMARKING_FASTLANE` (1) or `IFRTYPE_QOSMARKING_RFC4594` (2). For all other interfaces (plain Ethernet, plain Wi-Fi without Cisco Fastlane), the function falls through without touching the IP TOS byte.
- `bsd/net/dlil.c` line 7374: `IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED` is set on a Wi-Fi infrastructure interface only when `net_qos_policy_wifi_enabled != 0`. That sysctl **defaults to 0** in the kernel source (in_tclass.c line 120) and is only set non-zero by a userspace daemon on a Cisco Fastlane-capable network.
- `bsd/net/if.h` lines 511-513: `IFRTYPE_QOSMARKING_RFC4594 = 2` is annotated "supported: channel only" (not socket), meaning regular BSD sockets never get DSCP written via this path.
- `ip_output.c` lines 1520-1532: DSCP is written into `ip->ip_tos` only when `set_packet_qos()` changes the value; the comment at in_tclass.c line 1769 explicitly says "When on a Fastlane network, IP_TOS/IPV6_TCLASS are no-ops" — user-set IP_TOS values are overridden by the kernel's QoS map on Fastlane, and on non-Fastlane interfaces, the kernel doesn't touch the TOS byte from SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE at all.
- `in_tclass.c` line 1819: For Wi-Fi infra interfaces outside the Fastlane path, the code reads the DSCP back from the packet and uses it to promote the **mbuf service class** (L2/L1 WMM UP priority) — confirming that the Wi-Fi QoS UP path is operative even when IP-layer DSCP is not being written.

**4. For plain LAN (Ethernet) — no change in macOS 14+:** There is no Ethernet-specific DSCP marking path in set_packet_qos() for sockets at all. The XNU source contains no platform guard or OS-version gate that would make this behave differently on macOS 14 Sonoma versus earlier releases. The kernel structure is unchanged from macOS 11+ era public sources.

**5. What does work for DSCP on macOS (any interface including LAN):** Setting `IP_TOS` / `IPV6_TCLASS` directly via setsockopt() or sending per-packet cmsg does write the DSCP bits into the outgoing IP header on macOS — the XNU path does not strip these. The Quinn thread and community posts (Kentzo, Jan 2022) confirm this explicitly: "You can set Layer 3 QoS via the DSCP field of an IP frame either by setting IP_TOS directly." So DSCP can be written on macOS on any interface — just not *via SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE* (which only drives L2 WMM on Wi-Fi and requires Fastlane for IP-layer marking).
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/109915 — Quinn DTS, Oct 2018: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE on macOS drives Wi-Fi UP, not IP-header DSCP
https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c — XNU kernel, set_packet_qos() lines 1744-1842, net_qos_policy_wifi_enabled default=0 lines 120-122
https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/bsd/net/dlil.c — dlil.c lines 7374-7388: IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED gated on net_qos_policy_wifi_enabled!=0
https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/bsd/netinet/ip_output.c — ip_output.c lines 1520-1532: DSCP written only after set_packet_qos() changes the value
https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2016/714/ — WWDC 2016 'Networking for the Modern Internet': Wi-Fi QoS all devices, DSCP only iOS+Cisco
verdict.correction: The core claim is confirmed. One precision improvement: the claim says SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE sets Wi-Fi 802.11e UP but not IP-header DSCP. This is accurate with one nuance — IP-header DSCP *can* be written by SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE on macOS, but only when the interface has Cisco Fastlane (IFRTYPE_QOSMARKING_FASTLANE) enabled, which requires a Cisco-capable Wi-Fi network and an MDM/system policy enabling it (net_qos_policy_wifi_enabled sysctl set non-zero by configd). On a plain LAN (wired Ethernet or consumer Wi-Fi without Fastlane), SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE drives only L2 WMM/802.11e UP on Wi-Fi and does nothing for IP-header DSCP. The workaround for guaranteed DSCP in the IP header on any macOS interface (including LAN Ethernet) is to use IP_TOS / IPV6_TCLASS setsockopt() or per-packet cmsg directly — those write through unconditionally.

verifications.claim: QUIC datagram TLS encryption overhead on LAN is ~0.1-0.5ms — this is an estimate synthesized from general QUIC user-space overhead data; no Apple Silicon-specific LAN measurement was found. Verify with local benchmarking.

verdict.claim: QUIC datagram TLS encryption overhead on LAN is ~0.1-0.5ms — this is an estimate synthesized from general QUIC user-space overhead data; no Apple Silicon-specific LAN measurement was found. Verify with local benchmarking.
verdict.evidence:
The claim is self-disclaimed as an estimate, and the research confirms this accurately: no authoritative source provides an Apple Silicon-specific LAN measurement of QUIC datagram TLS encryption overhead in milliseconds.

What the evidence DOES establish, from multiple sources:

1. **Cryptographic throughput baseline (Apple M1/M2 AES-GCM)**: OpenSSL benchmarks show Apple M1 achieves ~4.5 GB/s for AES-128-GCM on large blocks (8K+), and M2 reaches ~10.9 GB/s. wolfSSL "tuned" mode shows 1.74 GB/s encrypt on M1 (earlier less-optimized builds). At 4.5 GB/s, encrypting a 1400-byte MTU packet takes roughly **0.3 microseconds (0.0003 ms)** — making pure AES-GCM cipher latency negligible at LAN scale. Sources: [wolfSSL M1 benchmark](https://www.wolfssl.com/cryptographic-benchmarks-new-apple-m1/), [OpenSSL speed gist](https://gist.github.com/hnakamur/8a90475c7e0a6693bdaa4b665095bab0).

2. **QUIC user-space overhead is the dominant overhead, not cipher math**: Multiple papers confirm the bottleneck is user-space packet processing (context switches, ACK generation, kernel↔userspace copies), not AES-GCM computation. The [Fastly QUIC CPU study](https://www.fastly.com/blog/measuring-quic-vs-tcp-computational-efficiency) found unoptimized QUIC throughput at only 40% of TLS/TCP due to per-packet kernel routing and ACK costs — none of which is attributable to TLS encryption itself. The ["QUIC is not Quick Enough" paper (arxiv:2310.09423)](https://arxiv.org/html/2310.09423v2) measured QUIC RTT of 16.2 ms vs HTTP/2 RTT of 1.9 ms on a high-speed link — but this is dominated by user-space processing overhead (231K netif_receive_skb calls), not encryption latency.

3. **QUIC datagram real-time network paper (ACM RTNS 2024)**: [ACM doi:10.1145/3696355.3699698](https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3696355.3699698) is the only paper focused on QUIC DG (RFC 9221) latency vs UDP and DTLS in a real-time context. It reports "QUIC DG introduces a small delay, less significant than the delay incurred when switching the programming language used" — qualitatively confirming low overhead but providing no specific sub-millisecond numbers. The paper compared 99.9th-percentile one-way delay for a smart-grid 5 ms latency budget, implying QUIC DG fits within that budget.

4. **The 0.1–0.5 ms range is plausible but unconfirmed**: At 4+ GB/s AES-GCM throughput on Apple Silicon, per-packet cipher overhead is sub-microsecond (<<0.1 ms). The 0.1–0.5 ms figure most likely reflects total QUIC user-space processing overhead (packet framing, encryption, header protection, syscall) rather than just TLS cipher work. This range is consistent with general user-space network stack latency on modern hardware but no LAN-specific Apple Silicon QUIC measurement was found in any published source.

5. **No Apple Network.framework QUIC latency numbers exist in public docs**: Apple's WWDC sessions on QUIC (WWDC18, WWDC21, WWDC22, WWDC25) discuss qualitative benefits only — no published latency overhead figures for NWProtocolQUIC vs raw UDP on Apple Silicon. Sources: [WWDC21 QUIC session](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2021/10094/), [NWProtocolQUIC docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/network/nwprotocolquic).

**Critical distinction the claim conflates**: "TLS encryption overhead" (AES-GCM cipher: sub-microsecond on Apple Silicon HW) vs "QUIC datagram total per-packet overhead" (user-space processing + encryption + header protection + syscall: plausibly 0.1–0.5 ms). The claim labels it "TLS encryption overhead" but the actual driver is user-space processing cost.
verdict.correction: The 0.1–0.5 ms figure is an unanchored estimate for total QUIC datagram per-packet overhead (user-space processing + framing + encryption + header protection + syscall), not specifically "TLS encryption overhead." The pure AES-GCM cipher cost on Apple Silicon is sub-microsecond (<0.001 ms) at 4–11 GB/s hardware-accelerated throughput for a 1400-byte packet, and is not the dominant latency contributor. The dominant overhead is QUIC's user-space processing (kernel↔userspace copies, ACK handling, header protection). The 0.1–0.5 ms range for total QUIC datagram overhead on a modern userspace stack is plausible but unvalidated by any Apple Silicon-specific or Network.framework-specific LAN benchmark in published literature. Local benchmarking with Instruments or a custom microbench comparing NWConnection QUIC datagram send-to-send vs plain UDP send-to-send is the only way to get an authoritative number for this specific stack.

rawCorpus.summary:
BSD sockets on macOS give full POSIX UDP with several Apple-specific extensions. The key QoS primitive is `SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE` (socket option `0x1116`), which controls Layer-2/Wi-Fi 802.11 User Priority scheduling. For video streaming use `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI` (value 3, "Interactive Video" — explicit comment in XNU `socket.h`: "E.g. video telephony") or `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV` (5, "Responsive Multimedia A/V — E.g. screen sharing"). For LAN-only use, always pair with `IP_TOS`/`IPV6_TCLASS` carrying a DSCP value (e.g., AF41 = dscp 34, shifted to TOS field as `34<<2 = 0x88`). On macOS 10.14+, `SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE` silently succeeds but DSCP may not appear in Wireshark packet captures; the actual effect is link-layer Wi-Fi prioritization. The production reference implementation is Sunshine's macOS `misc.mm`, which calls `setsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI)` for video and `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO` for audio, with an optional fallback to `IP_TOS`/`IPV6_TCLASS` for DSCP tagging on the same socket. There is no `SO_PRIORITY` (Linux-only) and no `SO_BUSY_POLL` equivalent on Darwin. The macOS equivalent of Linux's `SO_BINDTODEVICE` is `IP_BOUND_IF` (IPv4) / `IPV6_BOUND_IF` (IPv6), binding the socket to an interface by index from `if_nametoindex()`.

For batch UDP: macOS has its own private batch syscalls `sendmsg_x` (syscall 481) and `recvmsg_x` (syscall 480) operating on `struct msghdr_x` arrays, confirmed in [XNU `syscalls.master`](https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/kern/syscalls.master). These are stable at the syscall number level but remain officially undocumented/PRIVATE. Linux's `sendmmsg`/`recvmmsg` do NOT exist on macOS. Sunshine's macOS `misc.mm` currently returns `false` from `send_batch()` (i.e., falls back to per-packet `sendmsg()`), while its Linux path uses `sendmmsg()` and even GSO. This means the macOS path for batch video-frame packet bursts has an opportunity gap: calling `sendmsg_x` with a `cnt` argument would collapse N syscalls into 1, at the cost of using a private but stable API. The `msghdr_x` structure adds a `msg_datalen` field to the standard `msghdr`.

Network.framework (NWConnection) vs raw BSD sockets: WWDC18 session 715 measured a 30% CPU reduction on the receiver side for UDP video streaming when using Network.framework with user-space networking vs kernel-path sockets. User-space networking landed on macOS in macOS 12 (Monterey), confirmed by Apple DTS engineer Quinn in a 2023 forum thread. When active, NWConnections use kernel "flowsw" entries (visible via `sudo skywalkctl`) with zero-copy memory-mapped packet delivery — no kernel-to-user copy, no extra context switch per packet. User-space networking is disabled if: (a) the system firewall is on (System Settings > Network > Firewall), (b) legacy VPN/NKE software is installed, or (c) iCloud Private Relay is active. For a controlled LAN-only app with none of these active, Network.framework's user-space path should beat raw BSD sockets on CPU, which indirectly reduces tail latency under load. NWConnection service class should be set to `.interactiveVideo` (maps to `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI`); per-packet override via `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.serviceClass` is also possible.

Real-time scheduling: `thread_policy_set(THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY)` is the right tool for the network send/recv loop thread, elevating it to the Mach real-time scheduling band and preventing priority inversion under CPU load. The parameters (`period`, `computation`, `constraint`, all in Mach absolute time units from `mach_timebase_info`) must be tuned to realistic values (e.g., period = 1/frame-rate, computation = expected CPU time per period). THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY works on Apple Silicon (the concern about `getCpuSpeedInMegahertz()` returning 0 in JUCE is irrelevant for correctly computing Mach time — use `mach_timebase_info` directly). For less precision-critical use, `pthread_set_qos_class_self_np(QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, 0)` is sandboxed-safe, raises thread priority within the QoS system, and Apple's scheduler handles priority propagation automatically. True kernel bypass (DPDK style) is unavailable on macOS/iOS — Apple's SIP and entitlement model prevents userspace NIC access. The NetworkExtension `NEPacketTunnelProvider` is the only sanctioned "bypass"-adjacent path, but it adds significant overhead and is intended for VPN, not low-latency streaming. The practical floor for UDP round-trip on a wired Gigabit LAN with an Apple Silicon Mac is in the low-hundreds-of-microseconds range (no authoritative published figure found; Linux optimized 10G systems measure ~31–32 µs RTT in DPDK literature).

techniques.name: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE with NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI / NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV
techniques.how: Call `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, &val, sizeof(val))` where val = `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI` (3, Interactive Video) or `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV` (5, Responsive Multimedia A/V — explicitly described in XNU socket.h as 'screen sharing'). This is a public, non-private constant in `<sys/socket.h>`. Effect: sets 802.11 Wi-Fi User Priority (AC_VI or AC_VO at the MAC layer), giving the UDP flow hardware-level priority in the Wi-Fi transmit queue. On wired Ethernet, the scheduling effect is within the OS network stack. Sunshine's macOS misc.mm uses NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI for video and NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO for audio with this exact call.
techniques.latency_impact: High on Wi-Fi (reduces queuing in 802.11 access categories); medium on wired Ethernet (influences intra-stack scheduling priority). No published ms number found for this alone on LAN.

techniques.name: IP_TOS / IPV6_TCLASS for DSCP marking
techniques.how: After setting SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, also call `setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IP, IP_TOS, &dscp, sizeof(dscp))` (IPv4) or `setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IPV6, IPV6_TCLASS, &dscp, sizeof(dscp))` (IPv6). Use DSCP AF41 (value 34, left-shifted by 2 to fill TOS byte: `34 << 2 = 0x88`) for video. On macOS 10.14+ this sets the TOS byte in outgoing IP headers; however, on macOS with generic app entitlements the DSCP may not appear in Wireshark captures (L3 DSCP is stripped or not applied). The effect on LAN switches that honor DSCP is real if the switch is QoS-configured, but in typical home/office LAN environments this has no effect.
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal on LAN unless managed switches honor DSCP for queuing. Silent failure on macOS (setsockopt succeeds but packet capture shows TOS=0x0 on Mojave+); confirmed in Apple forum thread.

techniques.name: SO_NOSIGPIPE
techniques.how: Set `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NOSIGPIPE, &(int){1}, sizeof(int))` on every UDP socket. macOS does not support `MSG_NOSIGNAL` (Linux flag); without SO_NOSIGPIPE, a write to a socket where the peer has closed can raise SIGPIPE and kill the process. This is a correctness/robustness primitive that also avoids signal-handler overhead.
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal — prevents crash/signal overhead; not a latency optimization per se.

techniques.name: IP_BOUND_IF / IPV6_BOUND_IF — bind socket to specific interface
techniques.how: Get interface index with `if_nametoindex("en0")` (or en1/en2 for other NICs), then `setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IP, IP_BOUND_IF, &ifindex, sizeof(ifindex))`. IPv6 analog: `setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IPV6, IPV6_BOUND_IF, ...)`. This forces all traffic to go over the wired Ethernet NIC, avoiding routing through VPN, Wi-Fi, or Thunderbolt bridges. Critical for deterministic latency on LAN.
techniques.latency_impact: High — prevents inadvertent routing over a slower or higher-latency interface. Eliminates a class of non-deterministic routing bugs.

techniques.name: sendmsg_x / recvmsg_x — macOS batch UDP syscalls
techniques.how: Use Darwin syscall numbers 481 (`sendmsg_x`) and 480 (`recvmsg_x`) with `struct msghdr_x` arrays. `msghdr_x` is `msghdr` + a `msg_datalen` field. Signature: `user_ssize_t sendmsg_x(int s, struct msghdr_x *msgp, u_int cnt, int flags)`. Batching N video frame packets (each already a UDP datagram) into a single `sendmsg_x(fd, msgs, N, 0)` call collapses N context switches to 1. These syscalls are listed in XNU `bsd/kern/syscalls.master` (lines 480–481) and in `bsd/sys/socket.h` (PRIVATE section), so they are stable at the syscall-number level but not officially documented. Sunshine's macOS path does NOT use them (returns false from send_batch()), making this an open optimization opportunity.
techniques.latency_impact: High for throughput; medium for per-frame latency. Reduces syscall count proportional to batch size — for a 1080p60 HEVC stream broken into ~10 UDP shards/frame, batch of 10 reduces syscalls 10x per frame. Cloudflare's Linux sendmmsg work reduced syscalls from 904K to 15K with moderate but real throughput gains.

techniques.name: Network.framework NWConnection with user-space networking
techniques.how: Use `NWConnection` with `NWParameters.udp` and set `parameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo`. When the system firewall is disabled and no conflicting NKE/VPN is present, macOS 12+ delivers packets via user-space networking: memory-mapped, zero-copy from driver to app, eliminating the kernel-to-userspace data copy. Verify via `sudo skywalkctl` — active NWConnections should show as `flowsw` entries, not BSD socket file descriptors. For per-packet service class override (e.g., prioritizing keyframes), use `NWProtocolIP.Metadata` with `.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo` in the send `ContentContext`.
techniques.latency_impact: High — Apple's own WWDC18 measured 30% CPU reduction on UDP receiver side vs BSD sockets when user-space networking is active. Lower CPU burn directly reduces scheduling jitter and tail latency.

techniques.name: THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY for network send/recv thread
techniques.how: Obtain the Mach thread port via `pthread_mach_thread_np(pthread_self())`, fill a `thread_time_constraint_policy_data_t` with `period` = frame interval in Mach absolute time, `computation` = expected encode+send CPU time, `constraint` = deadline (must be > computation), `preemptible = 1`. Call `thread_policy_set(port, THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY, (thread_policy_t)&policy, THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY_COUNT)`. Convert from nanoseconds to Mach time units using `mach_timebase_info`. For 60 fps: period ≈ 16.7 ms = 16,700,000 ns × (numer/denom), computation ≈ 1–2 ms, constraint ≈ 3–4 ms. This elevates the thread to real-time scheduling band, preventing preemption by normal background threads.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical — prevents scheduler-induced send jitter. A preempted send thread can stall 5–20 ms on a loaded system. On Apple Silicon, this is especially important as the E-core/P-core scheduler can move network threads to efficiency cores without this policy.

techniques.name: pthread_set_qos_class_self_np(QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE) as lighter alternative
techniques.how: Call `pthread_set_qos_class_self_np(QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, 0)` on the network send thread at thread start. This is sandboxed-safe (no special entitlement needed), integrates with GCD priority propagation, and prevents the thread from being demoted by QoS inheritance. Less powerful than THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY but no risk of budget overrun.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — raises thread priority within QoS bands; does not guarantee real-time scheduling but substantially reduces preemption by background work.

techniques.name: Socket buffer sizing via SO_SNDBUF / SO_RCVBUF and kern.ipc.maxsockbuf
techniques.how: macOS default UDP receive buffer (`net.inet.udp.recvspace`) on this system is 786,896 bytes (~768 KB). The kernel ceiling `kern.ipc.maxsockbuf` is 8,388,608 (8 MB). For the sender, `SO_SNDBUF` may be left at default for a streaming sender (packet-at-a-time or batch). For the receiver, increase with `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_RCVBUF, &size, sizeof(size))` up to 4–8 MB to absorb bursts. To exceed `kern.ipc.maxsockbuf`, increase it at boot via `/etc/sysctl.conf`: `kern.ipc.maxsockbuf=8625000` (Storj/quic-go recommendation with 15% headroom). Note: increasing SO_RCVBUF does not increase latency if the receiver loop is fast enough to drain the buffer; it prevents packet drops during scheduling gaps.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — on LAN with reliable 1 Gbps links, drops are rare, but a single dropped keyframe causes a stall until the next IDR. Large recv buffer eliminates drop-induced IDR requests.

techniques.name: Kernel-bypass (DPDK-style) — not available on macOS/iOS
techniques.how: DPDK requires direct userspace NIC access via UIO/VFIO kernel modules and requires bypassing the OS TCP/IP stack entirely. macOS SIP (System Integrity Protection), the driver model, and App Store entitlements collectively prevent this. There is no DPDK macOS NIC PMD for Apple Silicon NICs, and no public entitlement for raw NIC DMA. NetworkExtension's NEPacketTunnelProvider can intercept packets at the IP layer but adds overhead (IPC to system extension) making it slower, not faster. XDP (Linux-specific) and io_uring (Linux-specific) do not exist on Darwin.
techniques.latency_impact: N/A — not possible without jailbreak/SIP disable on production deployments.

techniques.name: Non-blocking socket + kqueue (EVFILT_READ) for recv loop
techniques.how: Create UDP socket with `O_NONBLOCK`. Register with `kevent(kq, &ev, 1, NULL, 0, NULL)` using `EVFILT_READ`. Block on `kevent()` with timeout = next expected frame deadline. On event, call `recvmsg()` (or `recvmsg_x()` for batch). This avoids spinning a busy-poll loop (which macOS has no `SO_BUSY_POLL` for) while still waking the receive thread within microseconds of packet arrival, bounded by interrupt coalescing at the NIC. Set `EV_CLEAR` flag to edge-trigger.
techniques.latency_impact: High — avoids the ~0.1–1 ms context switch overhead of blocking recvmsg in a normal blocking call. On LAN, interrupt coalescing at the NIC (not controllable via macOS ethtool equivalent) is the remaining bottleneck.

techniques.name: SO_TIMESTAMP / SO_TIMESTAMP_MONOTONIC for one-way latency measurement
techniques.how: Set `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_TIMESTAMP, &(int){1}, sizeof(int))` to get kernel receive timestamps (wall clock) or `SO_TIMESTAMP_MONOTONIC` (monotonic clock, 0x0800 in XNU). Read via `recvmsg()` control message `SCM_TIMESTAMP`. In Network.framework: set `connection.shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true` and read `receiveTime` from the received content context. Use for glass-to-glass latency instrumentation, not a send-path optimization.
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal (measurement only, not a speed improvement). Enables accurate latency profiling to identify where pipeline stalls occur.

key_settings: setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, &(int){NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV}, sizeof(int))  // NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV=5 for screen-sharing class; VI=3 also acceptable
setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IP, IP_TOS, &(int){34<<2}, sizeof(int))  // AF41 DSCP for video (note: may not appear in packet captures on macOS 10.14+)
setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NOSIGPIPE, &(int){1}, sizeof(int))  // Required on macOS; MSG_NOSIGNAL not supported
setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_IP, IP_BOUND_IF, &(int){if_nametoindex("en0")}, sizeof(int))  // Pin to wired Ethernet, avoid VPN/WiFi routing
setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_RCVBUF, &(int){4*1024*1024}, sizeof(int))  // 4 MB recv buffer; requires kern.ipc.maxsockbuf >= 4718592
setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_SNDBUF, &(int){2*1024*1024}, sizeof(int))  // 2 MB send buffer
fcntl(fd, F_SETFL, O_NONBLOCK)  // Non-blocking for kqueue-driven recv loop
kern.ipc.maxsockbuf=8625000  // /etc/sysctl.conf — kernel ceiling for socket buffers
thread_policy_set(pthread_mach_thread_np(pthread_self()), THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY, ...)  // Real-time scheduling for send/recv thread
// OR: pthread_set_qos_class_self_np(QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, 0)  // Lighter, sandboxed-safe alternative
// Network.framework: params.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo  // NWParameters service class
// Network.framework: NWProtocolIP.Metadata().serviceClass = .interactiveVideo  // Per-packet QoS override for UDP
latency_numbers: ~30% CPU reduction on UDP receiver with Network.framework user-space networking vs BSD sockets (Apple WWDC18 session 715 live demo, uncompressed video stream benchmark — CPU % not absolute latency ms)
~31–32 µs UDP RTT floor on optimized Linux + 10 GbE (Cloudflare 'How to achieve low latency' post) — Linux, not macOS, but sets the physical floor for wired Ethernet
0.5–1.1 ms typical Ethernet ping RTT on Apple Silicon M3 MacBook Pro (Apple Community thread, anecdotal), 2–6 ms over Wi-Fi; spikes to 950 ms observed under load

Linux sendmmsg reduced syscall count from 904,539 to 15,676 in Cloudflare QUIC experiment — throughput gain 'not quite as big as the decrease in syscalls' (Cloudflare blog on accelerating UDP for QUIC)
200K datagrams/sec achievable with sendmmsg/recvmmsg at ~6K syscalls/sec on Linux (shadowsocks-rust GitHub issue #823, referenced by search)
claims_to_verify: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE silently succeeds but does NOT set DSCP in IP packet headers on macOS 10.14+; confirmed in Apple forums thread but worth packet-capture verification with Wireshark on the specific macOS 14+ target
Network.framework user-space networking (zero-copy, flowsw path) is active on macOS 12+ when firewall is disabled — verify with `sudo skywalkctl` on macOS 14/15 target machine

sendmsg_x (syscall 481) and recvmsg_x (syscall 480) are stable and callable directly on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon — confirmed in XNU syscalls.master HEAD but marked PRIVATE; verify no regression in macOS 15/26

THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY works correctly on Apple Silicon — the JUCE developer's forum question (unanswered) raised concern about Apple Silicon compatibility; the root issue was getCpuSpeedInMegahertz() returning 0 (irrelevant if using mach_timebase_info directly), but worth verifying the policy is honored on arm64 macOS

30% CPU overhead reduction claim from WWDC18 is for receiver-side CPU on iOS with user-space networking — verify whether this claim holds on macOS 14 (which got user-space networking later, in macOS 12) and whether the improvement transfers to latency reduction or is purely CPU throughput
NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RV (value 5) is indeed described as 'screen sharing' in XNU socket.h — confirm this is the most appropriate constant vs NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI (3) for a 60fps video stream
open_questions: What is the measured UDP round-trip latency (in µs) over wired Gigabit Ethernet between two Apple Silicon Macs running macOS 14? No authoritative benchmark found.
Does calling sendmsg_x via syscall number (using syscall(481, ...)) or via a dlsym lookup of a private symbol in libSystem actually work reliably in a sandboxed app on macOS 14+?
When user-space networking is active in Network.framework, does the zero-copy path apply to UDP sends as well as receives, or only receives?

Is there a NIC interrupt coalescing tuning knob on macOS equivalent to Linux ethtool -C to reduce receive interrupt latency below the default coalescing interval? (Not found in research; likely not exposed.)
Does QUIC datagram mode (NWProtocolQUIC with isDatagram=true) introduce measurable latency vs raw UDP NWConnection on LAN, given that QUIC adds congestion control feedback overhead?
On iOS/iPadOS client side, are SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE and IP_BOUND_IF available in the regular (non-NE) sandbox, or are they restricted by the iOS sandbox entitlement model?

verifications.claim: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE silently succeeds but does NOT set DSCP in IP packet headers on macOS 10.14+; confirmed in Apple forums thread but worth packet-capture verification with Wireshark on the specific macOS 14+ target

verdict.claim: SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE silently succeeds but does NOT set DSCP in IP packet headers on macOS 10.14+; confirmed in Apple forums thread but worth packet-capture verification with Wireshark on the specific macOS 14+ target
verdict.evidence:
XNU kernel source (apple-oss-distributions/xnu bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c, bsd/netinet/ip_output.c) and the Apple Developer Forums thread (developer.apple.com/forums/thread/109915) confirm the following precise behavior:

1. SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE setsockopt() always returns 0 (success) — it sets a field on the socket struct but does not directly touch the IP TOS byte. This "silent success without IP DSCP" part is CONFIRMED.

2. However, the claim is imprecise about WHY and about scope. DSCP is only written to outgoing IP packets when the outgoing interface has IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED set in its if_eflags AND if_qosmarking_mode is non-zero. That flag is only set by Fastlane (Cisco Wi-Fi) or RFC4594 infrastructure — never on standard Ethernet or non-Fastlane Wi-Fi interfaces. The set_packet_qos() function in in_tclass.c makes this conditional explicit: the DSCP update branch is gated on `(ifp->if_eflags & IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED) != 0`.

3. The claim says this is a macOS 10.14+ regression from 10.10 behavior. The Apple forums developer Quinn confirmed DSCP is not set and QoS requirement is "reflected via Wi-Fi level"; community member Kentzo noted that macOS 10.10 DID produce non-zero DSCP values but 10.14 does not. The kernel code shows this is an interface-capability-gated design, not a bug.

4. CRITICALLY FOR THE PROJECT (LAN/Ethernet context): IP_TOS setsockopt DOES reliably write DSCP to IP headers on Ethernet. In udp_usrreq.c, inp->inp_ip_tos is copied to ip->ip_tos before ip_output runs set_packet_qos(). When IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED is absent (all Ethernet interfaces), set_packet_qos() does NOT zero out dscp_inout — it only acts when the flag IS set (Fastlane). So the existing ip_tos value flows through unchanged to the wire.

5. There is a further wrinkle noted in the kernel code comment: "When on a Fastlane network, IP_TOS/IPV6_TCLASS are no-ops" — meaning on Fastlane Wi-Fi the kernel ignores IP_TOS and overrides with its own DSCP mapping. This is the inverse of the Ethernet case.

6. The claim's framing ("silently succeeds but does NOT set DSCP") is accurate only for the SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE→DSCP path on standard interfaces. The alternative — using IP_TOS setsockopt directly — does set DSCP in IP headers on Ethernet (and non-Fastlane Wi-Fi), which the claim does not mention and which is the correct fix for a LAN screen-sharing app.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/109915 (Apple Developer Forums: QoS DSCP value not set — Quinn 'The Eskimo!' Apple DTS + community analysis by Kentzo)
https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c (XNU kernel: set_packet_qos() function, IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED gate, lines 1685-1788)
https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/netinet/ip_output.c (XNU kernel: DSCP written to ip->ip_tos at lines 1622-1629, gated by set_packet_qos result)
https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/netinet/udp_usrreq.c (XNU kernel: inp_ip_tos copied to ip->ip_tos at line 1937 before ip_output runs)
verdict.correction: The claim is partially correct but imprecise in two important ways: (1) The "silent success without DSCP" behavior of SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE is confirmed on standard Ethernet and non-Fastlane Wi-Fi interfaces, but the mechanism is interface-capability gating (IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED flag, only set by Cisco Fastlane / RFC4594 Wi-Fi), not a version-specific regression. (2) The claim omits that IP_TOS setsockopt (with SOL_IP / IPPROTO_IP, option IP_TOS, value = DSCP << 2) DOES reliably write DSCP to IP headers on macOS Ethernet interfaces — the kernel does not zero out a user-set ip_tos on non-Fastlane interfaces. For a LAN/Ethernet screen-sharing app, the correct approach is: use IP_TOS (or IPV6_TCLASS for IPv6) for L3 DSCP marking, and optionally also set SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE for L2 Wi-Fi 802.11 UP priority. Wireshark verification is still warranted since behavior may differ on specific macOS 14+ builds, and on Fastlane Wi-Fi the kernel ignores IP_TOS entirely (overrides with its own map).

verifications.claim: Network.framework user-space networking (zero-copy, flowsw path) is active on macOS 12+ when firewall is disabled — verify with `sudo skywalkctl` on macOS 14/15 target machine

verdict.claim: Network.framework user-space networking (zero-copy, flowsw path) is active on macOS 12+ when firewall is disabled — verify with `sudo skywalkctl` on macOS 14/15 target machine
verdict.evidence:
Multiple authoritative sources confirm the core architecture but several sub-claims need qualification:

1. USER-SPACE NETWORKING AVAILABILITY: Confirmed correct. User-space networking (Skywalk/flowsw path) landed in macOS 12 and Network.framework uses it by default. Confirmed by Apple DTS engineer Quinn on the Apple Developer Forums thread (thread/751581).

2. SKYWALKCTL TOOL AND SPELLING: Confirmed correct. `skywalkctl` is the exact tool name (man page dated from macOS Mojave era, `flow` subcommand confirmed on more recent versions). The correct verification command is `sudo skywalkctl flow -n -P <pid>`, not just `sudo skywalkctl` alone. The man page at keith.github.io/xcode-man-pages confirms `flow` as a valid subcommand.

3. "FLOWSW PATH" TERMINOLOGY: Partially correct. Apple's internal abbreviation is `flowsw` (visible in `lsof` as `CHAN flowsw`), which stands for "flow switch." The claim's shorthand "flowsw path" aligns with Apple's internal lsof output terminology. This is NOT called "flowswitch" in any Apple source found.

4. "ZERO-COPY" CHARACTERIZATION: Uncertain/imprecise. Apple does not explicitly label the Network.framework/Skywalk path as "zero-copy" in any public documentation found. Skywalk uses channel-based kernel communication (Nexus/Channel objects) that avoids BSD socket overhead and file descriptor consumption, but Apple's own documentation describes the benefit as avoiding BSD sockets and enabling batch sends, not "zero-copy" specifically. The term appears in community sources (newosxbook.com/bonus/vol1ch16.html) describing Skywalk's design goals, not in Apple docs.

5. FIREWALL CONDITION — LOAD-BEARING DISCREPANCY FOR macOS 14 vs 15: This is where the claim is most imprecise. Quinn (Apple DTS) on the developer forums (thread/751581, tested on macOS 14.4.1) confirmed: "the built-in firewall (System Settings > Network > Firewall) disables user-space networking. I just tried it here in my office and, yeah, my test tool starts using sockets as soon as I enable the firewall." HOWEVER, a subsequent report (referenced in the same thread discussion) states that on macOS 15 (Sequoia) 15.3, "the firewall status doesn't affect this, and it always uses the user-space stack." This means: on macOS 14 the claim is correct (firewall must be OFF), but on macOS 15 the firewall condition appears to have been removed — making the firewall caveat version-specific and not uniformly applicable across "macOS 14/15." Apple explicitly flagged this as "an implementation detail that could well change in the future."

6. "ACTIVE ON macOS 12+" PHRASING: The user-space path is active by default on macOS 12+ subject to fallback conditions beyond just the firewall: iCloud Private Relay, Network Extension VPNs, and certain legacy network configurations also force BSD socket fallback. The claim's single "firewall is disabled" condition understates the full set of fallback triggers.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/751581 — Apple Developer Forums: 'Network framework on macOS' (Quinn/DTS, confirmed macOS 14.4.1 firewall behavior, flowsw path, skywalkctl flow command)
https://keith.github.io/xcode-man-pages/skywalkctl.8.html — Xcode man pages: skywalkctl(8), confirms 'flow' subcommand exists
https://manp.gs/mac/8/skywalkctl — skywalkctl man page (July 2018), subcommand list
https://newosxbook.com/bonus/vol1ch16.html — *OS Internals Vol. 1 Ch. 16: Skywalk subsystem internals, zero-copy channel architecture
verdict.correction:
The claim is directionally correct for macOS 14 but has three imprecisions:

(1) FIREWALL CONDITION IS VERSION-SPECIFIC: On macOS 14 (Sonoma), the built-in firewall forces Network.framework to fall back to BSD sockets — confirmed by Apple DTS Quinn on macOS 14.4.1. On macOS 15 (Sequoia) 15.3+, this fallback was apparently removed and the user-space stack is used regardless of firewall state. Since the project targets macOS 14+, both behaviors must be accounted for.

(2) "ZERO-COPY" IS IMPRECISE: Apple does not use "zero-copy" as the documented description for this path. The Skywalk/flowsw path avoids BSD socket overhead via kernel channel objects (Nexus/Channel architecture) but Apple's public documentation describes this as avoiding file descriptors and enabling efficient batching, not "zero-copy." Use "user-space networking path" or "Skywalk/flowsw path" instead.

(3) FIREWALL IS NOT THE ONLY FALLBACK TRIGGER: User-space networking also falls back to BSD sockets when iCloud Private Relay is active, when Network Extension VPNs are loaded, and potentially with certain legacy VPN configurations — even with the firewall disabled.

Corrected claim: "Network.framework uses the user-space Skywalk (flowsw) path by default on macOS 12+. On macOS 14 (Sonoma), this path is disabled and falls back to BSD sockets when the built-in firewall is enabled, iCloud Private Relay is active, or certain Network Extension VPNs are loaded. On macOS 15 (Sequoia) 15.3+, the firewall no longer forces this fallback. Verify with `sudo skywalkctl flow -n -P <pid>` — flowsw entries in the output confirm the user-space path is active."

verifications.claim: sendmsg_x (syscall 481) and recvmsg_x (syscall 480) are stable and callable directly on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon — confirmed in XNU syscalls.master HEAD but marked PRIVATE; verify no regression in macOS 15/26

verdict.claim: sendmsg_x (syscall 481) and recvmsg_x (syscall 480) are stable and callable directly on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon — confirmed in XNU syscalls.master HEAD but marked PRIVATE; verify no regression in macOS 15/26
verdict.evidence:
Multiple authoritative sources on the host machine and in XNU source confirm the claim, with one important precision correction about what "PRIVATE" actually means in practice.

**Syscall numbers are stable and correct:**
- `SYS_recvmsg_x = 480`, `SYS_sendmsg_x = 481` appear in [`xnu/bsd/kern/syscalls.master`](https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/kern/syscalls.master) lines 735–736, conditioned only on `#if SOCKETS` (always true on macOS). The numbers are identical across every SDK version from 10.10 through the installed macOS 15.4 SDK and macOS 26.5 SDK — zero regression confirmed by direct grep of the host SDKs.

**Confirmed in public SDK syscall.h (not just XNU source):**
- `/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/SDKs/MacOSX15.4.sdk/usr/include/sys/syscall.h` and `MacOSX26.5.sdk/usr/include/sys/syscall.h` both define `SYS_recvmsg_x 480` and `SYS_sendmsg_x 481`. These are public SDK headers — no special entitlement or private header needed to get the syscall numbers.

**Exported from libSystem:**
- Both `_sendmsg_x` and `_recvmsg_x` appear as exported symbols in `MacOSX15.4.sdk/usr/lib/libSystem.B.tbd` and `MacOSX26.5.sdk/usr/lib/libSystem.B.tbd`, meaning they are callable via the standard dynamic linker without a raw `syscall()` trap.

**"PRIVATE" marking is accurate but narrowly scoped:**
- The word "PRIVATE" appears in a documentation comment inside [`bsd/sys/socket_private.h`](https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/xnu/blob/main/bsd/sys/socket_private.h) and is reproduced in Bun's [`bsd.h`](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/packages/bun-usockets/src/internal/networking/bsd.h#L103): *"NOTE: This is a private system call, the API is subject to change."* The PRIVATE designation means the `msghdr_x` struct is defined in `socket_private.h` (not in the public `usr/include/sys/socket.h`) and there is no man page. However, the function symbols themselves are exported publicly from libSystem and the syscall numbers are in the public SDK `syscall.h`.

**macOS 13 hang — the 14+ qualifier is load-bearing:**
- Bun's runtime feature gate ([`src/analytics/lib.rs` line 469](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/src/analytics/lib.rs#L469), [`src/analytics/analytics.zig` line 296](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/src/analytics/analytics.zig#L296)) states explicitly: *"On macOS 13, tests that use sendmsg_x or recvmsg_x hang."* Their `Bun__doesMacOSVersionSupportSendRecvMsgX()` returns true only for macOS 14.0+. This is a known XNU bug in macOS 13 that was fixed in 14, making the "macOS 14+" qualifier in the original claim precisely correct and important.

**Practical callability:**
- Can be called as `sendmsg_x(fd, msgvec, cnt, flags)` by declaring `msghdr_x` locally (since the public headers omit it) and linking against libSystem normally, OR via `syscall(SYS_sendmsg_x, ...)`. Bun uses the former approach. The `sendmsg_x` version uses `struct mmsghdr` (Linux-compatible layout) in Bun's wrapper, not the XNU-native `struct msghdr_x` — a subtle but important implementation detail for any caller.
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate. One precision correction: "PRIVATE" means the companion `msghdr_x` struct lives only in `socket_private.h` (not in the public `usr/include/sys/socket.h`) and there is no man page — but the function symbols ARE exported in the public libSystem.B stub and SYS_sendmsg_x/SYS_recvmsg_x ARE defined in the public usr/include/sys/syscall.h. Callers must forward-declare `msghdr_x` themselves. Additionally, the macOS 13 hang is a confirmed real regression that Bun gates around; macOS 14+ is the correct lower bound for safe use.

rawCorpus.summary:
**Link-layer physics.** Gigabit Ethernet (even over Thunderbolt/USB-C adapters on Apple Silicon) delivers sub-1 ms RTT on a LAN with near-zero jitter — the only practical floor for the transport layer. Wi-Fi adds physical-layer overhead from CSMA/CA contention, SIFS/DIFS/AIFS inter-frame gaps, backoff slots, and retransmissions. Under light load on an uncontested 5 GHz or 6 GHz channel, modern Wi-Fi 6/6E achieves 2–5 ms RTT (measured on Moonlight LAN benchmarks). On a busy or poorly-configured network, Wi-Fi RTT spikes to 15–30 ms or higher. Wi-Fi 6E at 6 GHz consistently outperforms 5 GHz because the 6 GHz band has no legacy devices causing airtime waste, no overlapping-channel interference from neighbours, and much wider clean channels. The 6 GHz band effectively eliminates BSS contention latency, reducing jitter by ~80% in head-to-head tests against congested 5 GHz deployments.

**WMM/QoS and how to reach AC_VI on Apple.** 802.11e WMM defines four Access Categories: AC_BK (background), AC_BE (best effort), AC_VI (video), AC_VO (voice). Each has progressively smaller AIFSN, CWmin, and CWmax, giving higher-priority queues statistically shorter pre-transmission waits. Audio jitter in AC_BE averages ~67 ms vs ~7 ms in AC_VO — a 10x difference. The correct Apple API chain to reach AC_VI is: set `NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo` (Network.framework) or call `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI, ...)` on a raw BSD socket. XNU source (darwin-xnu `bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c`) confirms: `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI` → traffic class `SO_TC_VI` → DSCP `AF41` (Fastlane/RFC4594 maps) → MBUF service class `MBUF_SC_VI` → Wi-Fi driver maps to **AC_VI** (WMM User Priority 5). `NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VO` → `SO_TC_VO` → DSCP `EF` → `MBUF_SC_VO` → **AC_VO** (UP 6/7). Critically, XNU's `net_qos_policy_restricted` defaults to **0** (unrestricted), meaning any sandboxed macOS app can use `SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE` to reach AC_VI/AC_VO without an entitlement or MDM profile — the Cisco Fastlane restriction applies on iOS/iPadOS for DSCP L3 markings on cellular, not for Wi-Fi L2 UP marking on macOS. `.interactiveVideo` is the correct service class for the video stream UDP flow; `.responsiveData` is appropriate for control/input packets.

**AWDL: the hidden latency bomb.** AWDL (Apple Wireless Direct Link) runs continuously in the background on every macOS and iOS device to support AirDrop, AirPlay, and Sidecar. It operates by time-sharing the Wi-Fi radio: in idle/discovery mode, it allocates ≥25% of time slots to "social channels" (5 GHz ch 44 or 149; 2.4 GHz ch 6) to listen for nearby Apple devices. When the host is on a different channel, the radio physically hops every ~100–110 ms for a 16 ms window, causing periodic 50–200 ms latency spikes in the infrastructure Wi-Fi path roughly every 10–12 seconds. Christoff Visser (RIPE 91, October 2025) measured 20 ms latency with 25 ms variance on a streaming test and RTT bouncing between 3–90 ms with Steam's tool. Mitigation: configure your AP to use AWDL's preferred social channels (5 GHz ch 44 or ch 149) so the radio doesn't need to hop away; this eliminates the spike entirely. AWDL also has a "realtime mode" that reduces channel-switch overhead significantly — it is triggered automatically when Universal Control or Sidecar is established, not by a public API. An Apple DTS engineer explicitly confirmed (Apple Developer Forums thread 819926) there is no programmatic way to trigger realtime mode in Network.framework prior to iOS/iPadOS 26.

**Peer-to-peer: AWDL vs Wi-Fi Aware vs router path.** Using `NWParameters.includePeerToPeer = true` opts the connection into AWDL as an interface but this does NOT guarantee lower latency than the infrastructure path. Apple's own DTS engineer warned in developer forum thread 751839 that peer-to-peer Wi-Fi can introduce "hundreds of milliseconds" of latency and that "infrastructure Wi-Fi is generally better when available."
