MultipeerConnectivity RTT measured between two iPhones showed a cyclical pattern: 5–15 ms when good, spiking to 40–336 ms in a repeating ~5-second cycle. iOS/iPadOS 26 introduces the **Wi-Fi Aware framework** (IEEE 802.11mc NAN — Neighbor Awareness Networking) with `WAPerformanceMode.realtime` for explicit low-latency mode; this is iOS/iPadOS only (macOS support not mentioned in WWDC25 session 228), requires the `com.apple.developer.wifi-aware` entitlement, and is positioned for file transfer and media streaming between iPhones/iPads without a router. For a macOS-to-iOS/iPadOS screen-sharing app on a LAN that has a good AP, the infrastructure Wi-Fi path with proper QoS marking is more predictable than AWDL peer-to-peer.

techniques.name: Use Gigabit Ethernet (wired) where possible
techniques.how: Connect both host and client via Thunderbolt/USB-C Gigabit Ethernet adapters. On Apple Silicon Macs, the built-in Ethernet port (M1 Pro/Max/Ultra/M2+) is native and adds <0.1 ms vs USB-C adapters. Wired Ethernet on a LAN switch delivers <1 ms RTT and effectively zero jitter.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates 2–30 ms Wi-Fi overhead and all Wi-Fi jitter

techniques.name: Set NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo (or SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE = NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI)
techniques.how: In Network.framework Swift: `params.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo`. Via BSD socket: `setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, &val, sizeof(val))` with `val = NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI`. XNU kernel maps this to DSCP AF41 and ultimately to 802.11e WMM AC_VI (User Priority 5) at the Wi-Fi driver layer. On macOS, `net_qos_policy_restricted` defaults to 0, so no MDM or entitlement is required. For input/control packets use `.responsiveData` (NET_SERVICE_TYPE_RD → AC_BE with lower latency characteristics). Do NOT use `.interactiveVoice` (AC_VO / UP 6–7) as this is intended for VoIP and can cause unnecessary congestion at the AP.
techniques.latency_impact: high — reduces airtime wait from AC_BE (~67 ms jitter) to AC_VI (~7–15 ms jitter) in congested scenarios; marginal under light load

techniques.name: Use 5 GHz Wi-Fi 6 or 6 GHz Wi-Fi 6E with 80 MHz channel width
techniques.how: Configure the AP to use the 5 GHz or 6 GHz band. For macOS hosts with M1/M2/M3/M4 — which support Wi-Fi 6E — the 6 GHz band is strongly preferred: zero co-channel interference from legacy devices, no AWDL social channel collision, and wider clean spectrum. Apple recommends 'Auto or all widths' for 5/6 GHz. Note: portable Macs do NOT support 160 MHz below 6 GHz (only 80 MHz on 5 GHz). On 6 GHz, 80 or 160 MHz are both supported.
techniques.latency_impact: high — 6 GHz reduces jitter by ~80% vs congested 5 GHz; 6 GHz measured latency: 3–8 ms RTT vs 5–15 ms on 5 GHz

techniques.name: Configure AP to use AWDL social channels (5 GHz ch 44 or ch 149)
techniques.how: Set the AP's 5 GHz channel to 44 or 149. AWDL's preferred 5 GHz social channels are 44 and 149. When the infrastructure network already operates on these channels, the radio does not need to hop away for AWDL discovery, eliminating the 50–200 ms latency spikes that occur every 10–12 seconds on non-social channels. On 6 GHz with Wi-Fi 6E, AWDL uses a different channel scheme; verify with packet captures if needed.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates periodic 50–200 ms AWDL-induced latency spikes; without this, 25 ms variance is expected

techniques.name: Enable WMM on the AP (do not disable it)
techniques.how: Ensure WMM (Wi-Fi Multimedia / 802.11e) is enabled on the access point. Apple explicitly recommends 'Set to Enabled' in their router configuration guide. Without WMM, all traffic competes in AC_BE regardless of QoS markings set by the OS. WPA3 routers have WMM enabled by default; older routers may not.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — without WMM enabled, all serviceClass settings are ignored at the AP; AC_VI/AC_VO traffic gets no priority advantage

techniques.name: Avoid NWParameters.includePeerToPeer for the video/data stream
techniques.how: Do NOT use `includePeerToPeer = true` on the primary video stream connection. Apple's DTS engineers confirm that peer-to-peer Wi-Fi (AWDL) often introduces 'hundreds of milliseconds' of latency and infrastructure Wi-Fi is preferred when available. MultipeerConnectivity (which uses AWDL) shows 40–336 ms RTT spikes in a ~5-second cycle. Use infrastructure path (normal NWConnection to NWListener) for the latency-critical video/audio stream.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — avoids 40–336 ms RTT spikes from AWDL's channel-hopping in non-realtime mode

techniques.name: Wi-Fi Aware (WAPerformanceMode.realtime) for iOS/iPadOS 26+ peer-to-peer without AP
techniques.how: iOS 26 and iPadOS 26 introduce the `WiFiAware` framework. Use `WASession` with `WAPerformanceMode.realtime` and `NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo` for low-latency direct device-to-device without a router. Requires the `com.apple.developer.wifi-aware` entitlement. Pair with `DeviceDiscoveryUI` for user-facing connection setup. This is the only path to explicitly trigger low-latency peer-to-peer mode on iOS/iPadOS. macOS support is NOT confirmed — WWDC25 session 228 only mentions iPhone and iPad.
techniques.latency_impact: high — eliminates AP round-trip (~1–5 ms hop each way) for close-range device-to-device; WAPerformanceMode.realtime is explicitly described as 'lower latency' vs bulk mode

techniques.name: Avoid IP_TOS / IPV6_TCLASS for Wi-Fi QoS; use SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE instead
techniques.how: Apple's XNU source includes a comment: 'Applications should use the network service type socket option instead to select L2 QoS marking instead of IP_TOS or IPV6_TCLASS.' Setting IP_TOS directly does NOT reliably map to 802.11e UP on macOS — the DSCP-to-WMM-AC mapping in the kernel's `default_dscp_to_wifi_ac_map` is designed for *incoming* DSCP-tagged packets from external networks, and the macOS Wi-Fi driver uses the MBUF service class (set by SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE) as the authoritative source for UP assignment, not the IP ToS byte.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — using the wrong API means QoS marking may silently fail; correct API ensures AC_VI is actually applied

techniques.name: Use a dedicated AP or point-to-point link for screen-sharing traffic
techniques.how: If the LAN has multiple active clients, use a dedicated 5 GHz or 6 GHz radio/SSID exclusively for the host-client pair. WMM AC prioritization is per-station and per-EDCA-queue at the AP; heavy background traffic from other clients consumes airtime and causes contention even with AC_VI marking. On a dedicated AP or in the absence of competing STAs, the EDCA contention benefit of AC_VI is maximized.
techniques.latency_impact: high — eliminates multi-STA contention that can add 5–30 ms to AC_VI transmit wait time under load

key_settings: NWParameters.serviceClass = .interactiveVideo  // video UDP flow -> AC_VI (WMM UP 5)
NWParameters.serviceClass = .responsiveData   // input/control flow -> AC_BE low-latency
setsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NET_SERVICE_TYPE, &NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI, sizeof(int))  // BSD socket equivalent
net_qos_policy_restricted = 0 (XNU default) — no entitlement needed on macOS for Wi-Fi L2 UP marking
AP channel: 5 GHz channel 44 or 149 (AWDL social channels) OR 6 GHz (no AWDL channel contention)
AP WMM: Enabled (required for QoS markings to take effect)
AP channel width: Auto/80 MHz on 5 GHz; Auto/80 or 160 MHz on 6 GHz
NWParameters.includePeerToPeer = false (or omit) for video stream; rely on infrastructure path
Wi-Fi Aware (iOS/iPadOS 26+): WASession with WAPerformanceMode.realtime + serviceClass .interactiveVideo; entitlement: com.apple.developer.wifi-aware
latency_numbers: Gigabit Ethernet LAN (wired): <1 ms RTT, near-zero jitter — source: Moonlight FAQ / Joltfly benchmark
Wi-Fi 5 GHz (Wi-Fi 6, lightly loaded): 2–5 ms RTT — source: Moonlight LAN streaming benchmarks (Joltfly 2024)
Wi-Fi 6 5 GHz vs 6E 6 GHz: 5–15 ms vs 3–8 ms RTT — source: routerhaus.com gaming latency benchmark 2025
Wi-Fi 6E 6 GHz jitter reduction: ~80% less than congested 5 GHz — source: Cudy 6 GHz benchmark (300+ records)
Wi-Fi general overhead vs Ethernet: +10–30 ms — source: routerhaus.com / various gaming benchmarks
AWDL idle-mode latency spike: 50–200 ms every 10–12 seconds — source: networkweather.com AWDL interference study
AWDL latency measured (Visser/RIPE 91, Oct 2025): 20 ms with 25 ms variance; Steam tool: 3–90 ms range — source: The Register 2025-10-23
WMM AC_VO jitter: ~7 ms vs AC_BE ~67 ms for audio — source: CableLabs Impacts-of-WMM-on-Wi-Fi (2024)
MultipeerConnectivity (AWDL) RTT: 3.99–336 ms in ~5-second cycles — source: Apple Developer Forums thread 694882
AWDL peer-to-peer throughput (130 Mbps avg): source: Yggdrasil AWDL blog post 2019
Moonlight end-to-end input latency on LAN: 12–22 ms total — source: Joltfly 2024
XNU OFDM symbol: Wi-Fi 5 = 3.2 us; Wi-Fi 6 = 12.8 us (larger for MU-MIMO/OFDMA efficiency) — source: Intel Wi-Fi tutorial
claims_to_verify: net_qos_policy_restricted defaults to 0 on macOS (i.e., all apps get Wi-Fi L2 AC_VI marking without MDM) — derived from XNU source, but may differ at runtime if the Cisco Fastlane profile changes it via sysctl
AWDL social channels on 5 GHz are specifically ch 44 and ch 149 — widely cited but derived from reverse-engineering; Apple has not officially confirmed
Wi-Fi Aware (WAPerformanceMode.realtime) is NOT available on macOS 26 — based on WWDC25 session 228 omitting macOS; requires verification in SDK headers or docs

MultipeerConnectivity cyclic RTT spike (up to 336 ms every ~5 seconds) is a current behavior on modern iOS/macOS, not a fixed regression — the forum thread (694882) had 0 replies and no Apple response

NWParameters.includePeerToPeer on macOS-to-macOS uses AWDL as the transport when both devices are in range — Apple says AWDL is an implementation detail and the system selects the path; actual behavior may vary by OS version

The claim that AWDL realtime mode is only triggered by Universal Control / Sidecar / Mac Virtual Display and has no public API prior to iOS 26 Wi-Fi Aware — sourced from Apple DTS in forum thread 819926; verify this remains true on macOS 26

6 GHz AWDL channel behavior — the research on AWDL social channels (ch 44/149 on 5 GHz) predates widespread 6 GHz deployment; it is unknown whether AWDL on 6 GHz uses different social channels that could cause similar interference
open_questions: Does Wi-Fi Aware (WAPerformanceMode.realtime) work on macOS 26, or is it iOS/iPadOS only? If macOS-only peer-to-peer is needed, is there any public API to trigger AWDL realtime mode on macOS 26?
On 6 GHz Wi-Fi 6E, does AWDL still perform social channel hopping? If so, which 6 GHz channels does it prefer? Is the interference pattern the same as 5 GHz?
What is the actual latency improvement of AWDL realtime mode vs normal mode in a controlled LAN test (i.e., with both devices in range on the same network)?

For a macOS host to iOS/iPadOS client scenario, is it possible to maintain an AWDL realtime-mode connection while simultaneously having infrastructure Wi-Fi active for other traffic, or does realtime mode consume the full radio?

Does Thunderbolt-to-Ethernet adapter on M3/M4 MacBook Pro introduce any additional latency vs the built-in Ethernet on Mac mini/Mac Pro? (USB-C adapters with RTL8153 have known reliability issues; Thunderbolt adapters may differ)

verifications.claim: net_qos_policy_restricted defaults to 0 on macOS (i.e., all apps get Wi-Fi L2 AC_VI marking without MDM) — derived from XNU source, but may differ at runtime if the Cisco Fastlane profile changes it via sysctl

verdict.claim: net_qos_policy_restricted defaults to 0 on macOS (i.e., all apps get Wi-Fi L2 AC_VI marking without MDM) — derived from XNU source, but may differ at runtime if the Cisco Fastlane profile changes it via sysctl
verdict.evidence:
XNU source (apple-oss-distributions/xnu, bsd/netinet/in_tclass.c lines 108-117) confirms the default value of 0 is correct. The comment reads: "Fastlane QoS policy: By Default allow all apps to get traffic class to DSCP mapping." However, the claim that this means "all apps get Wi-Fi L2 AC_VI marking" is wrong on two counts:

1. Default socket traffic class is SO_TC_BE (Best Effort), not VI. When restricted==0, so_set_default_traffic_class() sets so->so_traffic_class = SO_TC_BE AND sets SOF1_QOSMARKING_ALLOWED (line 995-999). The flag means a socket is *permitted* to use elevated QoS marking if it requests a higher service class (NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI, etc.) — it does NOT automatically upgrade all traffic to AC_VI. A BE socket with SOF1_QOSMARKING_ALLOWED still transmits at WMM AC_BE, not AC_VI.

2. Even on a Fastlane-capable Wi-Fi interface (IFEF_QOSMARKING_ENABLED set), the flowswitch path (fsw_qos.c lines 114-128) clamps non-whitelisted flows to BE/BK DSCP. "Whitelisted" means FLOWENTF_QOS_MARKING is set on the flow entry, which derives from SOF1_QOSMARKING_ALLOWED. So restricted=0 allows all apps to be whitelisted (receive AC elevation on Fastlane networks) IF they request a high service class — but default BE traffic stays AC_BE.

3. The MDM/Cisco Fastlane mechanism operates through the NECP policy layer (QoSMarkingPolicy MDM payload keys QoSMarkingEnabled, QoSMarkingWhitelistedAppIdentifiers), which controls per-app NECP route rules that gate FLOWENTF_QOS_MARKING. There is no evidence in XNU that a Cisco Fastlane MDM profile directly writes the net_qos_policy_restricted sysctl; rather, it installs NECP policies that restrict which app bundle IDs get FLOWENTF_QOS_MARKING set. The net_qos_policy_restricted sysctl is writable (CTLFLAG_RW) by root, so an MDM-installed launchd or kernel extension could set it, but this is not the documented MDM mechanism.
verdict.correction: The default value of net_qos_policy_restricted = 0 is confirmed by XNU source. However, the meaning is mischaracterized: restricted=0 does NOT give all apps Wi-Fi L2 AC_VI marking. It means all apps are *permitted* to receive DSCP/L2 QoS elevation when they explicitly request a higher Network Service Type (NET_SERVICE_TYPE_VI, _VO, etc.) on a Fastlane-capable interface — but the default socket traffic class is SO_TC_BE, which maps to WMM AC_BE, not AC_VI. Apps that never set a service type stay at AC_BE regardless of this sysctl. The Cisco Fastlane MDM profile changes QoS behavior through the NECP policy layer (QoSMarkingPolicy payload with QoSMarkingWhitelistedAppIdentifiers), not by directly writing the net_qos_policy_restricted sysctl; that sysctl is CTLFLAG_RW and could be set by root, but that is not the MDM mechanism documented by Apple.

verifications.claim: AWDL social channels on 5 GHz are specifically ch 44 and ch 149 — widely cited but derived from reverse-engineering; Apple has not officially confirmed

verdict.claim: AWDL social channels on 5 GHz are specifically ch 44 and ch 149 — widely cited but derived from reverse-engineering; Apple has not officially confirmed
verdict.evidence:
Every element of the claim holds up under adversarial review:

1. Channel numbers are correct. Ch 44 and ch 149 are the two 5 GHz social channels, alongside ch 6 (2.4 GHz). This is hardcoded in the OWL open-source AWDL implementation (seemoo-lab/owl) as `CHAN_OPCLASS_6`, `CHAN_OPCLASS_44`, and `CHAN_OPCLASS_149` in src/channel.h, with corresponding bit flags `AWDL_SOCIAL_CHANNEL_6_BIT`, `AWDL_SOCIAL_CHANNEL_44_BIT`, `AWDL_SOCIAL_CHANNEL_149_BIT` in src/tx.c. The channel-sequence initializer (channel.c) uses only ch 149 and ch 6 slots, matching the protocol behaviour documented in the MobiCom'18 paper.

2. Derived from reverse-engineering. The foundational source is the Seemoo Lab paper "One Billion Apples' Secret Sauce: Recipe for the Apple Wireless Direct Link Ad hoc Protocol" (arxiv:1808.03156, MobiCom'18). The companion demo paper (arxiv:1812.06743) states directly: "since AWDL devices usually meet on one social channel (6, 44, or 149)." Both papers explicitly document their methodology as empirical frame capture and analysis, not access to Apple source or specifications.

3. Apple has not officially confirmed. Apple's closest public technical document — patent US20180083858A1 — discusses social channels only in the 2.4 GHz context (ch 1, 6, 11) and never enumerates ch 44 or ch 149 as social channels. No Apple developer documentation, SDK headers, or WWDC session lists these specific channel numbers. There is no official Apple publication that names ch 44 or ch 149 as AWDL social channels.

4. "Widely cited" is accurate. The ch 6/44/149 triad appears in Wireshark's AWDL dissector, the OWL project, WLAN Professionals practitioner resources, and multiple peer-reviewed papers — all tracing back to the same Seemoo Lab reverse-engineering lineage.

Additional nuance confirmed: ch 149 is the preferred US 5 GHz social channel; ch 44 is preferred in Europe and other regulatory domains where ch 149 is not permitted. The OWL channel.c initializer defaults to ch 149, consistent with this. This regional preference detail is also from empirical observation, not an Apple specification.
sources: https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/1812.06743 (MobiCom'18 demo paper, explicit "social channel (6, 44, or 149)" quote)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1808.03156 (MobiCom'18 main paper, "One Billion Apples' Secret Sauce", Stute et al.)
https://patents.google.com/patent/US20180083858A1/en (Apple patent — social channels cited only for 2.4 GHz, no 5 GHz channel enumeration)
https://owlink.org/wiki/ (Open Wireless Link project wiki, reverse-engineering based)

verifications.claim: Wi-Fi Aware (WAPerformanceMode.realtime) is NOT available on macOS 26 — based on WWDC25 session 228 omitting macOS; requires verification in SDK headers or docs

verdict.claim: Wi-Fi Aware (WAPerformanceMode.realtime) is NOT available on macOS 26 — based on WWDC25 session 228 omitting macOS; requires verification in SDK headers or docs
verdict.evidence: Apple's shipped SDK headers are conclusive. In MacOSX26.4.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/WiFiAware.framework/Versions/A/Modules/WiFiAware.swiftmodule/arm64e-apple-macos.swiftinterface (lines 479–485), WAPerformanceMode carries the explicit compiler directive @available(macOS, unavailable), alongside @available(iOS 26.0, *). The same annotation is present in iPhoneOS26.0.sdk through iPhoneOS26.5.sdk copies of the swiftinterface. This is a compile-time unavailability declaration, not merely a documentation omission. The entire Wi-Fi Aware public API surface (WAAccessCategory, WAPerformanceReport, WAEndpoint, WASubscriberBrowser, etc.) carries the same @available(macOS, unavailable) guard. WWDC25 session 228 transcript corroborates: "Introducing Wi-Fi Aware, a new framework that enables these experiences on iPhone and iPad." Notably, blacktop/ipsw-diffs shows WAPerformanceMode symbols DO appear in the macOS 26.0 wifip2pd system daemon binary — the subsystem runs on Mac internally — but it is not exposed as public developer API.
verdict.correction: The factual conclusion is correct (WAPerformanceMode.realtime is unavailable on macOS 26), but the stated basis was imprecise. The definitive evidence is not WWDC25 session 228 omitting macOS — it is the explicit @available(macOS, unavailable) compiler directive in Apple's shipped macOS 26.4 SDK swiftinterface headers, which enforce unavailability at compile time. The underlying Wi-Fi Aware subsystem does exist on macOS (symbols appear in the wifip2pd daemon), but it is intentionally not surfaced as a public API for third-party developers.

rawCorpus.summary:
The minimum hardware decode latency achievable with VTDecompressionSession on Apple Silicon is approximately 1–3 ms per frame for both H.264 and HEVC at streaming resolutions, measured end-to-end from submit to CVPixelBuffer callback. This figure comes from Moonlight issue #1087 (2023), which reported M2 iPad decode times around 1 ms (felt as responsive as sub-1 ms Windows devices), while the same M2 chip on macOS via FFmpeg+VT produced 4–6 ms due to renderer overhead, not the hardware decoder itself. Moonlight issue #1249 (2024) reported macOS M2 Mac Mini at 2–3 ms decode time, dropping to 1.2 ms when the renderer was not the bottleneck. The macOS 26 / Tahoe regression (issue #1696, 2025) showed the Metal renderer introducing a "presentation wait timed out" condition inflating apparent decode time to 80–100 ms on older Intel Macs and ~20 ms on M4 Apple Silicon; disabling the Metal renderer reduced it back to ~2.7 ms average decode time, confirming the hardware decode engine itself is not the source of extra latency.

The single most important API choice for hitting the decode floor is the flags parameter to VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame. Passing flags = 0 (both kVTDecodeFrame_EnableAsynchronousDecompression and kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing clear) enforces synchronous, blocking decode: the call does not return until the output callback or output handler has fired with the CVPixelBuffer in hand. This eliminates any internal queuing or reorder buffering the VT framework might otherwise accumulate. kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing is particularly dangerous for latency because it explicitly licenses the decoder to delay callbacks for display-order sorting; for I-frame-only GOP or streams with no B-frames and num_reorder_frames=0 in the SPS VUI, there is nothing to sort, but the flag still gives the driver permission to introduce pipeline depth. Always omit it. kVTDecodeFrame_EnableAsynchronousDecompression allows the call to return immediately before the frame is done; for very-low-latency use this means the caller must then wait on a semaphore or condition variable, converting async back into a measured wait — equivalent latency but more complex code. Synchronous mode (flags=0) is simpler and achieves the minimum latency on Apple Silicon because the hardware decode engine completes the frame in ~1–3 ms anyway; there is no benefit to async unless you want to pipeline CPU work with the next network read concurrently.

Session-level latency controls: set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue (the default, but always set it explicitly per iOS SDK header). This property, available since macOS 10.10 / iOS 8, tells VT to run the decode pipeline at real-time priority rather than a lower background priority allowed by kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency. Setting both at once is explicitly documented as producing undefined behavior. To guarantee hardware decode and avoid a silent fallback to software (which can be 5–20× slower), set kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder = kCFBooleanTrue in the decoderSpecification dictionary passed to VTDecompressionSessionCreate; verify after creation with kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder. On iOS hardware decode is always on and no special key is needed; on macOS 10.15+ it is on by default as well, but the Require key provides a hard failure instead of a silent software fallback.

Bitstream-level latency control via SPS VUI is the second critical lever. For H.264, the bitstream_restriction_flag group in the VUI allows setting num_reorder_frames=0 and max_dec_frame_buffering=1 (minimum for a P-frame-only stream). When these are present in the SPS, a conformant hardware decoder has explicit permission to output every frame immediately after it is decoded; without them, the decoder is free to buffer up to num_reorder_frames before emitting the first frame. WebCodecs issue #732 (w3c/webcodecs, 2023) documented Apple's VideoToolbox hardware decoder buffering 4 frames after each keyframe for MAIN profile streams lacking bitstream_restriction, while BASELINE profile (which has constraint_set1_flag=1, implying num_reorder_frames=0) decoded 1-in-1-out. WebRTC's SPS VUI rewriter (sps_vui_rewriter.cc) demonstrates the canonical fix: inject or overwrite the VUI with bitstream_restriction_flag=1, num_reorder_frames=0, max_dec_frame_buffering=1 in the SPS NAL immediately before feeding it to VTDecompressionSessionCreate. For HEVC, the equivalent fields are in the SPS temporal sub-layer ordering (sps_max_num_reorder_pics[0]=0). Since the project controls the encoder, simply set these fields at encode time rather than rewriting.

The output callback must be extremely fast. The iOS SDK header comment explicitly states "the decoder is blocked until the callback returns, which can lead to decoder congestion." On Apple Silicon, the hardware decode pipeline is tight; a slow callback directly increases per-frame decode time as observed by the caller. The correct pattern is to retain the CVPixelBuffer (CFRetain / CVPixelBufferRetain), store it in a lock-free slot, and return immediately. The CVPixelBuffer backed by an IOSurface on Apple Silicon can be wrapped into a MTLTexture via CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage with zero copy — the GPU reads directly from the same unified memory page the hardware decoder wrote into. Do not request a pixel format in destinationImageBufferAttributes that differs from the decoder's native output (420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for H.264/8-bit; 420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange for HEVC/10-bit) as format mismatch triggers an additional copy. Setting kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey = true ensures the buffer is Metal-importable without a copy.

IDR/error recovery cost: tearing down a VTDecompressionSession and recreating it introduces a ~30–100 ms stall (session initialization, driver resource allocation, first-IDR decode). The lighter path when bitstream or format changes are minor is VTDecompressionSessionCanAcceptFormatDescription; if true, continue without recreation. If a decode error occurs (status < 0 in the callback), the recommended approach (as seen in ALVR visionOS source) is to request an IDR from the encoder and let the existing session recover rather than immediately recreating it. Session recreation should be a last resort for kVTVideoDecoderMalfunctionError or hard GPU timeouts. For the target use case (single-window streaming with controlled encoder), the IDR will arrive within one GOP interval; with infinite GOP + on-demand IDR, this can be as fast as the network RTT (< 1 ms on LAN).

H.264 vs HEVC decode latency: both codecs achieve similar 1–3 ms per-frame hardware decode latency on Apple M-series chips at 1080p 60fps. Moonlight issue #1087 noted that HEVC has "significantly higher decode times" than H.264 (exact delta not measured) in Moonlight's macOS implementation which routes through FFmpeg+VT. In the ALVR visionOS implementation (pure Swift, direct VTDecompressionSession, flags=0, no temporal processing), both codecs decode cleanly. The discrepancy in Moonlight is attributable to FFmpeg's VT integration calling VTDecompressionSessionWaitForAsynchronousFrames after every frame (confirmed in FFmpeg videotoolbox.c line 203), which serializes the pipeline unnecessarily. For a native Swift/ObjC implementation using the outputHandler block variant (VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrameWithOutputHandler, available macOS 10.11+/iOS 9+) with flags=0, the latency floor should be equivalent across H.264 and HEVC on Apple Silicon. HEVC Main 10 at 10-bit depth with 4:2:0 on M1+ chips is fully hardware-accelerated via Media Engine; verify with kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder after session creation.

techniques.name: Synchronous decode (flags = 0)
techniques.how: Pass decodeFlags = 0 to VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame or VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrameWithOutputHandler. This clears both kVTDecodeFrame_EnableAsynchronousDecompression (bit 0) and kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing (bit 1). The API contract (VTDecompressionSession.h) guarantees: 'If both flags are clear, the decompression shall complete and your output callback function will be called before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns.' CVPixelBuffer is in hand the moment the call returns.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates all internal VT queue depth and reorder buffering; achieves 1–3 ms floor on Apple Silicon M-series

techniques.name: Omit kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing
techniques.how: Never set bit 1 of decodeFlags. kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing explicitly licenses the decoder to delay callbacks to enable processing in display order (reordering). Even with no B-frames in the bitstream, setting this flag gives the driver permission to accumulate internal pipeline depth. The VTDecompressionProperties.h header documents that kVTDecompressionProperty_DeinterlaceMode_Temporal is 'only used if kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing is set, otherwise a non-temporal mode will be used instead' — confirming this flag enables additional internal processing stages.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — setting this flag can add multiple frame periods of buffering; omitting it is required for 1-in-1-out decode

techniques.name: Set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue
techniques.how: Call VTSessionSetProperty(session, kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime, kCFBooleanTrue) after VTDecompressionSessionCreate. Available since macOS 10.10 / iOS 8.0 (iOS SDK VTDecompressionProperties.h). The header documents: 'By default, the VideoToolbox will treat the VTDecompressionSession as though it is being used for realtime playback. Setting the property to NULL is equivalent to setting it to kCFBooleanTrue.' Setting it to kCFBooleanFalse allows VT to run the pipeline at a lower priority — explicitly avoid that. Do NOT set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency = true simultaneously — the header documents this combination results in undefined behavior.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — ensures real-time QoS scheduling of the decode pipeline; prevents OS power-saving from throttling the hardware codec

techniques.name: Require hardware decode + verify
techniques.how: In the videoDecoderSpecification dictionary passed to VTDecompressionSessionCreate, set kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder = kCFBooleanTrue. This causes VTDecompressionSessionCreate to return an error rather than silently falling back to software decode. After session creation, verify with VTSessionCopyProperty(session, kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder, ...) that the value is true. Software fallback for HEVC Main10 without the right GPU/chip produces extremely high decode latency (>30 ms). VTIsHardwareDecodeSupported(kCMVideoCodecType_HEVC) provides a pre-flight check available on macOS 10.13 / iOS 11.0+.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — software decode is 10–30× slower than hardware; HEVC Main10 software fallback is particularly severe

techniques.name: SPS VUI bitstream_restriction: num_reorder_frames=0, max_dec_frame_buffering=1
techniques.how: For H.264: encode with bitstream_restriction_flag=1 in SPS VUI, num_reorder_frames=0, max_dec_frame_buffering=1 (or max_dec_frame_buffering=0 for IDR-only; set to 1 to allow P-frames). This is set in the SPS NAL that is passed to CMVideoFormatDescriptionCreateFromH264ParameterSets. For HEVC: set sps_max_num_reorder_pics[0]=0 in the SPS. If the encoder cannot be configured, rewrite the SPS inline before session creation (WebRTC's approach in sps_vui_rewriter.cc). W3C WebCodecs issue #732 confirmed that Apple's hardware decoder with MAIN profile without bitstream_restriction buffered 4 frames per keyframe, while BASELINE (implies num_reorder_frames=0) decoded 1-in-1-out. The 'bitstream_restriction' hint allows the hardware codec to skip allocating reorder buffers.
techniques.latency_impact: high — without this, hardware decoder may hold 1–4 frames before first output, adding 1–4 frame periods (~8–33 ms at 120 fps)

techniques.name: Zero-copy CVPixelBuffer → MTLTexture via CVMetalTextureCache
techniques.how: Pass kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey = @YES in destinationImageBufferAttributes at VTDecompressionSessionCreate. Do NOT request a pixel format different from the decoder's native output (420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for 8-bit H.264/HEVC; 420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange for 10-bit HEVC). In the output callback, call CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage(nil, textureCache, pixelBuffer, nil, planePixelFormat, width, height, planeIndex, &cvTexture) for each chroma plane. This creates an MTLTexture that shares the IOSurface backing the CVPixelBuffer — no copy. Setting a mismatched pixel format in destinationImageBufferAttributes triggers a VT-internal color conversion pass which adds ~0.5–2 ms of additional latency per frame.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — avoids a memcpy of potentially 4–8 MB per 4K frame; on Apple Silicon unified memory the IOSurface is directly GPU-accessible

techniques.name: Minimal output callback / output handler
techniques.how: The iOS SDK comment on VTDecompressionOutputCallback states: 'the decoder is blocked until the callback returns, which can lead to decoder congestion.' In the callback or outputHandler block: (1) CVPixelBufferRetain the imageBuffer, (2) store the pointer atomically (e.g., an os_unfair_lock-protected pointer or a lock-free ring slot), (3) signal a semaphore or condition variable, (4) return immediately. All rendering work (Metal encode, display scheduling) must happen outside the callback on a dedicated thread. The ALVR visionOS implementation (VideoHandler.swift line 602–621) calls callback(imageBuffer) from within the handler which is the correct minimal-work pattern.
techniques.latency_impact: high — a slow callback adds directly to per-frame decode time; blocking the callback starves subsequent frames
sources: https://www.objc.io/issues/23-video/videotoolbox/ ('the decoder is blocked until the callback returns')

techniques.name: Use VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrameWithOutputHandler (block API)
techniques.how: Available macOS 10.11+ / iOS 9.0+. Create the session with outputCallback = NULL and call VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrameWithOutputHandler(..., outputHandler: ^(status, infoFlags, imageBuffer, pts, dur) { ... }). This is cleaner than the C callback variant because the block captures its own context without a global/static refcon pointer. Semantics of the flags parameter are identical to the callback variant per the iOS SDK header. The ALVR visionOS VideoHandler.swift uses this exact API (line 602: VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame with outputHandler closure).
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — equivalent performance to C callback variant; advantage is code clarity and closure capture of per-session state

techniques.name: Avoid AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer for latency-critical paths
techniques.how: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer (used by Moonlight iOS) internally queues frames in a display timeline and introduces additional buffering for presentation timing. Moonlight-qt maintainer cgutman confirmed (issue #1087 comment): 'I have not been very impressed with AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's performance on the Mac. It seems to introduce additional display latency that's not present on other platforms.' The Metal renderer path (VTDecompressionSession → CVPixelBuffer → CVMetalTextureCache → MTLTexture → Metal render pass) avoids this extra stage. Moonlight issue #1696 confirms that VT_FORCE_METAL=0 (disabling Metal renderer, falling back to AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer) actually reduced overall pipeline latency by removing a CAMetalDisplayLink presentation timeout issue on macOS 26, but in normal operation the Metal path is faster. For the target design (VTDecompressionSession → Metal), AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer is not in the decode pipeline at all.
techniques.latency_impact: high — AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds 1–3 frame periods of display buffering; Metal renderer with direct CVPixelBuffer→MTLTexture avoids this entirely

techniques.name: IDR recovery: prefer bitstream repair over session teardown
techniques.how: VTDecompressionSession creation takes ~30–100 ms (driver resource allocation, first hardware context setup). On a decode error, first check VTDecompressionSessionCanAcceptFormatDescription — if true, continue with the existing session by requesting an IDR from the encoder. The IDR will arrive within one network RTT (<1 ms on LAN). Only invalidate and recreate the session for kVTVideoDecoderMalfunctionError or when the format description actually changes (resolution, profile/level, codec). The ALVR visionOS source (VideoHandler.swift line 610) demonstrates the correct pattern: on status < 0, call resetEncoding() (which triggers IDR request) but does NOT recreate the session.
techniques.latency_impact: high — session recreation adds 30–100 ms stall; IDR-based recovery keeps the session alive and costs only 1 RTT (~<1 ms on LAN)

techniques.name: Private YCbCr MTLPixelFormat for zero-copy 10-bit decode
techniques.how: Apple Silicon exposes undocumented MTLPixelFormat values for direct YCbCr texture sampling without a shader-side unpack step. ALVR visionOS VideoHandler.swift documents these (lines 22–102): MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500, MTLPixelFormatYCBCR10_420_2P = 505, and their sRGB variants (e.g., MTLPixelFormatYCBCR10_420_2P_sRGB = 525). Setting forceFastSecretTextureFormats=true maps a 10-bit CVPixelBuffer plane directly to MTLPixelFormat(rawValue: 505), skipping the normal r16Unorm + manual luminance offset path. Source: https://github.com/WebKit/WebKit/blob/f86d3400c875519b3f3c368f1ea9a37ed8a1d11b/Source/WebGPU/WebGPU/BindGroup.mm#L43 — WebKit uses the same approach. This reduces GPU texture sampling from 2 planes + shader math to a single-plane hardware-path YCbCr sample.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — reduces GPU shader complexity; main benefit is lower GPU occupancy at high frame rates (120fps+), saving ~0.1–0.3 ms GPU time

techniques.name: Feed NALU slices as complete access unit, not per-slice
techniques.how: VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame expects a CMSampleBuffer containing a complete access unit (one full frame's worth of NALUs for H.264/HEVC in AVCC/HVCC length-prefix format). Feeding slices individually or across call boundaries causes VT to buffer waiting for frame completion, adding latency proportional to the number of slices. Build one CMBlockBuffer from all NALUs for a frame (converting Annex B 00 00 01 prefix to 4-byte length prefix), create a single CMSampleBuffer, and submit once. The Moonlight iOS VideoDecoderRenderer.m and ALVR VideoHandler.swift both demonstrate this complete-frame submission pattern.
techniques.latency_impact: high — per-slice submission causes multi-call latency; complete-frame submission triggers immediate decode start

key_settings: VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame(session, sampleBuffer, flags: 0, ...)  // Both async and temporal processing bits CLEAR
VTSessionSetProperty(session, kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime, kCFBooleanTrue)
decoderSpec[kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder] = kCFBooleanTrue
destinationAttribs[kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey] = true  // Enable zero-copy IOSurface→MTLTexture

destinationAttribs pixel format = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange (8-bit) or kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange (10-bit HEVC)  // Match native decoder output to avoid conversion
H.264 SPS VUI: bitstream_restriction_flag=1, num_reorder_frames=0, max_dec_frame_buffering=1
HEVC SPS: sps_max_num_reorder_pics[0]=0
DO NOT set kVTDecodeFrame_EnableTemporalProcessing
DO NOT set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency simultaneously with RealTime
VTDecompressionSessionCreate outputCallback: nil → use VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrameWithOutputHandler block API (macOS 10.11+/iOS 9.0+)
Verify post-creation: VTSessionCopyProperty(session, kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder, ...) == kCFBooleanTrue
latency_numbers: ~1 ms: M2 iPad decode time (Moonlight issue #1087, 2023) — felt identical to sub-1 ms Windows Intel devices; direct AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer path
~1.2 ms: M2 Mac Mini minimum decode time under high mouse activity load (Moonlight issue #1249, 2024)
2–3 ms: Typical M2 Mac Mini and M1 MacBook Pro decode time via FFmpeg+VT+AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer renderer (Moonlight issue #1249, 2024)
2.37–2.69 ms: Average decode time M4 Pro Mac Mini via VTDecompressionSession with Metal renderer enabled (Moonlight issue #1696, Metal disabled path: 2.69 ms average)
4–6 ms: M2 Mac macOS HEVC decode time via Moonlight Qt (FFmpeg+VT path) (Moonlight issue #1087, 2023)
~9 ms: Snapdragon Gen 2 Android decode time as comparison point (Moonlight issue #1087, 2023)
20–25 ms: Apparent decode time on older Intel Mac macOS 26 Tahoe in fullscreen with Metal renderer regression (Moonlight issue #1696, 2025)
80–100 ms: Apparent decode time older Intel Mac macOS 26 windowed mode with Metal renderer regression — NOT hardware decode time but presentation pipeline issue (Moonlight issue #1696, 2025)
~100 ms: WWDC21 session 10158 quoted up to 100 ms latency reduction for low-latency encoding mode vs default (encode side, not decode side)
< 1 ms: Windows hardware (Intel/AMD) decode time via NVDEC/QSV as comparison (Moonlight issue #1087 context)
claims_to_verify: kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime default is kCFBooleanTrue — SDK header says 'By default, the VideoToolbox will treat the VTDecompressionSession as though it is being used for realtime playback. Setting the property to NULL is equivalent to setting it to kCFBooleanTrue.' Verify this is still true on macOS 14+ and not overridden by system power state.

flags=0 guarantees synchronous completion — SDK header says 'If both flags are clear, the decompression shall complete and your output callback function will be called before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns.' Verify this holds for hardware (not software) decoders on Apple Silicon; there is a note in Apple Developer Forums (thread 19046) that even with flags=0 the decode may internally run asynchronously with kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous set in infoFlags.

Setting a mismatched pixel format in destinationImageBufferAttributes triggers an extra copy/conversion pass — this is widely stated but not explicitly documented in the SDK header; Apple's implementation may optimize this path on unified memory.

Private MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P (500) and MTLPixelFormatYCBCR10_420_2P (505) work in production on visionOS and macOS — these are undocumented; ALVR uses them and WebKit uses them, but Apple could remove or change them in a future OS update.

VTDecompressionSession hardware decode for HEVC Main 10 is fully hardware-accelerated on all Apple Silicon (M1+) — Moonlight source (vt_base.mm) documents that certain AMD and Intel GPUs on macOS do NOT support HEVC Main10 hardware decode; verify which M-series chips have full Main10 support in the Media Engine.

M2 iPad ~1 ms decode time — this is reported as a felt experience in Moonlight issue #1087, not an instrumentally measured value from Instruments or a dedicated latency tool; the actual measured decode time could be higher.
Session recreation takes 30–100 ms — this range is inferred from community reports; Apple has not documented VTDecompressionSessionCreate initialization latency officially.
open_questions: Does Apple's hardware HEVC decoder internally add a pipeline delay (e.g., 1–2 frame lookahead) even with flags=0 and num_reorder_frames=0 in HEVC SPS? This is observable by measuring time between submit and callback completion.

What is the exact minimum CVPixelBufferPool size VT allocates internally? Setting kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey too low may cause buffer starvation and force synchronous waits; too high wastes memory. ALVR uses 3 in its destinationImageBufferAttributes.

macOS 26 / Tahoe introduced a regression where the Metal renderer causes 'Presentation wait timed out after 100 ms' (Moonlight issue #1696). Is this a CAMetalDisplayLink API change or a VT pipeline change? Using CADisplayLink + manual present scheduling instead of CAMetalDisplayLink may be the workaround.

Is there an Apple-private property key (analogous to kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl on the encode side) for decode-side low-latency hint beyond kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime?

For HEVC Main 10 at 4:2:0 on iPhone hardware (A-series), is kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange truly zero-copy to Metal on all chips, or does the 10-bit pixel format require a VT-internal unpack step on some A-series (vs M-series) chips?

The w3c/webcodecs issue #732 documented 4-frame buffering per keyframe on Apple hardware for H.264 MAIN profile without bitstream_restriction. Has this been fixed in newer macOS/iOS versions, or does it persist as of macOS 14/iOS 17?

verifications.claim: kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime default is kCFBooleanTrue — SDK header says 'By default, the VideoToolbox will treat the VTDecompressionSession as though it is being used for realtime playback. Setting the property to NULL is equivalent to setting it to kCFBooleanTrue.' Verify this is still true on macOS 14+ and not overridden by system power state.

verdict.claim: kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime default is kCFBooleanTrue — SDK header says 'By default, the VideoToolbox will treat the VTDecompressionSession as though it is being used for realtime playback. Setting the property to NULL is equivalent to setting it to kCFBooleanTrue.' Verify this is still true on macOS 14+ and not overridden by system power state.
verdict.evidence: Verified directly against the local macOS 15.4 SDK header (/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/SDKs/MacOSX15.4.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/Versions/A/Headers/VTDecompressionProperties.h, lines 188–197) and the macOS 26.5 SDK header (same path under MacOSX26.5.sdk). Both are byte-for-byte identical on this property. The verbatim header text is: "By default, the VideoToolbox will treat the VTDecompressionSession as though it is being used for realtime playback. Setting the property to NULL is equivalent to setting it to kCFBooleanTrue." The inline comment reads: "// Read/write, CFBoolean or NULL, Optional, default is true". This wording is unchanged from iOS 13.0 SDK mirrors going back to macOS 10.10 availability. No documentation in any SDK version checked (macOS 10.8–26.5) asserts that system power state overrides this default. The adjacent property kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency (available macOS 10.14+) is the explicit opt-in for power-efficiency mode; the header explicitly warns that setting both kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency and kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime simultaneously "is unsupported and results in undefined behavior," confirming they are independent controls. There is one important nuance: the property is formally described as a "hint" to the decoder, not an enforceable contract. The decoder is told "it is OK to run this decode pipeline at a lower priority" when set to false — language implying advisory rather than mandatory behavior. Apple's documentation and headers contain no assertion that thermal pressure or App Nap can silently flip this hint; however, because it is a hint, an implementation is not contractually obligated to honor it under all system conditions. This is not documented as a known risk anywhere in Apple's materials, and no WWDC session or developer forum post found addresses it.
verdict.correction: The factual claim is correct and the quoted text is verbatim from the SDK header, unchanged through macOS 26.5. One precision gap: the property is a "hint," not an enforced contract — the decoder can technically ignore it (e.g., under extreme thermal pressure), but Apple documents no such override mechanism and provides no caveat about power state in any SDK version. For a latency-critical decoder pipeline, callers should still set this property explicitly to kCFBooleanTrue rather than relying on the default, and must NOT set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_MaximizePowerEfficiency simultaneously (undefined behavior per the same header).

verifications.claim: flags=0 guarantees synchronous completion — SDK header says 'If both flags are clear, the decompression shall complete and your output callback function will be called before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns.' Verify this holds for hardware (not software) decoders on Apple Silicon; there is a note in Apple Developer Forums (thread 19046) that even with flags=0 the decode may internally run asynchronously with kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous set in infoFlags.

verdict.claim: flags=0 guarantees synchronous completion — SDK header says 'If both flags are clear, the decompression shall complete and your output callback function will be called before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns.' Verify this holds for hardware (not software) decoders on Apple Silicon; there is a note in Apple Developer Forums (thread 19046) that even with flags=0 the decode may internally run asynchronously with kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous set in infoFlags.
verdict.evidence:
Three authoritative sources bear on the claim:

1. SDK HEADER — CONFIRMED. The macOS 26.5 SDK header (VTDecompressionSession.h, lines 170–171, copyright 2006–2023) states verbatim: "If both flags are clear, the decompression shall complete and your output callback function will be called before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns." This language is identical across all four decode-frame variants in the same header (including the WithOutputHandler, WithOptions, and WithMultiImageCapableOutputHandler overloads).

2. VTErrors.h — CONFIRMS SAME CONTRACT FROM DECODER SIDE. Lines 81–83: "With the kVTDecodeFrame_EnableAsynchronousDecompression bit clear, the video decoder is compelled to emit every frame before it returns. With the bit set, the decoder may process frames asynchronously, but it is not compelled to do so." The "compelled to emit" framing applies regardless of whether the decoder is hardware or software — the header makes no hardware/software distinction.

3. kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous OUTPUT FLAG. VTErrors.h line 107 documents this as informational: "The kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous bit may be set if the decode ran asynchronously." For this to be set with flags=0, the hardware decoder would have to run asynchronously while still satisfying the "compelled to emit" guarantee — which would mean the internal implementation is pipelined/threaded but still blocks the calling thread until completion. This is architecturally possible (the decoder could submit work to the media engine and spin/block until done, internally setting the async bit to report that the ANE/HW was used), but Apple's public documentation says nothing about this behavior and no SDK comment distinguishes hardware from software paths.

4. FORUM THREAD 19046 — NOT VERIFIABLE. The thread returns HTTP 403 and no cached version is accessible. Web search result snippets for thread 19046 consistently describe deadlock/hang issues when backgrounding the app (VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame blocking indefinitely), not kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous being set unexpectedly with flags=0. The specific claim that thread 19046 documents kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous being set even with flags=0 on hardware decoders cannot be independently confirmed from any accessible source.

5. OPEN SOURCE IMPLEMENTATIONS. Telegram (TGVTVideoView.m), ijkplayer (IJKVideoToolBoxAsync.m), WebRTC, and others were examined. Notably ijkplayer's code passes flags=0 to VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame and has the comment 'Wait for delayed frames even if kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous is not set' — this is explicitly about the case where kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous is NOT set, suggesting the authors did not observe it being set with flags=0. The ijkplayer code passes NULL (0) for infoFlagsOut, so it could not even observe that flag. No open-source implementation was found that documents or handles kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous being unexpectedly set when flags=0.

6. KEY ARCHITECTURAL NUANCE. The header's "shall complete" guarantee is a contract about the calling thread: the output callback will be called before the function returns. kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous in the output is reported in the callback's infoFlags parameter, not in the infoFlagsOut of DecodeFrame itself (which documents the decode-operation state). It is technically coherent that hardware dispatch is internal/async (triggering kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous in the callback's infoFlags) while the function still blocks the caller per contract — but this is unverified speculation about Apple Silicon behavior, not documented fact.
sources: https://github.com/bilibili/ijkplayer/blob/master/ios/IJKMediaPlayer/IJKMediaPlayer/ijkmedia/ijkplayer/ios/pipeline/IJKVideoToolBoxAsync.m:693-703 (ijkplayer flags=0 usage with async wait comment)
verdict.correction: The SDK header guarantee ("shall complete ... before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame returns") is confirmed verbatim and applies universally — the header makes no hardware/software decoder distinction. The secondary claim about Apple Developer Forums thread 19046 documenting kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous being set with flags=0 on hardware decoders is unverifiable: the thread is inaccessible (HTTP 403), and all obtainable evidence about its content indicates it discusses deadlocks/hangs during app backgrounding, not the kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous output behavior. The correct framing is: (a) flags=0 is documented to force synchronous completion of the output callback from the caller's perspective; (b) whether the Apple Silicon HW decoder internally dispatches to the ANE and reports kVTDecodeInfo_Asynchronous in the callback's infoFlags even while the calling thread is blocked is architecturally plausible but undocumented; (c) no confirmed source — SDK, WWDC, or accessible forum thread — documents this behavior as an observed edge case on Apple Silicon hardware decoders. The claim about thread 19046 specifically should be treated as unverified/hearsay until that thread content is confirmed.

verifications.claim: Setting a mismatched pixel format in destinationImageBufferAttributes triggers an extra copy/conversion pass — this is widely stated but not explicitly documented in the SDK header; Apple's implementation may optimize this path on unified memory.

verdict.claim: Setting a mismatched pixel format in destinationImageBufferAttributes triggers an extra copy/conversion pass — this is widely stated but not explicitly documented in the SDK header; Apple's implementation may optimize this path on unified memory.
verdict.evidence:
The claim has two sub-assertions, one of which is factually wrong and one of which is unverified speculation:

1. "Widely stated but not explicitly documented in the SDK header" — REFUTED for the "not documented" portion. Apple explicitly documented and demonstrated the extra copy behavior in WWDC 2014 Session 513 ("Direct Access to Video Encoding and Decoding"), where an Apple engineer stated verbatim: "the decoder inside of our VTDecompressionSession will decode to a YUV format and will ask whether this CVPixelBuffer is compatible with the requested output requirements... it will need to do an extra buffer copy to convert that YUV data to BGRA data. So extra buffer copies are bad. They decrease efficiency and they can lead to decreased battery life. So the moral story here is — don't over specify." This is Apple's own first-party documentation of the behavior (a WWDC session), not just community lore. The C header (VTDecompressionSession.h) indeed omits this detail — the header only says "Describes requirements for emitted pixel buffers. Pass NULL to set no requirements." — but the behavior is Apple-documented in official sessions.

WWDC 2020 "Decode ProRes with AVFoundation and VideoToolbox" reinforces this: "Your video pipeline will be most efficient if your renderer is able to consume buffers in a format that is native to the decoder... avoiding these buffer copies will improve your application efficiency immensely."

The FFmpeg 2017 patch (accepted into FFmpeg mainline) independently documented: "if the native format does not match with the requested format, it will be converted, which is slow" — the patch removed forced kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey to let VT output its native format.

2. "Apple's implementation may optimize this path on unified memory" — UNVERIFIED SPECULATION. No Apple documentation, WWDC session (2020–2024), SDK header, or engineering source was found that claims unified memory eliminates or reduces the format-mismatch conversion penalty. Apple's unified memory zero-copy claims (WWDC20 session 10686, WWDC21 session 10153) describe the case where formats DO match and buffers are IOSurface-backed — the GPU and media engine can share the same IOSurface with no PCIe copy. This is a different property from the format-conversion overhead. When a mismatch forces a color-space/chroma conversion, that compute work must happen regardless of memory topology; unified memory removes the PCIe transfer but not the conversion compute. There is no evidence Apple has short-circuited the conversion pass on M-series hardware.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2014/513/ — WWDC 2014 Session 513 transcript: explicit Apple engineer statement about extra buffer copy on format mismatch in destinationImageBufferAttributes
https://asciiwwdc.com/2014/sessions/513 — ASCIIwwdc transcript confirming the exact quote about BGRA mismatch forcing an extra copy

https://patchwork.ffmpeg.org/project/ffmpeg/patch/20170224094423.12401-1-nfxjfg@googlemail.com/ — FFmpeg patch justification: 'if the native format does not match with the requested format, it will be converted, which is slow'
https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2020/10090/ — WWDC 2020 Decode ProRes: 'avoiding these buffer copies will improve your application efficiency immensely'

https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2020/10686/ — WWDC 2020 Apple Silicon architecture: unified memory zero-copy applies to format-matched IOSurface-backed buffers, not to format-conversion paths

https://github.com/phracker/MacOSX-SDKs/blob/master/MacOSX11.3.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/Versions/A/Headers/VTDecompressionSession.h — SDK header confirms destinationImageBufferAttributes is documented only as 'Describes requirements for emitted pixel buffers' with no mention of copy/conversion behavior
verdict.correction: The extra copy/conversion pass on pixel format mismatch IS explicitly documented by Apple — in WWDC 2014 Session 513, Apple engineers demonstrated with a worked example that requesting a non-native format (e.g., BGRA when the decoder natively outputs YUV) forces "an extra buffer copy." The C header (VTDecompressionSession.h) is silent on this, but Apple's WWDC sessions are authoritative documentation. The claim's characterization of this as "widely stated but not explicitly documented" is therefore incorrect. The unified memory optimization speculation is unverified: Apple's zero-copy claims for unified memory address the case where formats match and buffers are IOSurface-backed (GPU-to-media-engine sharing with no PCIe transfer); there is no Apple documentation or engineering evidence that unified memory eliminates the compute cost of a format-conversion pass when a mismatch is requested via destinationImageBufferAttributes. The safe engineering conclusion remains: match the decoder's native format (typically kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for H.264/HEVC on Apple Silicon) to avoid the conversion pass entirely.

rawCorpus.summary:
The macOS display pipeline from CVPixelBuffer to photons has four potential latency contributions: (1) the vsync wait itself (8–16 ms at 60 Hz, 4–8 ms at 120 Hz), (2) the compositor/WindowServer pass, (3) draw submission timing relative to the beam, and (4) the Metal drawable queue depth. Each can be minimized but none is fully eliminable without caveats.

**CAMetalLayer + CAMetalDisplayLink is the lowest-latency path on Apple Silicon / macOS 14+.** The key knobs are: `displaySyncEnabled = false` (disables vsync-gated present, allows tearing but zero vsync wait), or `displaySyncEnabled = true` + `CAMetalDisplayLink` with `preferredFrameLatency = 1` (target one-frame latency, no extra buffering). Moonlight's Metal renderer (vt_metal.mm) uses exactly this: `m_MetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = params->enableVsync` and, on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon, creates a `CAMetalDisplayLink` with `preferredFrameLatency = 1.0f`. The `CAMetalDisplayLink` fires the callback with `update.drawable` and `update.targetTimestamp` — the app must complete rendering and commit before `targetTimestamp` to appear on the corresponding refresh cycle. Apple DTS engineers explicitly recommend 3 drawables (not 2) for the macOS/iOS compositor, and only 2 drawables if always within frame budget. Critically, `CAMetalDisplayLink` is restricted to Apple Silicon on macOS (Moonlight's code guards it with `isAppleSilicon()` check in addition to `@available(macOS 14, *)`).

**AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds at least one extra frame of latency** compared to the Metal path. Moonlight issue #1885 confirms this is perceptibly worse even with identical network/codec conditions. The AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer path buffers decoded frames internally and presents them to the compositor through a separate timing queue, while Metal with `CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage` maps the VideoToolbox CVPixelBuffer directly to a Metal texture as a zero-copy IOSurface-backed surface that is then committed to the CAMetalLayer drawable, skipping the AVFoundation presentation queue. No specific ms delta for the AVSB overhead is published, but the Moonlight issue describes it as "buffering one or more extra frames," implying at minimum 8–16 ms at 60 Hz or 4–8 ms at 120 Hz per frame.

**Fullscreen and adaptive sync:** macOS requires apps to be in fullscreen mode for adaptive sync (variable refresh rate) scheduling to take effect. NSScreen.minimumRefreshInterval != maximumRefreshInterval detects adaptive sync capability. On a 40–120 Hz adaptive-sync display, a frame that completes after just 1 ms of wait can be presented at 1 ms penalty rather than waiting up to 8 ms for the next fixed-rate vsync. Adaptive sync is supported on all Apple Silicon Macs (macOS Monterey+) and select Intel models; it requires DisplayPort/Thunderbolt (not HDMI adapters). This is only available to Metal apps in fullscreen — windowed apps do not benefit from adaptive sync scheduling.

**WindowServer bypass / direct-to-display:** macOS does not offer true exclusive-fullscreen or direct framebuffer access like Windows DirectFlip. All applications, including Metal, go through WindowServer. However, macOS does implement a "compositor fast path" that can bypass the full compositing pass when a fullscreen Metal surface is the sole content — this is distinct from the windowed case where WindowServer must composite your layer with other windows. The Zed blog reports that on M2, the "direct mode" where `waitUntilCompleted` blocks until pixels are written to the framebuffer actually performed *worse* than the composited path (longer blocking periods), so blindly enabling direct scanout is not guaranteed to help; `wait_until_scheduled` (scheduled means GPU work is submitted, not complete) proved better. This is a nuanced finding: `presentsWithTransaction` (which synchronizes Metal drawable presentation with a Core Animation transaction) is useful only when you have Metal + UIKit/CA layer hierarchy to synchronize; for a pure Metal streaming renderer it adds unnecessary complexity and latency.

**CVDisplayLink vs CAMetalDisplayLink:** CVDisplayLink fires approximately 7 ms before vsync (on a single-display setup using kernel vsync data), but this is a timer, not a true vsync interrupt. On multi-display setups it reverts to a pure timer with no vsync correlation. CAMetalDisplayLink (macOS 14+, Apple Silicon in practice for this use case) directly receives the compositor-assigned drawable with its target timestamp, providing a tighter integration with the display scheduler. ProMotion displays (120 Hz variable) on MacBook Pro M-series require CADisplayLink or CAMetalDisplayLink with a `preferredFrameRateRange` to maintain steady high-rate output; absent this, the system may downclock. For a streaming receiver where every incoming decoded frame should display ASAP, setting `CAFrameRateRangeMake(fps, fps, fps)` pins the display link to the stream frame rate.

techniques.name: CAMetalDisplayLink with preferredFrameLatency=1 (Apple Silicon / macOS 14+)
techniques.how: Create `CAMetalDisplayLink` with `initWithMetalLayer:`, set `preferredFrameLatency = 1.0` (one display cycle of latency budget), set `preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRangeMake(fps, fps, fps)` to lock to stream frame rate. In the delegate callback `metalDisplayLink:needsUpdate:`, render the latest decoded CVPixelBuffer into `update.drawable` and commit before `update.targetTimestamp`. This replaces the legacy CVDisplayLink pattern and gives the system direct control over drawable timing and beam scheduling. Moonlight's vt_metal.mm implements exactly this (startDisplayLink method, DisplayLinkDelegate).
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates the CVDisplayLink ±7 ms timer drift. Enables the system to schedule the drawable for the correct vsync cycle with minimal OS buffering. On a 120 Hz ProMotion display this means potential 4–8 ms savings over a misconfigured double-buffer path that misses a vsync.

techniques.name: displaySyncEnabled = false (no vsync wait, immediate present)
techniques.how: Set `CAMetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = NO` before presenting any drawables. Frames are presented to the display as soon as GPU work completes with no vsync gating — effectively tearing-allowed immediate mode. This is the V-sync-off path. Moonlight's Metal renderer uses `m_MetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = params->enableVsync` — when vsync is disabled, this is set to false. Note: tearing will occur on non-adaptive-sync displays. On adaptive-sync displays in fullscreen mode, this is theoretically equivalent to adaptive sync (present immediately) but the adaptive sync path via `presentDrawable:afterMinimumDuration:` is cleaner.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical — eliminates the entire vsync wait penalty (up to 16.7 ms at 60 Hz, 8.3 ms at 120 Hz). Best-case glass-to-glass improvement of ~8–17 ms at the cost of possible tearing artifacts.

techniques.name: maximumDrawableCount = 2 (minimum buffer depth)
techniques.how: Set `CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount = 2`. This allows only 2 in-flight drawables, guaranteeing that the frame most recently rendered displays on the next vsync if GPU work completes before the ~5 ms pre-vsync deadline. With 3 drawables, the queue can hold 2 pending frames, adding up to 2 × frame_interval (32–50 ms at 60 Hz) of latency in the worst case. Apple DTS recommends 3 drawables for consistent throughput, but 2 is the right choice for minimum latency when the render budget is reliably met.
techniques.latency_impact: High — prevents queued-frame latency buildup. With 3 drawables, measured latency varied 16–50 ms at 60 Hz; with 2 drawables, it converges toward ~16 ms. The trade-off is occasional `nextDrawable` blocking if GPU work overruns the frame budget.

techniques.name: CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage for zero-copy VideoToolbox → Metal path
techniques.how: After VideoToolbox decodes to a CVPixelBufferRef (IOSurface-backed), call `CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage` to create an `MTLTexture` that is a zero-copy view of the same IOSurface. This texture can be bound as a fragment shader input and rendered into the CAMetalLayer drawable in a single render pass with a CSC (YCbCr → RGB) shader. This avoids any CPU-side pixel copy and the AVFoundation presentation queue. Moonlight's vt_metal.mm uses this exact path (lines 434–450, createTexturesFromFrame).
techniques.latency_impact: High — eliminates the AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer internal frame queue (which adds at least 1 extra frame = 8–16 ms at 60 Hz). The Moonlight issue #1885 confirms this is the primary latency improvement of the Metal renderer over the AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer renderer.

techniques.name: Fullscreen mode for adaptive sync scheduling
techniques.how: Present the streaming window as macOS fullscreen (NSWindowStyleMaskFullScreen or present via NSWindow enterFullScreenMode). Check adaptive sync support with `screen.minimumRefreshInterval != screen.maximumRefreshInterval`. Use `[commandBuffer presentDrawable:drawable afterMinimumDuration:averageGPUTime]` to enable variable-duration frames. On a 40–120 Hz display, a frame completed after just 1 ms can display 1 ms later rather than waiting for the next fixed-rate slot.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — on a 120 Hz adaptive-sync display this saves up to 7–8 ms of vsync-wait latency compared to a fixed 120 Hz wait. On a 60 Hz fixed display there is no benefit. For LAN streaming apps where frame completion time is tightly bounded, the average savings is likely 3–5 ms.

techniques.name: Avoid AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer; use Metal renderer instead
techniques.how: Do not use AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer for decoded CVPixelBuffer presentation in a low-latency path. Instead, use CAMetalLayer with CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage (see above). If using VideoToolbox for decode, the decoded CVPixelBufferRef arrives in the VTDecompressionOutputCallback and can be immediately wrapped in a Metal texture and drawn. The AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer path buffers decoded frames in an AVFoundation presentation queue, which introduces at minimum one additional frame of latency on top of the compositor.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical — qualitatively reported as 'one or more extra frames' of latency in Moonlight issue #1885. At 120 Hz that is ≥8 ms; at 60 Hz that is ≥16 ms. Real-world user testing confirms a perceptible responsiveness difference in A/B comparison even with identical network and decode conditions.

techniques.name: framebufferOnly = YES (enable compositor fast path / direct scanout optimization)
techniques.how: Set `CAMetalLayer.framebufferOnly = YES` (default). This tells Metal the drawable texture is exclusively used as a render target and not sampled or read back. This allows the GPU driver and compositor to apply optimizations such as lossless compression (AFBC/AFKN on Apple GPUs) and may enable direct scanout paths when the layer is the full display surface. Setting it to NO allows shader read of the drawable but disables these optimizations.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — exact ms savings are not publicly benchmarked, but enabling lossless GPU compression reduces bandwidth in the display path and can allow compositor fast-path activation that reduces latency by avoiding a full compositor blit pass.

techniques.name: presentsWithTransaction = NO (default — async Metal present, no CA transaction sync)
techniques.how: Keep `CAMetalLayer.presentsWithTransaction = NO` (the default). When YES, the Metal drawable present is synchronized with a Core Animation commit, which means the GPU present blocks until the next CA transaction, adding unnecessary latency. For a pure streaming renderer with no UIKit/CA overlay content that needs frame-exact sync, keeping this false is the correct choice. Only set it to YES if you need Metal and CA (NSView, UI labels) to appear in the same frame.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — presentsWithTransaction = YES forces synchronization with CA's frame cycle, potentially adding up to one CA frame (~16 ms at 60 Hz) of latency. Keeping it false allows the GPU to present as early as the vsync schedule allows.

techniques.name: Use wait_until_scheduled (not waitUntilCompleted) after presenting
techniques.how: After calling `[commandBuffer presentDrawable:drawable]` and `[commandBuffer commit]`, wait using the scheduled handler or `waitUntilScheduled` rather than `waitUntilCompleted`. In the Zed 120fps engineering post, switching from `waitUntilCompleted` (which blocks until pixels are in the display framebuffer, very long in direct mode) to `wait_until_scheduled` (GPU work is queued, not necessarily rendered) eliminated excessive blocking delays in the Metal renderer, especially in fullscreen direct mode.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — avoids multi-millisecond stalls in the present call. In direct scanout mode the 'completed' state blocks until display scan starts reading pixels; this can add 5–15 ms of unnecessary blocking on the render thread, pipeline-stalling subsequent decode/render work.

techniques.name: CVDisplayLink per-display (not CVDisplayLinkCreateWithActiveCGDisplays) as fallback
techniques.how: If CAMetalDisplayLink is not available (pre-macOS 14 or non-Apple-Silicon), use `CVDisplayLinkCreateWithCGDisplay(displayId, &link)` where displayId is obtained from the NSWindow's screen, not `CVDisplayLinkCreateWithActiveCGDisplays`. The latter uses a timer-based approximation when multiple displays are active; the former tracks the actual display's vsync via kernel shared memory (~7 ms before vsync on single display setups). Update the display link if the window moves to a different screen. Moonlight's AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer renderer (vt_avsamplelayer.mm) does exactly this at lines 132–177.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — fires callback ~7 ms before vsync (empirically measured), giving a narrow window for late-commit rendering. Using the all-displays version on multi-monitor setups can cause complete vsync desynchronization, adding up to a full frame of jitter.

techniques.name: wantsExtendedDynamicRangeContent = YES only for 10-bit HDR content
techniques.how: Set `CAMetalLayer.wantsExtendedDynamicRangeContent = YES` only when streaming 10-bit HDR content (VIDEO_FORMAT_MASK_10BIT). This enables EDR tonemapping by the display subsystem. When set to YES on an SDR stream, the compositor may apply additional tone-mapping passes. Pair with `CAEDRMetadata HDR10MetadataWithDisplayInfo:contentInfo:opticalOutputScale:` (203.0 nits scale for PQ) for correct HDR10 passthrough. Moonlight's Metal renderer sets this conditionally (line 739: `m_MetalLayer.wantsExtendedDynamicRangeContent = !!(params->videoFormat & VIDEO_FORMAT_MASK_10BIT)`).
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal — enabling EDR when not needed may add a compositor tonemapping pass (~1–2 ms). More importantly, it affects pixel format selection: 10-bit paths use MTLPixelFormatBGR10A2Unorm with kCGColorSpaceITUR_2100_PQ colorspace, which changes the display path compared to 8-bit BGRA8Unorm.

techniques.name: CAFrameRateRange lock to exact stream FPS via CAMetalDisplayLink
techniques.how: Set `m_MetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRangeMake(fps, fps, fps)` where fps is the stream frame rate (e.g., 60 or 120). This prevents the ProMotion display from downclocking between frames (which would cause irregular vsync timing for the streaming app). The display link will maintain the specified rate, and the system will schedule drawables at that rate. Without this, ProMotion may drop to a lower rate during inter-frame quiet periods, then require a ramp-up that causes presentation jitter.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — prevents vsync schedule disruption on ProMotion displays, eliminating jitter spikes that can add 8–25 ms of unpredictable latency when the display transitions between refresh rates.

techniques.name: Beam-racing: render as late as possible relative to targetTimestamp
techniques.how: In the CAMetalDisplayLink callback, calculate available time as `waitTimeMs = ((targetTimestamp - CACurrentMediaTime()) * 1000) / 2` and wait up to that long for the latest decoded frame. This is the exact approach in Moonlight's renderLatestFrameOnDrawable (vt_metal.mm lines 912–937). By waiting until just before the deadline (not rendering the moment a frame arrives), you ensure the most recently decoded frame is used, minimizing the time between encode and display. The half-interval wait leaves margin for GPU encode + present.
techniques.latency_impact: High — eliminates presentation of a frame that arrived one decode cycle ago. At 120 fps / 8.3 ms intervals, the difference between 'render immediately on arrival' vs 'render latest frame just before vsync deadline' can save 4–8 ms of stale-frame latency.

key_settings: CAMetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = NO  // vsync-off: zero vsync wait, immediate present
CAMetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = YES + CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0  // vsync-on low latency
CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount = 2  // minimum queue depth, reduces max buffering to 1 frame
CAMetalLayer.framebufferOnly = YES  // enable GPU lossless compression + compositor fast path
CAMetalLayer.presentsWithTransaction = NO  // default, do NOT sync to CA transaction
CAMetalLayer.wantsExtendedDynamicRangeContent = YES  // only for 10-bit HDR streams
CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRangeMake(fps, fps, fps)  // lock ProMotion rate
CVDisplayLinkCreateWithCGDisplay(displayId, &link)  // per-display link, not all-displays (fallback path)
commandBuffer.waitUntilScheduled  // NOT waitUntilCompleted — avoids blocking until scanout
CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage(...)  // zero-copy VideoToolbox → Metal texture mapping
latency_numbers: 16.7 ms worst-case vsync wait at 60 Hz (1 missed vsync) — eliminated by displaySyncEnabled=false or adaptive sync
8.3 ms worst-case vsync wait at 120 Hz on ProMotion display
~1–2 extra frames (8–32 ms at 60 Hz) latency added by AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer vs Metal path (Moonlight issue #1885: described as 'buffering one or more extra frames')
~7 ms before vsync: CVDisplayLink fires ~7 ms before next vsync on single-display setups via kernel shared memory (Tristan Hume, 2017)
~5 ms before vsync: CAMetalLayer submission deadline to appear on next vsync (Apple Developer Forums thread/711033)
16–50 ms: measured CPU-to-display latency with maximumDrawableCount=3 vs ~16 ms with count=2 (Apple Developer Forums thread/711033)
33 ms mean latency from compositor model (16 ms render + 16 ms composite + scan — Raph Levien compositor analysis)
2–3 ms: average rendering time regression in Moonlight 6.0.0 vs 0.1–0.2 ms in 5.x (macOS M1, 120fps HEVC, measured via Moonlight overlay)
80 ms: reported decoding latency on macOS 26 / Intel Iris Plus (Moonlight issue #1696)
1 ms: minimum adaptive-sync presentation penalty on a 40–120 Hz display vs up to 8 ms on fixed 120 Hz (WWDC21 session 10147)
Under 4 ms: Zed renderer frame time on M2 MacBook, yet still subject to display sync stalls in direct mode
1.6 ms standard deviation of refresh intervals in HiDPI mode under compositor interference (Psychtoolbox macOS testing)
claims_to_verify: CAMetalDisplayLink is restricted to Apple Silicon only at runtime — Moonlight's code gates it with isAppleSilicon() check AND @available(macOS 14, *). Whether CAMetalDisplayLink officially requires Apple Silicon or just macOS 14 needs verification against Apple's official docs.

CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0 meaning: claimed to mean 'one display cycle of latency' — exact semantics (is it frames or seconds? what does the system do with this hint?) should be verified in Apple's developer documentation.
Fullscreen requirement for adaptive sync: WWDC21 says 'full-screen mode required' but the Apple Support page (102144) does NOT mention this requirement. The discrepancy should be confirmed.

waitUntilScheduled vs waitUntilCompleted latency difference: Zed reports significant blocking with waitUntilCompleted in direct mode — the exact conditions under which this occurs (only in direct/fullscreen mode? always?) need verification.
