AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer internal queue depth: described as 'one or more extra frames' — the exact number and whether it can be configured (e.g., via controlTimebase) to reduce this needs verification.

CAMetalLayer in windowed mode: whether WindowServer applies a full compositing blit pass (adding latency) versus a fast-path scanout for windowed Metal layers — Apple has not publicly documented whether windowed CAMetalLayer gets a direct-to-display optimization on Apple Silicon.

CVDisplayLink ~7 ms pre-vsync: Tristan Hume's 2017 finding that CVDisplayLink fires ~7 ms before vsync via kernel shared memory on single-display setups — this may have changed on Apple Silicon Macs with different display controller architecture.

presentsWithTransaction impact: whether setting presentsWithTransaction=YES actually delays present by up to 16 ms (one full CA frame) or only within the same render cycle. The actual latency penalty is not precisely documented.
open_questions: Does CAMetalLayer in windowed mode (not fullscreen) on Apple Silicon get a compositor fast path (IOSurface direct scanout) or does WindowServer always blit? If the former, the fullscreen requirement may be less relevant for LAN streaming.
What is the exact latency overhead of the Quartz compositor for a full-screen CAMetalLayer vs a windowed one on M-series Macs? No authoritative Apple measurement is publicly available.
Can AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's control timebase be used to achieve presentation timing parity with the Metal path for HDR content, or is the extra-frame latency architectural?

For a single-window streaming receiver (not fullscreen, sharing one window), can adaptive sync still be leveraged via CAMetalDisplayLink with afterMinimumDuration:, or is fullscreen a hard requirement for any VRR benefit?

On macOS 26 (Tahoe), are there new display pipeline APIs or changes to the compositor that affect streaming latency? The Moonlight issue #1696 reports 80 ms decode latency regression on macOS 26 with Intel GPU.
Does MTKView's automatic triple-buffering add more latency than a manually configured CAMetalLayer with maximumDrawableCount=2? The forums suggest yes but no precise measurement is available.

On an external display connected via Thunderbolt/DisplayPort on Apple Silicon, is there additional latency from the Thunderbolt retimer that doesn't exist on the built-in display? And does this affect CAMetalDisplayLink scheduling?

verifications.claim: CAMetalDisplayLink is restricted to Apple Silicon only at runtime — Moonlight's code gates it with isAppleSilicon() check AND @available(macOS 14, *). Whether CAMetalDisplayLink officially requires Apple Silicon or just macOS 14 needs verification against Apple's official docs.

verdict.claim: CAMetalDisplayLink is restricted to Apple Silicon only at runtime — Moonlight's code gates it with isAppleSilicon() check AND @available(macOS 14, *). Whether CAMetalDisplayLink officially requires Apple Silicon or just macOS 14 needs verification against Apple's official docs.
sources: Moonlight-Qt source: https://github.com/moonlight-stream/moonlight-qt/blob/master/app/streaming/video/ffmpeg-renderers/vt_base.mm:33-47 (isAppleSilicon definition via sysctlbyname hw.cputype)
verdict.correction: CAMetalDisplayLink is officially declared `API_AVAILABLE(macos(14.0), ios(17.0), tvos(17.0))` with NO Apple Silicon hardware restriction — the SDK header contains no `__arm64__` guard or architecture-specific annotation. It runs on any Intel or Apple Silicon Mac running macOS 14+. Moonlight-Qt independently adds an `isAppleSilicon()` runtime check in its `startDisplayLink()` function as an application-level guard (likely because ProMotion frame-pacing benefits are Apple Silicon-specific, or to avoid Intel-specific behavioral issues), but this is Moonlight's own policy, not Apple's API requirement. The `@available(macOS 14, *)` check in Moonlight's code reflects Apple's actual availability requirement; the `isAppleSilicon()` check is an additional, Moonlight-specific restriction beyond what Apple requires.

verifications.claim: CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0 meaning: claimed to mean 'one display cycle of latency' — exact semantics (is it frames or seconds? what does the system do with this hint?) should be verified in Apple's developer documentation.

verdict.claim: CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0 meaning: 'one display cycle of latency' — exact semantics (is it frames or seconds? what does the system do with this hint?) should be verified in Apple's developer documentation.
verdict.evidence: Apple's documentation JSON (https://developer.apple.com/tutorials/data/documentation/quartzcore/cametaldisplaylink/preferredframelatency.json) provides the authoritative answer. Abstract: "The amount of time, in frames, your app requests to render a frame." Type: Swift Float / ObjC float. Value 1.0 = one display cycle (frame) of render latency. Discussion: "The final latency may be bigger if the system needs more time, such as for windowed modes on macOS." Important callout: "The only acceptable values are 1.0 and 2.0." SDK headers from iOS 17.0 through iOS 26.5 (xybp888/iOS-SDKs) confirm the declaration as @property(nonatomic) float preferredFrameLatency with no inline comments. Apple's WWDC23 sample code (gromb57/ios-wwdc23) uses preferredFrameLatency = 2 and other open-source Metal apps use preferredFrameLatency = 1, consistent with the binary choice documented.
verdict.correction: The claim is correct that 1.0 means one display cycle of latency and that the units are frames. Two additions are load-bearing for this project: (1) The system may exceed the requested latency — it is a preferred minimum, not a hard cap (explicitly: windowed macOS modes may add more). (2) Only 1.0 and 2.0 are documented as acceptable values (Apple marks this Important), making it a binary choice rather than a continuous float. The corrected/complete statement: preferredFrameLatency is a Float in units of display frames; 1.0 requests one-frame (one display cycle) latency — the minimum the API allows; 2.0 requests two-frame latency. The system may schedule with higher latency than requested when needed. No other float values are documented as valid.

verifications.claim: Fullscreen requirement for adaptive sync: WWDC21 says 'full-screen mode required' but the Apple Support page (102144) does NOT mention this requirement. The discrepancy should be confirmed.

verdict.claim: Fullscreen requirement for adaptive sync: WWDC21 says 'full-screen mode required' but the Apple Support page (102144) does NOT mention this requirement. The discrepancy should be confirmed.
verdict.evidence:
Both halves of the original claim hold up under adversarial scrutiny:

1. WWDC21 session 10147 ("Optimize for variable refresh rate displays") explicitly states the fullscreen requirement. The session presents Objective-C code for `isWindowFullscreen:` (checking `NSFullScreenWindowMask`) and combines it with the Adaptive Sync detection check into a single `isAdaptiveSyncSchedulingEnabled:` method, with the spoken line: "And lastly, your app needs to be running in full-screen mode." Independent session notes at saurabhs.org confirm verbatim: "App must be running in full-screen mode on supported display to do Adaptive-Sync."

2. Apple Support article 102144 (fetched directly; last updated April 07 2026, so current) contains exactly 2,990 characters of body text. A full-text scan for the word "full" returns zero matches. The article lists only three requirement categories: macOS version (Monterey or later), compatible Mac hardware (Apple Silicon or specific Intel models), and cable/port type (Thunderbolt/DisplayPort, not HDMI). No fullscreen or windowed-mode constraint appears anywhere in the article.

The discrepancy is real and confirmed: the developer-facing WWDC session imposes a hard fullscreen constraint for the Adaptive Sync scheduling APIs (NSScreen minimumRefreshInterval / maximumRefreshInterval + the combined check), while the end-user support article 102144 is silent on this constraint entirely. This is not a contradiction — the support article describes enabling the display feature at the OS level (System Settings > Displays > Refresh Rate), while WWDC21 describes the additional per-app requirement for an app to benefit from adaptive sync frame scheduling through the Metal/AppKit APIs. Both documents are accurate within their own scope; the discrepancy is one of audience and scope, not factual conflict.

Implication for the screen-sharing project: a single-window (non-fullscreen) sharing app on macOS will NOT benefit from adaptive sync frame scheduling via the WWDC21 APIs. The display itself can still be set to a variable refresh rate by the user, but the NSScreen minimumRefreshInterval/maximumRefreshInterval pacing path explicitly requires the window to carry NSFullScreenWindowMask. Windowed rendering falls back to the fixed-rate compositor path.
verdict.correction: The claim as stated is accurate and confirmed. No correction needed. The precise characterization is: WWDC21 session 10147 imposes a full-screen window requirement specifically for the Adaptive Sync *scheduling* APIs (the NSScreen minimumRefreshInterval/maximumRefreshInterval + NSFullScreenWindowMask combined check). Apple Support 102144 describes how to *enable* Adaptive Sync at the display/OS level and correctly omits the per-app full-screen constraint because that constraint is irrelevant to end users toggling the display setting. The discrepancy is real but stems from the two documents addressing different layers (OS display feature vs. app-level scheduling API), not from one being wrong.

rawCorpus.summary:
iOS/iPadOS display latency from decoded frame to photons has several distinct contributors that must each be minimized. The theoretical floor at 120 Hz is approximately 8.33 ms worst-case vsync wait (half of 60 Hz's 16.67 ms); on 60 Hz non-ProMotion devices the worst-case vsync wait is 16.67 ms. In practice, the iOS Render Server adds one additional frame of pipeline latency, so the effective decoded-frame-to-photon budget is roughly 1–2 frame periods, meaning 8–17 ms on ProMotion hardware and 17–33 ms on 60 Hz devices — assuming the renderer hits its commit deadline. The iOS compositor does NOT bypass itself for CAMetalLayer; Metal drawables still pass through Core Animation's Render Server before scanout, but CAMetalLayer skips UIKit's CALayer compositing overhead and goes more directly to the GPU, making it meaningfully lower latency than going through the full UIKit tree.

For the decode→display path in a streaming receiver, the decision between AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer and Metal (CAMetalLayer/MTKView) is critical. AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds buffering latency — reports from Moonlight developers describe it as "buffering one or more extra frames" of cursor/display lag versus forced Metal rendering (VT_FORCE_METAL=1 on macOS). It also lacks presentedHandler feedback making vsync deadline estimation impossible. The preferred path for minimum latency is: VideoToolbox decode → CVPixelBuffer → CVMetalTextureCache zero-copy texture (no CPU copy, IOSurface-backed) → CAMetalLayer drawable → Metal render pass → presentDrawable → compositor. CAMetalDisplayLink (iOS 17+) is Apple's recommended driver for this loop, replacing CADisplayLink for Metal work; it provides the drawable directly in its Update struct alongside targetTimestamp (the deadline for compositor compositing), eliminating the mismatch between display-link callbacks and drawable acquisition.

ProMotion devices support dynamic refresh rates. iPhone 13 Pro and newer Pro iPhones support 12 discrete rates from 10–120 Hz; iPad Pro supports 5 rates from 24–120 Hz. iPhone 17 (non-Pro) is the first standard iPhone to gain 120 Hz (2025, LTPS panel without full LTPO ProMotion granularity). To unlock ProMotion in an app: add CADisableMinimumFrameDurationOnPhone = true to Info.plist AND set CADisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange (or CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange) to CAFrameRateRange(minimum:30, maximum:120, preferred:120). Without the plist key, the system caps at 60 Hz on iPhone regardless of the API call. Low Power Mode and the "Limit Frame Rate" accessibility setting both hard-cap at 60 Hz regardless of app configuration. At 120 Hz, the per-frame budget is 8.33 ms for all CPU+GPU work, which is the tightest constraint: Flutter/Impeller measurements show that premature nextDrawable acquisition can eat 4 ms of that 8.33 ms budget. Defer drawable acquisition as late as possible — immediately before encoding the on-screen render pass.

Key optimizations to minimize iOS display latency: (1) Set CAMetalLayer.opaque = true — empirically reduces presentation delay from ~21–26 ms to ~13 ms on ProMotion devices (Flutter/Impeller measurement). (2) Use maximumDrawableCount = 2 cautiously on iOS — Apple notes 2 drawables is NOT recommended for iOS/iPadOS due to increased nil nextDrawable risk, but it guarantees next-vsync presentation if the frame completes; 3 drawables is safer but introduces variable 16–50 ms CPU-to-display latency with triple buffering. (3) Use CAMetalDisplayLink (not CADisplayLink) on iOS 17+ for Metal rendering — it delivers the drawable and targetTimestamp together, enabling precise frame pacing. Set preferredFrameLatency = 1.0 (minimum) to request the tightest render-to-display pipeline. (4) For a streaming receiver running at a fixed stream frame rate (e.g. 60 fps) on a 120 Hz display, set CAFrameRateRange(minimum:60, maximum:120, preferred:60) — this lets the system pick 60 Hz from the ProMotion menu, which is a clean divisor, avoiding phase jitter. Setting preferred higher (120) on a 60 fps stream just double-scans frames without benefit.

techniques.name: CAMetalDisplayLink with preferredFrameLatency = 1.0
techniques.how: Use CAMetalDisplayLink (iOS 17+, introduced at WWDC 2023) instead of CADisplayLink for Metal rendering. Set preferredFrameLatency = 1.0 (minimum; requests system schedule render exactly 1 frame ahead of display). The delegate callback receives a CAMetalDisplayLink.Update struct containing both the MTLDrawable and targetTimestamp — the deadline by which your GPU work must complete for that frame's compositor commit. Configure: let link = CAMetalDisplayLink(layer: metalLayer); link.preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRange(minimum:60, maximum:120, preferred:120); link.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0; link.delegate = self. In metalDisplayLink(_:needsUpdate:) use update.drawable and present before update.targetTimestamp.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates mismatch between display-link callbacks and drawable availability; reduces nextDrawable blocking that could consume up to 4 ms of the 8.33 ms budget at 120 Hz

techniques.name: CADisableMinimumFrameDurationOnPhone plist key + CAFrameRateRange for 120 Hz
techniques.how: Add CADisableMinimumFrameDurationOnPhone = true (Boolean) to Info.plist. This is required to unlock ProMotion on iPhone; without it the display link is capped at 60 Hz regardless of preferredFrameRateRange calls. Then set preferredFrameRateRange on CADisplayLink or CAMetalDisplayLink: displayLink.preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRange(minimum: 60, maximum: 120, preferred: 120). CAFrameRateRange is a struct with Float fields: minimum, maximum, preferred. For a streaming receiver at 60 fps stream rate, set preferred:60, maximum:120 so the system picks the clean 60 Hz divisor on 120 Hz hardware — avoiding phase jitter.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — halves worst-case vsync wait from 16.67 ms (60 Hz) to 8.33 ms (120 Hz) on ProMotion devices

techniques.name: CAMetalLayer.opaque = true
techniques.how: Set metalLayer.opaque = true before first drawable acquisition. Flutter/Impeller measurements found this reduces CPU-to-display presentation latency from the 21–26 ms range down to ~13 ms on high refresh rate iOS devices. The mechanism is that an opaque layer allows Core Animation to skip alpha compositing passes in the Render Server, enabling a faster path to scanout. This is a single-line change: metalLayer.isOpaque = true (Swift) or metalLayer.opaque = YES (ObjC).
techniques.latency_impact: high — ~8–13 ms reduction in presentation delay empirically measured on ProMotion iPhones

techniques.name: Deferred drawable acquisition (acquire immediately before on-screen render pass)
techniques.how: Do not call CAMetalLayer.nextDrawable() or MTKView.currentDrawable at the start of the frame. Instead, complete all CPU work (decode, CVMetalTextureCache texture creation, off-screen passes) first, then acquire the drawable immediately before encoding the on-screen render pass. With CAMetalDisplayLink this is automatic: the Update.drawable is provided in the callback at the correct time. With manual CAMetalLayer: call nextDrawable() only after all other GPU submissions. This is the primary technique to avoid the 4 ms blocking penalty documented in Flutter/Impeller at 120 Hz.
techniques.latency_impact: high — reclaims up to ~4 ms of the 8.33 ms per-frame budget at 120 Hz that is otherwise lost blocking on drawable acquisition

techniques.name: CVMetalTextureCache zero-copy path from VideoToolbox CVPixelBuffer to Metal texture
techniques.how: Configure VideoToolbox decode session to output IOSurface-backed CVPixelBuffers (default on Apple Silicon). Create a CVMetalTextureCache: CVMetalTextureCacheCreate(kCFAllocatorDefault, nil, mtlDevice, nil, &textureCache). In the decode callback: call CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage passing the decoded CVPixelBuffer, pixel format (e.g. MTLPixelFormatBGRA8Unorm or MTLPixelFormatYCbCr_420 variants), and plane index. Extract the MTLTexture via CVMetalTextureGetTexture(). This avoids any CPU-side copy — the IOSurface that VideoToolbox wrote to is the same memory the Metal shader reads from. Keeps MTLTexture-to-IOSurface mapping alive via the cache pool.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates CPU copy of decoded frame (~1–3 ms on large frames); ensures GPU can access the decoded buffer with zero extra memcpy latency

techniques.name: Prefer Metal (CAMetalLayer) over AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer for minimum latency
techniques.how: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer has an internal queue and timing model that introduces at least one extra frame of buffering versus a direct Metal render path. Moonlight reports AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer gives 'noticeably laggy cursor/display feel' that feels like 'buffering one or more extra frames' versus Metal (VT_FORCE_METAL=1). It also lacks addPresentedHandler feedback for vsync deadline estimation. For minimum latency: decode with VideoToolbox, use CVMetalTextureCache to get an MTLTexture, render a full-screen quad (or blit) to a CAMetalLayer drawable, and present. Only consider AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer for HEVC Main 10 HDR display path where the system compositor handles tone mapping — not when minimizing decode-to-photon latency is the primary goal.
techniques.latency_impact: high — avoids ~1 extra frame (~8–17 ms) of buffering latency intrinsic to AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's internal queue model

techniques.name: maximumDrawableCount = 2 for minimum pipeline depth
techniques.how: Set metalLayer.maximumDrawableCount = 2 (down from default 3) to eliminate one frame of queuing latency. With 3 drawables, CPU-to-display latency varies between 16–50 ms; with 2 drawables, next-vsync presentation is nearly guaranteed when frames complete in time. Caveat: Apple explicitly notes 2 drawables is 'not recommended for iOS/iPadOS' due to higher nextDrawable nil risk under system pressure. Requires robust nil handling and frame drop logic. Pair with a render semaphore (dispatch_semaphore_wait with value=1) to prevent CPU racing ahead. At 120 Hz with 8.33 ms frame budget and fast decode (<3 ms), this is feasible.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — reduces maximum pipeline-queued latency from ~50 ms to ~8–17 ms (1 frame) when frames complete in budget

techniques.name: ProMotion display rate selection: align stream frame rate to display divisor
techniques.how: Set CAFrameRateRange such that the preferred rate is a clean divisor of the display's maximum rate to avoid phase mismatch. On 120 Hz hardware: 60 fps stream → set preferred:60 (120/2); 30 fps stream → set preferred:30 (120/4). If the stream runs at a non-divisor rate (e.g. 50 fps), the display link will fire at 60 Hz (nearest factor) and every 12th frame will be double-scanned. For a remote desktop app targeting LAN at 60 fps, CAFrameRateRange(minimum:60, maximum:60, preferred:60) locks to 60 Hz and avoids phase jitter entirely. If targeting variable rates, use CADisplayLink.targetTimestamp to determine actual frame interval and skip/hold frames to match.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — eliminates up to 8.33 ms phase jitter from stream/display rate mismatch on ProMotion hardware

techniques.name: presentsWithTransaction for deterministic presentation timing
techniques.how: Set metalLayer.presentsWithTransaction = true to have the drawable presented as part of a Core Animation transaction rather than asynchronously. This ties Metal's present to the CA commit cycle, making frame timing more deterministic and reducing presentation jitter. Best used from the main thread or a render thread that commits a CA transaction. Reports suggest it reduces nextDrawable blocking behavior from a background thread and stabilizes frame timing at the cost of requiring the render thread to be synchronized with the CA transaction commit.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — improves frame timing stability; can reduce effective latency variance from ±8 ms to lower jitter

techniques.name: UIUpdateLink (iOS 18+) with low-latency mode
techniques.how: UIUpdateLink is a new iOS 18 API (UIKit) that replaces CADisplayLink for UIKit-hosted animations with additional features: automatic view tracking (adjusts to the display the view is on), low-latency mode for drawing applications, and better power management. Set requiresContinuousUpdates = true for streaming. The isPerformingLowLatencyPhases property on UIUpdateInfo indicates when the update is in a low-latency dispatch phase. For a Metal streaming receiver that uses UIKit hosting, prefer UIUpdateLink over CADisplayLink; for pure Metal CAMetalLayer without UIKit, CAMetalDisplayLink remains the right choice.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — provides tighter UIKit event dispatch latency for drawing scenarios; quantified numbers not published by Apple

techniques.name: targetTimestamp usage in render callbacks for frame prediction
techniques.how: In any display-link callback (CADisplayLink, CAMetalDisplayLink, UIUpdateLink), use targetTimestamp (not timestamp) for all animation and rendering decisions. targetTimestamp is the time at which the next frame will be composited by Core Animation — it is the deadline for GPU work submission. Use CACurrentMediaTime() vs targetTimestamp to compute remaining headroom. For a streaming receiver: in the CAMetalDisplayLink callback, check if a decoded frame's presentation timestamp matches the targetTimestamp window, and skip/hold frames accordingly to avoid phase jitter.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — eliminates animation jitter during frame rate transitions and ensures rendered content is timed to the actual scanout deadline rather than the callback invocation time

techniques.name: Disable Low Power Mode / handle thermal throttling gracefully
techniques.how: Low Power Mode hard-caps ProMotion to 60 Hz, halving the effective frame rate and doubling worst-case vsync wait. The app cannot override this. Similarly, the accessibility 'Limit Frame Rate' setting caps at 60 Hz. Check UIScreen.main.maximumFramesPerSecond at runtime (returns 120 even in Low Power Mode — it reflects hardware capability, not current capability). Instead, read the actual rate from CADisplayLink.duration (= 1.0/actualHz) in callbacks. For a streaming app, adapt the stream frame rate request to the client's current display rate rather than assuming 120 Hz is active.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — operational note; cannot improve beyond the 60 Hz limit when Low Power Mode is active

key_settings: Info.plist: CADisableMinimumFrameDurationOnPhone = true (required for ProMotion on iPhone)
CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange = CAFrameRateRange(minimum: 60, maximum: 120, preferred: 120)
CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency = 1.0
CAMetalLayer.opaque = true (reduces presentation delay to ~13 ms)
CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount = 2 (reduces pipeline depth; handle nil drawable)
CAMetalLayer.presentsWithTransaction = true (ties present to CA transaction for deterministic timing)
CVMetalTextureCacheCreate + CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage (zero-copy VideoToolbox → Metal path)
CADisplayLink.preferredFrameRateRange (for non-Metal rendering path)
UIUpdateLink.requiresContinuousUpdates = true + low-latency mode (iOS 18+, UIKit path only)
latency_numbers: Worst-case vsync wait at 60 Hz: 16.67 ms (1 frame period)
Worst-case vsync wait at 120 Hz: 8.33 ms (1 frame period) — halved by ProMotion
CPU-to-display latency with maximumDrawableCount=3: 16–50 ms variable (source: Apple Developer Forums thread/711033)
CPU-to-display latency with maximumDrawableCount=2: ~8–16 ms when frame completes in budget (source: Apple Developer Forums thread/711033)
CAMetalLayer with opaque=true: ~13 ms presentation delay (vs 21–26 ms without; source: Flutter/Impeller issue #134959)
nextDrawable blocking at 120 Hz: up to ~4 ms of 8.33 ms budget (source: Flutter/Impeller issue #138490)
Apple WWDC 2015 specification: maximum touch latency 2 frames / 32.2 ms at 60 Hz (source: Apple Developer Forums thread/71301)
Empirical touch latency measurement (iPhone 6, 2017): ~5 frames / ~100 ms discrepancy vs Apple spec (source: Apple Developer Forums thread/71301)
AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer vs Metal latency delta: qualitative 'one or more extra frames' (~8–17 ms) (source: Moonlight issue #1885)
iOS Render Server pipeline: commit hitch = +16.67 ms penalty at 60 Hz; +8.33 ms at 120 Hz per missed vsync (source: WWDC 2021 tech talks 10855–10857)
Parsec LAN claimed latency: 7 ms (source: Parsec marketing; unverified for iOS client specifically)
DeskIn measured latency: 38 ms glass-to-glass in 4K mode (source: Orangesoft case study)
claims_to_verify: CAMetalLayer.opaque = true reduces presentation latency to ~13 ms on ProMotion iPhones — observed in Flutter/Impeller testing but not documented by Apple as a guaranteed behavior
maximumDrawableCount = 2 is 'not recommended for iOS/iPadOS' per Apple — exact source within Apple docs needs confirmation (referenced in forum discussions but not in official best-practices doc)

AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds 'one or more extra frames' of buffering latency versus Metal on iOS — the macOS observation from Moonlight may not directly translate to iOS where the compositor architecture differs

CAMetalDisplayLink.preferredFrameLatency minimum value is 1.0 — the exact minimum and semantics need verification against the actual header (limneos.net had SSL error; could not confirm from runtime header)

CADisableMinimumFrameDurationOnPhone vs CADisableMinimumFrameDuration — there are two distinct plist keys; only the Phone variant unlocks iPhone ProMotion; iPad may use the other key without plist requirement
iPhone 17 120 Hz is LTPS (fixed 120 Hz) not LTPO (adaptive ProMotion) — meaning no 1–10 Hz adaptive range; verify the CAFrameRateRange behavior differs

UIUpdateLink low-latency mode property name — 'wantsLowLatencyEventDispatch' was not confirmed; the exact Swift property name for enabling low-latency phase in UIUpdateLink needs verification from iOS 18 headers
Apple Vision Pro R1 chip 12 ms photon-to-photon claim applies to visionOS passthrough, not to standard iOS display pipeline — do not conflate with iPhone/iPad display latency
open_questions: Does CAMetalDisplayLink on iOS actually bypass the Render Server for display, or does it still pass through Core Animation compositing (adding ~1 frame of pipeline latency)?
What is the exact scanner latency from drawable present() to first photon on iPhone 16 Pro at 120 Hz? No publicly measured number found.

For a 60 fps remote desktop stream displayed on a 120 Hz ProMotion device: is setting preferred:60 in CAFrameRateRange better than preferred:120 and double-scanning? What is the phase jitter impact in practice?
Does AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer with kCMSampleAttachmentKey_DisplayImmediately = true actually bypass its internal queue and present at next vsync, making it viable for low-latency streaming on iOS?
Is the com.apple.developer.low-latency-streaming entitlement (visionOS) also available or relevant on iOS/iPadOS for a LAN streaming app?

Does Metal's direct-to-display path on Apple Silicon iPhones/iPads have a private fast path that skips WindowServer, similar to how CoreAnimation can use direct display access on macOS in fullscreen exclusive mode?

verifications.claim: CAMetalLayer.opaque = true reduces presentation latency to ~13 ms on ProMotion iPhones — observed in Flutter/Impeller testing but not documented by Apple as a guaranteed behavior

verdict.claim: CAMetalLayer.opaque = true reduces presentation latency to ~13 ms on ProMotion iPhones — observed in Flutter/Impeller testing but not documented by Apple as a guaranteed behavior
verdict.evidence: The "~13 ms" figure originates from a single empirical observation by Flutter engineer jonahwilliams in flutter/flutter issue #134959 (September 2023). The exact quote: "if I follow the advice in the profile above and set the CAMetalLayer.opaque property to YES, then presentation delay drops to 13ms." The issue author provided no mechanistic explanation for WHY opaque reduces latency, and explicitly noted it "may be an option for regular flutter apps but not add2app" — meaning it was already known to be situational. Flutter did not adopt opaque=YES as its shipped solution; instead PR #48226 introduced FlutterMetalLayer, a custom IOSurface-backed layer bypassing CAMetalLayer's drawable acquisition entirely. From Apple's side: (1) CAMetalLayer does not have its own opaque property — it inherits CALayer.isOpaque; (2) Apple's Core Animation guide says opaque=YES lets the compositor skip alpha-blending "saves time during rendering" but gives no latency numbers; (3) Apple's Metal Performance HUD docs distinguish a "direct path" (least buffering) from a "composited path" (additional buffering and display latency) but do not tie this distinction to the opaque property or give a 13 ms figure; (4) Apple's ProMotion optimization documentation makes no mention of opaque/latency relationship; (5) no WWDC session found that quantifies opaque's effect on presentation latency. The claim's caveat — "not documented by Apple as guaranteed behavior" — is accurate. The 13 ms number itself is plausible (it sits between one 120 Hz frame ~8.33 ms and two frames ~16.67 ms) but is a single profiling snapshot, not a reproducible baseline. The claim conflates a one-time engineering measurement with a behavioral property of the opaque flag itself; the actual benefit may come from the compositor placing an opaque-marked layer on a faster composition path (reducing pipeline stages) rather than any explicit latency guarantee tied to opaque. The claim is therefore neither confirmed (insufficient evidence the effect is reproducible or that opaque is the causal variable) nor refuted (the measurement was real and the mechanism is plausible), making "uncertain" the correct verdict.
verdict.correction: The claim is imprecise in two ways: (1) The ~13 ms figure is a single profiling observation by one Flutter engineer in a specific test setup, not a reproducible measured baseline for the opaque flag in general. Flutter's shipped fix for the latency problem was not setting opaque=YES but rather replacing CAMetalLayer with a custom IOSurface-backed FlutterMetalLayer (PR #48226). (2) The opaque property is inherited from CALayer.isOpaque — it is not a property native to CAMetalLayer — and Apple documents its effect only as allowing the compositor to skip alpha-blending (a rendering cost reduction), with no mention of ProMotion, frame-pacing, or any ms-level presentation timing guarantee. A more accurate statement: "Setting CALayer.isOpaque = true on a CAMetalLayer may reduce presentation latency on ProMotion devices by enabling a faster compositing path that skips alpha-blending, with one Flutter/Impeller profiling session measuring ~13 ms; however, this is an undocumented side effect with no guarantee from Apple, the effect is use-case-dependent (not applicable to add2app or transparent views), and Flutter's actual solution to its ProMotion latency problem was a custom IOSurface-backed layer, not this flag."

verifications.claim: maximumDrawableCount = 2 is 'not recommended for iOS/iPadOS' per Apple — exact source within Apple docs needs confirmation (referenced in forum discussions but not in official best-practices doc)

verdict.claim: maximumDrawableCount = 2 is 'not recommended for iOS/iPadOS' per Apple — exact source within Apple docs needs confirmation (referenced in forum discussions but not in official best-practices doc)
verdict.evidence:
No Apple documentation, SDK header, WWDC session, or official forum post contains "not recommended for iOS/iPadOS" language for maximumDrawableCount = 2.

1. The official Apple documentation for CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/quartzcore/cametallayer/2938720-maximumdrawablecount) defines the ONLY constraint as: valid values are 2 or 3. This is corroborated identically across multiple independent open-source Metal wrappers (golang/exp, hajimehoshi/ebiten, slint-ui/slint, hexops/mach) that cite the Apple docs directly with the text: "It can set to 2 or 3 only." There is no platform split — the constraint applies equally to iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and tvOS.

2. The only official Apple engineer statement found about the trade-off of value 2 (Apple Developer Forums thread 105308, from an Apple Graphics & Games Engineer) is: "Be aware that this makes it more likely -[CAMetalLayer nextDrawable] or MTKView equivalents will return nil forcing you to drop a frame." This is a general performance trade-off warning applicable on all platforms, not an iOS/iPadOS-specific "not recommended" designation.

3. The Apple Metal Best Practices Guide Triple Buffering page (https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/3DDrawing/Conceptual/MTLBestPracticesGuide/TripleBuffering.html) recommends triple buffering for CPU-side dynamic data buffer management (the kMaxInflightBuffers = 3 pattern) and does not mention maximumDrawableCount at all, nor does it make any platform-specific recommendation about drawable pool size.

4. The Apple Metal Best Practices Guide Drawables page makes no mention of maximumDrawableCount or any iOS-specific restriction on buffering count.

5. The iOS runtime header for CAMetalLayer (nst/iOS-Runtime-Headers) declares maximumDrawableCount as a plain unsigned long long property with no range comments.

The claim as originally stated conflates two distinct concepts: (a) Apple's triple-buffer recommendation for CPU data buffers (general, not iOS-specific, not about maximumDrawableCount), and (b) the actual maximumDrawableCount property, for which Apple's only documented constraint is the range [2, 3] with no platform differentiation.
verdict.correction: Apple does NOT designate maximumDrawableCount = 2 as "not recommended for iOS/iPadOS." The only official Apple constraint on this property is that values outside [2, 3] are invalid (the setter raises an exception for out-of-range values) — this applies uniformly across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and tvOS. The sole trade-off warning from an Apple engineer is that value 2 increases the likelihood of nextDrawable returning nil (forcing a dropped frame), which is a platform-neutral performance consideration. There is no iOS/iPadOS-specific prohibition or "not recommended" guidance anywhere in Apple's official documentation, SDK headers, or WWDC content. Using maximumDrawableCount = 2 on iOS/iPadOS is fully supported and is the correct choice for minimizing glass-to-glass latency.

verifications.claim: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds 'one or more extra frames' of buffering latency versus Metal on iOS — the macOS observation from Moonlight may not directly translate to iOS where the compositor architecture differs

verdict.claim: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds 'one or more extra frames' of buffering latency versus Metal on iOS — the macOS observation from Moonlight may not directly translate to iOS where the compositor architecture differs
verdict.evidence:
The claim conflates two sub-claims that have different evidentiary standing:

SUB-CLAIM A: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds "one or more extra frames" of buffering on iOS vs Metal.
No authoritative source — Apple documentation, WWDC sessions, SDK headers, or peer-reviewed work — quantifies AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's internal buffering overhead on iOS in frame count terms. The "one or more extra frames" phrasing originates from a qualitative user-perception report in moonlight-qt issue #1885 (macOS, Radeon Pro 580 discrete GPU): "It feels like cursor movement is consistently behind, as if the display path is buffering one or more extra frames." This was macOS-only, subjective, and contains no measured frame counts or millisecond figures.

Moonlight-iOS (VideoDecoderRenderer.m, the entire iOS renderer) uses only AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer — there is no Metal renderer alternative on iOS and no developer-documented "extra frames" complaint in moonlight-ios issues. The absence of a Metal comparison path on iOS means no apples-to-apples A/B measurement has been published.

SUB-CLAIM B: The macOS observation may not translate to iOS because the compositor architectures differ.
This part is well-supported. macOS uses WindowServer as its master compositor; all app windows pass through it before reaching the display. iOS uses backboardd for event routing and compositing. The structural differences are real and documented. iOS's display pipeline is known to have a more direct hardware video plane path for AVFoundation layers. This architectural difference is a legitimate reason to expect the macOS-specific overhead (believed to be related to WindowServer's handling of CVDisplayLink timing and the dGPU path) may not appear identically on iOS.

However, "may not directly translate" stops short of "definitely does not" — no source confirms the iOS compositor eliminates the overhead, and no measured iOS latency benchmark comparing AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer against Metal exists in the public record.

ADDITIONAL CONTEXT: The Orangesoft case study measured AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer at 350ms total end-to-end latency vs a Metal-based custom decoder at lower latency on iPhone XS, but this included the full decode pipeline (RTP parsing, network, etc.) and cannot be attributed to the display layer alone. Apple's kCMSampleAttachmentKey_DisplayImmediately API bypasses timestamp-based queuing, which can reduce queued-frame latency on both platforms, but this is about enqueue policy, not internal compositor buffering.

VERDICT: The specific framing ("adds one or more extra frames on iOS") is unsubstantiated for the iOS platform. The cautionary observation that macOS behavior may not translate to iOS is reasonable given architectural differences. Neither side of the claim has been confirmed or refuted with hard measurements on iOS.
sources: https://github.com/moonlight-stream/moonlight-qt/issues/1885 — macOS-specific user report of subjective 'one or more extra frames' buffering with AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer
https://github.com/moonlight-stream/moonlight-ios (Limelight/Stream/VideoDecoderRenderer.m) — Moonlight-iOS uses only AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer; no Metal renderer alternative exists on iOS
https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2014/513/ — WWDC 2014 Session 513: AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer internal decode queue explained; no frame-count buffering overhead specified

https://developer.apple.com/documentation/coremedia/kcmsampleattachmentkey_displayimmediately — kCMSampleAttachmentKey_DisplayImmediately bypasses timestamp queuing for immediate display on both platforms
https://theapplewiki.com/wiki/Backboardd — iOS backboardd vs macOS WindowServer compositor architecture differences

https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/711033 — CAMetalLayer on iOS: maximumDrawableCount=2 gives ~16ms best case, maximumDrawableCount=3 gives variable 16-50ms; no AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer comparison
verdict.correction: The claim should be split and qualified: (1) The "one or more extra frames" figure for iOS is unsubstantiated — it derives from a subjective macOS observation on a discrete-GPU machine, not an iOS measurement. (2) The assertion that the macOS observation may not directly translate to iOS is architecturally reasonable (iOS lacks WindowServer; backboardd's compositor path for video layers differs), but no published measurement confirms the iOS path is free of the overhead either. The most defensible statement is: "AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on macOS has been reported to exhibit subjective extra-frame buffering latency compared to Metal on discrete-GPU hardware; whether this translates to iOS is unknown — no iOS-specific Metal vs AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer latency benchmark exists in the public record, and Moonlight-iOS uses only AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer with no Metal fallback."

rawCorpus.summary:
The input-to-photon path for a single-window macOS-to-macOS/iOS remote control app decomposes into six stages, each with its own latency floor. On a LAN with RTT ~1ms, the bottleneck quickly becomes the rendering pipeline, not the network, so the practical budget is dominated by display timing on both ends.

**Stage 1 — Client input capture (macOS host cursor: ~1–3 ms; iOS touch: ~8–17 ms).** On macOS a physical mouse generates a HID report at its polling interval (USB: 1–8 ms depending on bInterval; Bluetooth: ~7.5–15 ms). The HID driver timestamps the event and forwards it through WindowServer. An NSEvent callback measured in isolation costs ~0.034 ms at p0 to ~0.5 ms at p75, with rare spikes to 36 ms (developer benchmark from Apple Forums thread/111358). A passive CGEventTap adds the same overhead plus a kernel mach IPC round-trip; active taps must return quickly or be disabled (the system's tapDisabledByTimeout mechanism fires when callbacks are too slow, though the exact threshold is undocumented—commonly cited as ~1 second for user-mode active taps). On iOS/iPadOS at 120 Hz ProMotion, touch hardware scans at up to 240 Hz (4.17 ms resolution) but UIKit coalesces and delivers at display-frame boundaries (8.33 ms @ 120 Hz). Raw latency from touch to UIKit touchesBegan observed experimentally at 16–35 ms on older 60 Hz devices (5-frame budget per Apple Developer Forums thread/71301); modern 120 Hz ProMotion devices should be approximately half that (8–17 ms) but no published first-party number exists. Using coalescedTouches(for:) and predictedTouches(for:) can recover sub-frame precision and add ~1-frame of prediction headroom respectively (WWDC 2015 session 233).

**Stage 2 — Client network send (~0.05–0.2 ms on LAN wired).** A plain UDP send on macOS via Network.framework or BSD sockets on a wired Gigabit LAN completes in tens of microseconds (kernel syscall + NIC DMA). Wi-Fi adds 1–5 ms per hop. Cloudflare measured UDP round-trips at ~60 µs average on 10 GbE localhost; LAN wired is ~0.1–0.5 ms one-way. The right protocol design is: send button events immediately on generation, batch mouse-motion events at ~1 ms intervals (matching USB HID report rate). Coalescing motion beyond 1 ms adds perceptible lag; sending every delta immediately under Wi-Fi creates burst jitter. No motion prediction or extrapolation is worth doing at LAN RTT ~1 ms — the round-trip is shorter than one display frame.

**Stage 3 — Host-side CGEvent injection (~0.2–2 ms, with important security caveats).** CGEventPostToPid delivers events directly to a target PID without entering the global HID stream and without moving the system cursor. The call path goes: userspace → WindowServer Mach IPC → target app event queue. No published benchmark exists for this specific IPC hop; a Mach IPC message round-trip on Apple Silicon is on the order of 50–200 µs. The injected event must then be processed by the target app's run loop, which adds another ~0.1–0.5 ms depending on thread scheduling. Critical security change in macOS Tahoe 26: WindowServer's CGXSenderCanSynthesizeEvents() gate now rejects events from unsigned daemons for certain event categories (modifier+key combinations to Carbon hotkey listeners are silently dropped). For a properly signed app with kTCCServicePostEvent permission, CGEventPostToPid still works. For future-proofing and lowest latency, a DriverKit virtual HID device (as used by Karabiner-Elements) injects at the IOHIDSystem layer — the same path real hardware uses — bypassing WindowServer event synthesis restrictions entirely. The DriverKit path requires Apple entitlement com.apple.developer.driverkit.userclient-access.

**Stage 4 — Activate-then-control and the window raise problem.** Full SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions raises the window and can trigger a Space switch (100–300 ms). The correct technique, as documented by trycua/cua and yabai, is to call SLPSPostEventRecordTo twice (once to the previous frontmost process's PSN, once to the target's) — this flips the AppKit-active state for input routing without raising the window. After this, CGEventPostToPid routes correctly to the target. Total overhead for this activate-without-raise pattern is two Mach IPC messages (~0.1–0.3 ms), performed once per session or when focus is lost. Chromium-based apps require an additional workaround: inject a "decoy click" at coordinate (-1,-1) to satisfy the user-activation gate before real events. SLEventPostToPid (private SkyLight symbol) follows an auth-signed channel that bypasses the CGEvent tap chain entirely and handles Chromium's renderer-level filter.

**Stage 5 — Network return path: host display change travels back to client as video (~1–8 ms encoding + ~0.5 ms network + ~1–5 ms decoding).** This is covered by other research dimensions (encode/decode). The key point for input-to-photon: on a LAN the total host-to-client video latency is ~3–15 ms at 120 fps with HW encode/decode.

**Stage 6 — Client display pipeline (~8–17 ms at 120 Hz, ~2 ms if using local cursor overlay).** Metal present-to-pixels: CAMetalLayer with maximumDrawableCount=2 and displaySyncEnabled=true commits a frame ~5 ms before the next vsync and it appears on the next scan-out. At 120 Hz that is a worst-case ~8.33 ms display pipeline wait plus ~5 ms pre-vsync window = up to ~13 ms. AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds one or more additional buffered frames of latency vs. a direct Metal renderer path — subjectively significant in A/B tests (Moonlight issue #1885) — prefer Metal directly. For the cursor specifically, drawing the cursor in the video pipeline means the cursor position update must travel the full capture→encode→network→decode→display cycle (~15–25 ms on a good LAN), whereas a local overlay cursor drawn directly by the client app updates at input-event time (~0–1 ms after the input is generated), making the cursor appear effectively instant.

**Stage summary budget (wired LAN, 120 Hz displays, no local cursor):** Input capture 1–3 ms + network send 0.2 ms + injection 0.5–2 ms + target processing 0.5 ms + return video 3–15 ms + client render 8–13 ms = **~14–36 ms glass-to-glass** not counting cursor. With local cursor overlay: cursor visual feedback in ~2–5 ms (input capture + draw); window update confirmation in ~15–25 ms for the video frame showing the result. This matches Parsec's "4–8 ms on LAN at 240 fps" claim (which is for the pure encode-network-decode pipeline without counting display scan-out) and industry baselines of ~15–30 ms glass-to-glass on a well-tuned LAN system.

techniques.name: Local cursor overlay (draw cursor from input channel, not video)
techniques.how: On the client, intercept mouse/touch coordinates from the input stream and render a cursor overlay directly via Metal or SwiftUI — never encode the cursor into the video stream. On the host, use CGWarpMouseCursorPosition or ScreenCaptureKit SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false to hide the host cursor. The client draws the overlay cursor at the position it sent, updating at input rate (~8–16 ms intervals at 120 Hz), rather than waiting for the video roundtrip (~15–25 ms extra). Implement cursor image sharing: when the host cursor changes shape, send the new cursor image out-of-band over the control channel (not via video).
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates the full video roundtrip from cursor visual feedback (~15–25 ms saved on LAN). Cursor appears to move at ~0–2 ms after input event vs ~17–30 ms in video. This is the single highest-value latency reduction for perceived responsiveness. RDP, PCoIP, and NoMachine all implement this.

techniques.name: CGEventPostToPid for background window input injection
techniques.how: Use CGEventPostToPid(pid, event) to deliver mouse and keyboard events directly to the target process without entering the global HID stream. This prevents cursor movement and does not require the window to be foreground. Pair with SLPSPostEventRecordTo (private SkyLight API, accessed via dlopen/dlsym) to flip AppKit-active state without raising the window (avoiding the 100–300 ms Space-switch penalty). Code: dlsym SLPSPostEventRecordTo, call it with the target PSN and a fFrontProcessSendsForegroundEvents event record type, then CGEventPostToPid. Requires kTCCServicePostEvent + Accessibility permissions.
techniques.latency_impact: Avoids 100–300 ms window raise / Space switch. Injection itself adds ~0.2–2 ms (Mach IPC path). Critical vs alternative of full window activation.

techniques.name: DriverKit virtual HID device for trusted event injection
techniques.how: Implement an IOHIDDevice-compatible DriverKit extension that appears as a real HID keyboard/mouse to the system. Events injected through it follow the exact same path as physical hardware: IOHIDSystem → WindowServer → target app. Bypasses CGXSenderCanSynthesizeEvents() gate in WindowServer (critical on macOS Tahoe 26 where unsigned daemon CGEventPost is blocked for some event types). Reference implementation: pqrs-org/Karabiner-DriverKit-VirtualHIDDevice on GitHub. Client app communicates via UNIX domain socket to the daemon. Requires Apple entitlement com.apple.developer.driverkit.userclient-access (apply through developer portal).
techniques.latency_impact: Same injection latency as physical hardware path. Eliminates macOS Tahoe 26 CGEventPost blocking issue. Future-proof against further CGEventPost restrictions. Estimated latency: indistinguishable from physical keyboard/mouse.

techniques.name: Immediate button/key send, batched motion (1 ms cadence)
techniques.how: Design the input protocol to send button events (mouseDown, keyDown, scroll clicks) immediately on generation with zero queuing delay. Motion events (mouseMoved, mouseDragged) are batched at the USB HID polling cadence: collect all deltas received within a 1 ms window and send as a single packet. At USB 1000 Hz polling the host sees a mouse move every 1 ms; the client should match this. Do NOT batch button events with motion — button events must be sent as their own datagram with no artificial delay. For touch input on iOS at 120 Hz, the coalesced touch delivery rate is 8.33 ms; send one motion update per UIEvent delivery to match.
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates artificial queuing latency for buttons (potentially 1–8 ms saved vs naive fixed-timer batching). Motion already benefits from hardware polling rate alignment.

techniques.name: iOS coalescedTouches + predictedTouches for sub-frame touch delivery
techniques.how: In touchesMoved:withEvent:, call event.coalescedTouches(for: touch) to retrieve all 240 Hz touch samples since last delivery (at 120 Hz display, up to 2 samples per delivery). Send all coalesced touch positions, not just the last one, to give the host finer-grained motion data. Optionally call event.predictedTouches(for: touch) to get 1–2 predicted future positions and send those as well for smooth cursor extrapolation on the host side. This recovers the extra resolution that UIKit normally hides by coalescing.
techniques.latency_impact: Recovers ~1 frame (8.33 ms at 120 Hz) of touch position precision. With prediction, can visually eliminate ~1 additional frame of cursor lag. WWDC 2015 session 233 quantified: coalescing saves ~0.5 frames, prediction saves ~1 frame (at 60 Hz baseline: ~8.3 ms and ~16.7 ms respectively).

techniques.name: Metal direct rendering (not AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer) for minimum display latency
techniques.how: Use CAMetalLayer with maximumDrawableCount=2, displaySyncEnabled=true. Call CAMetalDisplayLink (preferred over CADisplayLink on macOS 14+) to schedule render callbacks aligned to vsync. In the callback, decode the video frame directly to a Metal texture, draw any cursor overlay, and present. Avoid AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer — it adds internal buffering that results in subjectively noticeable extra latency even when reported decode numbers look good. Use VTDecompressionSession with Metal texture output (kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey) to zero-copy from VideoToolbox into the CAMetalLayer drawable.
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates 1–2 frames of AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer buffering (potentially 8–17 ms at 120 Hz). Empirically observed as 'significant' subjective improvement by Moonlight users in direct A/B comparison (issue #1885).

techniques.name: Passive CGEventTap for macOS client input capture (not addLocalMonitorForEvents)
techniques.how: Install a passive CGEventTap at kCGHIDEventTap with kCGEventTapOptionListenOnly and kCGHeadInsertEventTap placement. This intercepts events at the HID level before WindowServer routing — the earliest point in the userspace pipeline. A passive tap cannot modify events and therefore cannot be blocked by the tapDisabledByTimeout mechanism. Use a dedicated high-priority thread (QoS userInteractive) for the tap's run loop. Compare to NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents which fires later (post-WindowServer routing) and NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents which only works for in-process events.
techniques.latency_impact: ~0.1–0.3 ms advantage over post-routing monitors. Ensures hardware timestamps (NSEvent.timestamp) are captured as early as possible. Critical for accurate timing of input send.

techniques.name: macOS Tahoe 26 signed-app CGEventPost path (avoid unsigned daemon pattern)
techniques.how: Distribute the input injection component as a properly code-signed app (not a bare daemon) with com.apple.security.automation.apple-events and kTCCServicePostEvent entitlements. On macOS Tahoe 26, CGEventPost from unsigned/ad-hoc signed daemons is dropped by WindowServer's CGXSenderCanSynthesizeEvents() gate for modifier+key combinations, causing 2–3 second input lag (observed in input-leap issue #2367). Building against the macOS 15 SDK temporarily works around this but is not a viable long-term strategy. The proper fix is code signing or the DriverKit HID device approach.
techniques.latency_impact: Avoids 2–3 second input lag regression on macOS Tahoe 26. Critical correctness fix rather than optimization.

techniques.name: No motion prediction/extrapolation on LAN
techniques.how: Do not implement client-side cursor extrapolation or Kalman prediction for motion on LAN. At LAN RTT of ~0.5–2 ms, the round-trip is shorter than one display frame (8.33 ms at 120 Hz), so prediction adds complexity and potential divergence artifacts without meaningful latency benefit. Reserve prediction only for WAN scenarios with RTT > 20 ms. The local cursor overlay technique (see above) already eliminates the visual cursor lag without requiring prediction — the cursor renders from the sent position, not the received frame.
techniques.latency_impact: Avoids prediction artifacts. Saves implementation complexity. Prediction would save ~0 ms on LAN (RTT < 1 display frame).

techniques.name: CAMetalDisplayLink with 3 drawables and presentAfterMinimumDuration for 120 Hz frame pacing
techniques.how: On macOS, use CAMetalDisplayLink (introduced macOS 14) instead of CADisplayLink or CVDisplayLink. Apple DTS recommends 3 drawables on macOS (not 2 as on iOS) to avoid drawable starvation. Set presentAfterMinimumDuration to ~4 ms to allow the system to pace frames without introducing extra buffering. Call present(drawable) before commit() on the command buffer (not wait_until_scheduled) to avoid blocking the render thread. On iOS/iPadOS, 2 drawables is correct and CAMetalDisplayLink naturally paces to ProMotion rates.
techniques.latency_impact: Correct frame pacing at 120 Hz. Without this, frames oscillate between 8ms and 16ms (documented in Zed blog post). Worst-case display wait reduces from ~16.7 ms to ~8.33 ms at 120 Hz.

key_settings: SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false (hide host cursor, use client-side overlay instead)
CGEventPostToPid(pid, event) — not CGEventPost — to target window without cursor warp
SLPSPostEventRecordTo via dlopen/dlsym — activate-without-raise for input routing to background window
CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount = 2 (iOS) / 3 (macOS)
CAMetalLayer.displaySyncEnabled = true
CAMetalDisplayLink (macOS 14+) — preferred over CADisplayLink/CVDisplayLink
VTDecompressionSession output: kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey = true (zero-copy to Metal)
Avoid AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer for low-latency rendering — use Metal directly
UIEvent.coalescedTouches(for:) — retrieve all 240 Hz touch samples per delivery
UIEvent.predictedTouches(for:) — 1–2 predicted future touch positions
CGEventTap placement: kCGHIDEventTap, kCGHeadInsertEventTap, kCGEventTapOptionListenOnly
kTCCServicePostEvent entitlement — required for CGEventPostToPid on macOS 14+
com.apple.developer.driverkit.userclient-access — for DriverKit virtual HID device (alternative injection path)
processType: Interactive in launchd plist — for Karabiner-style HID daemon high-priority scheduling
latency_numbers: NSEvent nextEventMatchingMask polling cost: p0=0.034 ms, p50=0.134 ms, p75=0.5 ms, p95=0.72 ms, p100=36.6 ms — Apple Developer Forums thread/111358
USB HID mouse polling interval: 1 ms at 1000 Hz, 8 ms at 125 Hz (standard) — USB HID spec; macOS Apple Silicon may cap at 500 Hz per tinyusb issue #1705
iOS touch-to-UIKit callback: ~5 frames observed empirically on 60 Hz iPhone 6 = ~83 ms total; Apple claims 2-frame max (32.2 ms) at 60 Hz — Apple Developer Forums thread/71301
WWDC 2015 touch pipeline improvement: iOS 9 reduced from 4 frames to ~1.5 frames (at 60 Hz = ~50 ms to ~25 ms) using coalescing + prediction combined
ProMotion 120 Hz frame period: 8.33 ms; coalesced touch delivery rate at 120 Hz: 8.33 ms per UIEvent
Adaptive-Sync display pipeline: frame can be displayed 1 ms after rendering completes (vs waiting up to 8.33 ms on fixed 120 Hz) — WWDC 2021 session 10147
Metal render-to-present deadline: ~5 ms before vsync for frame to appear on next scan-out — Apple Forums thread/711033
GameBench iPhone XS Max game touch latency (touch-to-screen-update): 82–94 ms (includes display lag, app logic) — GameBench 2019
Parsec total pipeline on LAN: 4–8 ms (encode+network+decode only, excludes display scan-out) at 240 fps — Parsec blog
AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer vs Metal renderer: subjectively 'one or more extra frames' of buffering — Moonlight issue #1885 (no precise ms figure)
CVDisplayLink fires ~7 ms before next vsync on 60 Hz single-display setup — thume.ca 2017 measurement
macOS Tahoe 26 unsigned-daemon CGEventPost lag: 2–3 seconds input lag — input-leap issue #2367
LAN UDP round-trip: ~60 µs average minimum on 10 GbE — Cloudflare blog; ~0.1–0.5 ms one-way typical on Gigabit wired LAN
Touch-to-display latency range across 26 smartphones (2022-2023 study): 35–140 ms total — PMC study
claims_to_verify: CGEventPostToPid Mach IPC round-trip latency on Apple Silicon M-series: stated ~50–200 µs based on general Mach IPC overhead — no direct benchmark found for this specific call
SLPSPostEventRecordTo still works on macOS Tahoe 26 for activate-without-raise — private API, may have been restricted; paneru issue #123 shows SIGABRT crash on macOS 14.2.1 in this function
iOS 120 Hz ProMotion touch-to-UIKit callback latency: estimated ~8–17 ms by halving the 60 Hz figure — no direct measurement found for modern 120 Hz devices
SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false API existence and behavior on macOS 14+ — needs verification in SCStreamConfiguration header
AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds exactly 'one or more frames' of display latency vs Metal renderer — only subjective reports found, no instrumented measurement
CGEventTap tapDisabledByTimeout threshold: cited as ~1 second in community but undocumented in Apple headers — actual threshold should be verified

macOS Tahoe 26 fully blocks unsigned-daemon CGEventPost for all event types vs only modifier+key to Carbon listeners — the input-leap issue shows lag, the nick-liu post shows only hotkey synthesis is blocked; behavior for plain mouse/keyboard CGEventPost unclear
Mouse poll rate hard cap at 500 Hz on Apple Silicon MacBook: reported in tinyusb issue #1705 but unverified for all Apple Silicon Mac models

CAMetalDisplayLink targeting 120 Hz with displaySyncEnabled=true + 3 drawables on macOS reliably achieves 8.33 ms frame pipeline — Apple Forums thread/763426 reports struggles; recommendation is from Apple DTS but outcomes vary with external monitor configuration
open_questions: What is the exact latency of CGEventPostToPid on Apple Silicon M-series when the target app is backgrounded? A microbenchmark using mach_absolute_time before/after the call plus measuring NSEvent.timestamp delta at the target would settle this.
Does SLEventPostToPid (private SkyLight, not CGEvent) have meaningfully lower latency than CGEventPostToPid, or is the difference only about bypassing Chromium's renderer filter?
On macOS Tahoe 26, does plain mouse motion CGEventPostToPid still work without restrictions for non-hotkey events from a signed-but-not-MAS app with PostEvent TCC permission?

For the iOS client, is there any API to get touch events at the full 240 Hz hardware sampling rate (not coalesced to 120 Hz frame boundaries) without jailbreaking? The developer forum thread/718550 from 2022 received no replies.
What is the end-to-end latency contribution of a DriverKit virtual HID device compared to CGEventPostToPid? Is there measurable scheduling latency in the driver→IOHIDSystem→WindowServer→app path?

Does NSCursor or a custom Metal overlay have lower display latency for cursor rendering on macOS? NSCursor updates through WindowServer compositing; a Metal overlay committed to the same CAMetalLayer as the video could be presented atomically.

verifications.claim: CGEventPostToPid Mach IPC round-trip latency on Apple Silicon M-series: stated ~50–200 µs based on general Mach IPC overhead — no direct benchmark found for this specific call

verdict.claim: CGEventPostToPid Mach IPC round-trip latency on Apple Silicon M-series: stated ~50–200 µs based on general Mach IPC overhead — no direct benchmark found for this specific call
verdict.evidence:
Three problems with the claim:

1. CATEGORY ERROR — CGEventPostToPid is not a direct Mach IPC call. The macOS SDK header (CGEvent.h, confirmed in the current Xcode SDK at MacOSX.sdk) documents it as: "Post an event into the event stream for a specific application. This function posts the specified event immediately before any event taps instantiated for the specified process, and the event passes through any such taps." The delivery path is: caller → CoreGraphics → WindowServer's annotated per-process event tap pipeline → target process. WindowServer itself uses Mach IPC (MIG-generated stubs) internally, but CGEventPostToPid is not a two-party mach_msg round-trip — it is a multi-hop delivery through the WindowServer event routing layer. Research on macOS internals (Keen Security Lab, trycua blog on macOS window internals) confirms that events go through WindowServer's CGXSenderCanSynthesizeEvents filter and HID/SkyLight pipelines.

2. THE 50–200 µs NUMBER IS UNANCHORED. No published benchmark for CGEventPostToPid specifically was found in any authoritative source — not Apple docs, WWDC, SDK headers, academic papers, or open-source repos. The best available macOS IPC benchmark data (github.com/steven-joruk/macos-ipc-benchmarks, run on a 2017 MacBook Pro Intel) shows ~11–12 µs round-trip for small XPC/UDS payloads. Raw mach_msg on modern Apple Silicon is expected to be faster (sub-10 µs, consistent with the Linux IPC baseline of "10s of microseconds" for a comparable monolithic kernel; seL4 on ARM64 achieves <1 µs showing the theoretical floor). The 50–200 µs estimate is plausible for the full CGEventPostToPid path (which includes a WindowServer round-trip, not just mach_msg), but there is no source to confirm this specific range.

3. THE FUNCTION SPELLING IS CORRECT. Confirmed in the SDK: CG_EXTERN void CGEventPostToPid(pid_t pid, CGEventRef event) API_AVAILABLE(macos(10.11)). Swift name: CGEvent.postToPid(_:). Not deprecated as of the current macOS SDK.

Bottom line: The latency estimate may be in the right ballpark for the full WindowServer-mediated delivery (which involves at least two Mach IPC hops: caller→WindowServer and WindowServer→target), but calling it "Mach IPC round-trip latency" is technically wrong — it is a CoreGraphics/WindowServer event-injection latency, not a bare mach_msg round-trip. No direct benchmark exists for this specific path on Apple Silicon.
sources: MacOSX.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreGraphics.framework/Headers/CGEvent.h (local Xcode SDK header, confirmed via grep)
https://github.com/steven-joruk/macos-ipc-benchmarks (macOS IPC benchmark: ~11 µs for small XPC/UDS round-trip on 2017 MBP Intel)
https://github.com/trycua/cua/blob/main/blog/inside-macos-window-internals.md (CGEventPostToPid routes through WindowServer CGXSenderCanSynthesizeEvents, not direct to target)
https://www.nick-liu.com/posts/tahoe-hotkey-dead-end/ (WindowServer event dispatch path: events route through WindowServer before per-app dispatch)
verdict.correction: The claim misidentifies CGEventPostToPid as a "Mach IPC round-trip." It is a CoreGraphics/WindowServer event-injection API that routes through at least two Mach IPC hops (caller→WindowServer, WindowServer→target process event tap), plus WindowServer-side processing. No direct benchmark exists for this path on Apple Silicon. The 50–200 µs range is unsubstantiated for this specific API; the closest public data (Intel, XPC/UDS small-message round-trip) shows ~11 µs, suggesting the full CGEventPostToPid path (which has more hops and WindowServer overhead) is likely higher — but the exact figure for Apple Silicon M-series remains unmeasured in any published source. A corrected statement: "CGEventPostToPid is a CoreGraphics/WindowServer event-injection API, not a direct Mach IPC call; its end-to-end latency on Apple Silicon M-series is unknown (no published benchmark found) but is expected to exceed bare mach_msg overhead due to multi-hop WindowServer routing."

verifications.claim: SLPSPostEventRecordTo still works on macOS Tahoe 26 for activate-without-raise — private API, may have been restricted; paneru issue #123 shows SIGABRT crash on macOS 14.2.1 in this function

verdict.claim: SLPSPostEventRecordTo still works on macOS Tahoe 26 for activate-without-raise — private API, may have been restricted; paneru issue #123 shows SIGABRT crash on macOS 14.2.1 in this function
verdict.evidence:
The claim contains one confirmed sub-fact and one materially wrong sub-fact, plus an unverifiable assertion about macOS 26.

CONFIRMED: [paneru issue #123](https://github.com/karinushka/paneru/issues/123) is real and documents a SIGABRT crash loop on macOS 14.2.1 Sonoma. The fix is in [paneru src/manager/windows.rs lines 283–302](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/karinushka/paneru@c38f172/-/blob/src/manager/windows.rs%3FL283-302), where `make_key_window()` is entirely skipped when `macos_major_version() == 14`.

WRONG (root cause): The crash is NOT in `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` itself being "restricted." The inline code comment (lines 284–286) states the actual cause: "On macOS 14 (Sonoma), `CGSEncodeEventRecord` serializes the raw event buffer via `NSKeyedArchiver`, misinterpreting 0xFF fill as an ObjC class pointer, causing SIGABRT." The bug is in how the 0xf8-byte record is populated — specifically `event_bytes[0x20..0x30].fill(0xff)` — which triggers an abort inside the SkyLight serialization layer. `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` is still callable; the issue is a buffer-contents invariant violation introduced with macOS 14's changed CGSEncodeEventRecord behavior.

UNVERIFIABLE for macOS Tahoe 26: No Apple developer documentation, SDK headers, WWDC sessions, or any indexed public sources (as of June 2026) confirm or deny whether `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` continues to function on macOS 26 Tahoe. The paneru version guard only covers `== 14` and does not hint at breakage on 15 or 26. Projects that use this API (alt-tab-macos, AutoRaise) list Sequoia 15 support, but no Tahoe-specific test report exists in public sources. The API lives in the private SkyLight.framework and its stability across major macOS versions is inherently unguaranteed.

EXACT SPELLING CONFIRMED: The symbol is `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` — confirmed by Sourcegraph keyword search across paneru, alt-tab-macos (src/macos/api-wrappers/SkyLight.framework.swift line 200–201), and AutoRaise (AutoRaise.mm line 68). The spelling in the claim is correct.
verdict.correction: The paneru issue #123 SIGABRT on macOS 14.2.1 is real, but the crash is NOT caused by `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` being restricted or blocked. The crash originates in `CGSEncodeEventRecord` (called inside SkyLight after `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` is invoked) which, on macOS 14, serializes the raw event buffer via NSKeyedArchiver and aborts when it encounters the 0xFF fill at bytes [0x20..0x30] — misinterpreted as an invalid ObjC class pointer. The function `SLPSPostEventRecordTo` itself remains callable; the fix in paneru PR #124 is to skip the `make_key_window()` helper (which constructs the malformed buffer) entirely on macOS 14, not to avoid calling `SLPSPostEventRecordTo`. Whether it works on macOS Tahoe 26 is unknown — no authoritative source confirms or denies Tahoe compatibility.

verifications.claim: iOS 120 Hz ProMotion touch-to-UIKit callback latency: estimated ~8–17 ms by halving the 60 Hz figure — no direct measurement found for modern 120 Hz devices

verdict.claim: iOS 120 Hz ProMotion touch-to-UIKit callback latency: estimated ~8–17 ms by halving the 60 Hz figure — no direct measurement found for modern 120 Hz devices
verdict.evidence:
No authoritative source — Apple docs, WWDC sessions, Apple Developer Forums, or peer-reviewed papers — publishes a direct measurement of the touch-to-UIKit callback latency for ProMotion (120 Hz display) iPhone or iPad devices. The original claim's "halving the 60 Hz figure" methodology is unsound for three independent reasons:

1. TOUCH SAMPLING ≠ DISPLAY REFRESH. The 60 Hz "figure" that could be halved is ambiguous. At 60 Hz the WWDC 2015 session 233 (Advanced Touch Input on iOS) characterised the pipeline as "approximately 1.5 frames" (~25 ms) after iOS 9 optimisations, down from 4 frames (~67 ms). That figure is a display-latency budget, not a pure digitiser-to-UIKit-callback number. Even non-ProMotion iPhones from iPhone X onwards use 120 Hz touch *sampling* regardless of the 60 Hz display refresh rate — meaning the digitiser scan interval is already 8.3 ms on those devices. ProMotion on iPhone 13 Pro+ pairs a 120 Hz *display* with a 360 Hz touch *sampling* rate (2.8 ms scan interval), so a simple "halve 60 Hz" rule conflates at least two independent clock domains.

2. END-TO-END LATENCY IS NOT HALVED BY DOUBLING REFRESH RATE. The DXOMARK high-speed camera benchmark (1,200 fps, precision robot) measured touch-to-display response time for the iPhone 15 Pro Max (ProMotion, 120 Hz, 360 Hz touch sampling) at a mean of ~67 ms — essentially identical to its 60 Hz predecessor the iPhone 14 (non-Pro). GameBench's earlier measurements on iPhone XS Max (60 Hz display, 120 Hz touch sampling) showed a mean of 88 ms across several games. A PMC/NIH 2023 study covering 8 iOS devices (iPhone 7 through iPhone 13, though none were confirmed ProMotion Pro models) found a mean capacitive touch registration latency of 14.25 ms with a total touch-to-display range of 35–140 ms across all 26 smartphones tested. None of these datasets isolate the UIKit-callback sub-component at 120 Hz.

3. NO DIRECT MEASUREMENT EXISTS FOR THE CLAIMED RANGE. The 8–17 ms estimate in the original claim lacks any cited empirical basis. The touch digitiser scan interval at 360 Hz is ~2.8 ms; at 120 Hz it is ~8.3 ms. The UIKit callback is delivered at the next run-loop iteration after a display refresh boundary, meaning at 120 Hz (8.3 ms frame budget) the worst-case wait from a freshly registered touch to the touchesBegan callback is ~1 display frame = ~8.3 ms, but real delivery passes through: digitiser → kernel → backboardd (IPC) → UIKit run-loop, adding pipeline overhead that is not quantified in public Apple documentation. Apple's own WWDC 2015 claim of "2 frames maximum" at 60 Hz (≈33 ms) and "1.5 frames" typical (≈25 ms) does not have an official 120 Hz equivalent published anywhere. The 8–17 ms range could be plausible as a theoretical lower bound for the digitiser-to-kernel segment alone, but calling it "touch-to-UIKit callback latency" overstates precision.

In summary: the claim that no direct measurement exists for 120 Hz ProMotion devices is CORRECT. The claim that 8–17 ms is obtained by "halving the 60 Hz figure" is methodologically flawed because (a) 60 Hz iPhones already use 120 Hz touch sampling so the relevant 60 Hz figure is unclear, (b) DXOMARK end-to-end measurements show ProMotion does not halve total latency relative to 60 Hz devices, and (c) the UIKit callback pipeline has uncharacterised IPC overhead that is not captured by frame-interval arithmetic alone.
verdict.correction: The claim should be restated as: "iOS 120 Hz ProMotion touch-to-UIKit callback latency: no direct measurement published for any ProMotion device. The 8–17 ms estimate obtained by halving the 60 Hz figure is methodologically unsound. Non-ProMotion iPhones already use 120 Hz touch sampling (8.3 ms scan interval), so the relevant 60 Hz baseline is unclear. DXOMARK end-to-end measurements (~67 ms on iPhone 15 Pro Max) show doubling display refresh rate does not halve total touch-to-photon latency. The digitiser scan interval alone at 360 Hz (iPhone 13 Pro+) is ~2.8 ms, but the full digitiser→kernel→backboardd→UIKit pipeline adds unquantified IPC overhead. Apple's WWDC 2015 '1.5 frames typical at 60 Hz' (~25 ms) has no published 120 Hz equivalent. A best-case UIKit callback at 120 Hz is bounded by ~1 display frame (~8.3 ms) plus pipeline overhead; a realistic range likely sits in the low tens of milliseconds, but no authoritative direct measurement exists."

rawCorpus.summary:
Apple's XNU kernel exposes three overlapping real-time scheduling mechanisms. The oldest and lowest-level is the Mach time-constraint policy (`THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY`), set via `thread_policy_set()`. It takes four fields in `thread_time_constraint_policy_data_t`: `period` (interval between work cycles in Mach absolute time units), `computation` (CPU time needed within the period), `constraint` (maximum wall-clock span from start to end of the computation — must be ≥ `computation`), and `preemptible` (whether the computation cycles may be interrupted). All three time values are in Mach absolute time ticks, which differ per CPU: on M-series Apple Silicon the numerator/denominator from `mach_timebase_info()` is 125/3 (one tick = 41.67 ns), so conversions from nanoseconds are essential. On Intel both are 1, making ticks equal to nanoseconds. A straightforward formula for a 60 fps video thread: `period = 16_666_666 ns × denom/numer`, `computation = period/2`, `constraint = period × 0.85`. Setting this policy directly elevates the thread into the kernel's real-time band, bypassing QoS decay. The Chromium browser engine's reference implementation uses a 2.9 ms time quantum with a 75% guaranteed / 85% maximum duty cycle as a conservative fallback when no explicit period is supplied.

The second mechanism is the POSIX `SCHED_RR` policy at raw pthread priority 45–48, set via `pthread_attr_setschedpolicy`/`pthread_attr_setschedparam`. This prevents priority decay (a QoS-only thread can be penalized by the kernel when it continuously burns CPU), but it is incompatible with QoS — setting it permanently opts the thread out of QoS bookkeeping. SCHED_RR priority 48 with `pthread_cond_wait`/`pthread_cond_broadcast` has been shown to work in real media loops on macOS (no specific sub-millisecond jitter numbers confirmed), while QoS `.userInteractive` (approximately priority 47 in raw terms) is subject to decay and is not recommended as the sole RT mechanism. Using `yield()` must be completely avoided: on Apple platforms it tanks thread priority to zero and can stall the thread for up to 10 ms.

The third and most important mechanism for Apple Silicon is the **os_workgroup** API (`os/os_workgroup.h`, macOS 11+). Workgroups communicate deadlines to the kernel's "performance controller," which governs CPU frequency scaling and P-core vs E-core allocation. A critical discovery from developer forum research: joining an audio I/O workgroup (obtained via `kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup`) requires the joining thread to already have `THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY` set — attempting to join without it returns `EINVAL` with the message "thread is not realtime." For custom (non-CoreAudio) pipelines, `os_workgroup_interval_create` + `os_workgroup_interval_start` / `os_workgroup_interval_finish` creates an independent interval workgroup with per-cycle deadlines using `mach_absolute_time()`. There is no dedicated video-capture or VideoToolbox workgroup equivalent in public API; the audio workgroup APIs are the only OS-level workgroup surface currently available. A private `_os_workgroup_get_current` / `_dispatch_thread_getspecific(os_workgroup_key)` API is used by some third-party audio middleware as a workaround, but it is undocumented and risky.

On Apple Silicon, the asymmetric core topology creates a crucial scheduling hazard: threads that sleep via `usleep()` or `pthread_cond_timedwait()` for more than a few hundred microseconds can be migrated to E-cores by the scheduler. Thread affinity APIs (`THREAD_AFFINITY_POLICY`) are not supported on ARM — `thread_policy_set` returns `KERN_NOT_SUPPORTED` (46) for affinity on Apple Silicon. The practical mitigations are: (1) keep waits short (< a few hundred µs) or use `mach_wait_until()` with a real-time thread, (2) join the relevant os_workgroup so the performance controller knows when the next deadline is.
