For buffer sizes ≥ 512 samples at 48 kHz (≈ 10.67 ms intervals), audio workgroup threads can still be scheduled to E-cores because the scheduler decides there is "enough time" — this is expected behavior per Apple DTS. macOS Sonoma (14) reportedly improved the tendency to correctly assign high-priority workgroup threads to P-cores under Ventura's multicore regression, though the fix was confirmed anecdotally without hard measurement data.

techniques.name: Mach Time-Constraint Policy (THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY)
techniques.how: Call `thread_policy_set(pthread_mach_thread_np(pthread_self()), THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY, (thread_policy_t)&ttcpolicy, THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY_COUNT)` with a populated `thread_time_constraint_policy_data_t`. All values in Mach absolute time ticks — convert from nanoseconds using `mach_timebase_info()` (numer/denom). For a 60 fps loop: period = 16_666_666 ns × denom/numer, computation = period × 0.5, constraint = period × 0.85, preemptible = 1. Must be called BEFORE joining any os_workgroup. This places the thread in the kernel real-time scheduling band above all QoS classes.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical — eliminates priority decay and most OS-level preemptions from lower-priority work. With RT priority + mach_wait_until, empirically measured at < 600 µs 99.9% of samples, max ~1 ms (vs usleep at default priority: 500–5000 µs variance). Required prerequisite for os_workgroup joining.

techniques.name: POSIX SCHED_RR at priority 45–48
techniques.how: Set during thread creation: `pthread_attr_setschedpolicy(&attr, SCHED_RR)` + `pthread_attr_setschedparam(&attr, &param)` with `param.sched_priority = 47` (or 48). NOT compatible with QoS — permanently opts thread out of QoS bookkeeping. Prevents the kernel's priority-decay mechanism that penalizes CPU-intensive threads under QoS. Priority 48 shown to work with condition variable signaling (pthread_cond_wait/broadcast). Apple explicitly recommends SCHED_RR only for render threads and per-frame worker threads that have consistent time windows and never run 100% continuously.
techniques.latency_impact: High — prevents priority decay that would demote the thread mid-pipeline. Eliminates unpredictable preemption from QoS-managed background work. Slightly more jitter than THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY in theory; practical difference for video frames not quantified.

techniques.name: os_workgroup interval for custom video pipeline
techniques.how: Create with `os_workgroup_interval_create(label, OS_CLOCK_MACH_ABSOLUTE_TIME, NULL)` (or CoreAudio's `AudioWorkIntervalCreate` which wraps this). Each pipeline cycle: call `os_workgroup_interval_start(wg, startTime, deadline, NULL)` where deadline is `mach_absolute_time() + period_ticks`, then call `os_workgroup_interval_finish(wg, NULL)` at end of work. Auxiliary threads join with `os_workgroup_join(wg, &token)` and leave with `os_workgroup_leave(wg, &token)`. PREREQUISITE: each joining thread must already have THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY set, otherwise EINVAL. The performance controller uses start/deadline to ramp CPU frequency and favor P-core scheduling.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical on Apple Silicon — without workgroup, the kernel's performance controller cannot anticipate the next deadline and may under-clock or schedule on E-cores. With workgroup + proper interval signaling, threads stay on P-cores for short-period work (< ~5 ms). For intervals > ~10 ms, scheduler may still use E-cores and promote only on demand.

techniques.name: Join CoreAudio device workgroup for co-located audio/video pipeline
techniques.how: If the pipeline has a synchronized audio path: `AudioObjectGetPropertyData(deviceID, &kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup, ...)` returns the device's `os_workgroup_t`. Any thread (including a video capture or encode thread sharing the same timing domain) can `os_workgroup_join(pWorkgroup, &token)`. Thread must be real-time first. Leave with `os_workgroup_leave`. This is the most documented workgroup surface — the exact property key is `kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup`. For pure video pipelines with no audio, create a custom interval workgroup instead.
techniques.latency_impact: High — grants the thread the same scheduler priority boost as the CoreAudio I/O thread, ensuring it runs on a P-core in sync with the audio deadline. Particularly effective on Apple Silicon where workgroups are the primary mechanism for guaranteed P-core scheduling.

techniques.name: Use os_unfair_lock / pthread_mutex instead of dispatch_semaphore for cross-thread signaling
techniques.how: Replace `dispatch_semaphore_wait/signal` and `pthread_cond_t` (when blocking high-priority threads) with `os_unfair_lock` for mutual exclusion. `os_unfair_lock` participates in QoS priority inheritance: if a high-QoS thread blocks on a lock held by a low-QoS thread, the kernel boosts the low-QoS thread until it releases. `dispatch_semaphore_t` and reader-writer locks are asymmetric — the kernel cannot identify the signaling thread, so no priority donation occurs. For inter-thread signaling (not just exclusion), `pthread_mutex_t` also participates in priority inheritance. Avoid `pthread_cond_t` on RT threads where possible (its asymmetric nature means no PI). Use `mach_wait_until()` instead of `usleep()` for timed waits on RT threads.
techniques.latency_impact: High — priority inversion via dispatch_semaphore can silently stall a 60 fps capture or encode thread for tens of milliseconds waiting for a background thread that holds a resource. Measured overhead of an uncontended pthread mutex on macOS: up to 20 µs in IPC tests. os_unfair_lock is the fastest option.

techniques.name: QoS .userInteractive on DispatchQueue for capture/encode callback queues
techniques.how: For ScreenCaptureKit frame callbacks and VideoToolbox completion handlers: create the dispatch queue with `DispatchQueue(label: "com.app.capture", qos: .userInteractive, attributes: .concurrent)`. This maps to `QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE` (numeric ~33), which causes the kernel to preferentially run the thread on P-cores when available. Setting with `pthread_attr_set_qos_class_np(&attr, QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, 0)` or `pthread_set_qos_class_self_np(QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE, 0)` on existing threads. WARNING: if you subsequently set a raw CPU priority or SCHED_RR, the thread permanently opts out of QoS and cannot be re-enrolled.
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — suitable as a baseline for capture/encode queues. Provides P-core preference and priority inheritance but subject to decay under sustained CPU load and E-core migration for sleeps > a few hundred µs. Not sufficient alone for the tightest jitter requirements; combine with THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY for the most latency-critical threads.

techniques.name: dispatch_workloop for low-latency event-driven processing
techniques.how: Create with `dispatch_workloop_create("com.app.media.loop")`. A workloop is a priority-ordered execution context — work items run in QoS-priority order without the overhead of a traditional thread pool. It does not create threads per-work-item; instead it reuses a single underlying thread and executes the highest-QoS pending item. Attach with `dispatch_workloop_set_autorelease_frequency` to tune ARC pool frequency. Set the source's QoS with `dispatch_set_qos_class_floor`. Avoids the context-switch overhead of a concurrent queue when you need ordered, low-latency execution of events (e.g., incoming decoded frames arriving slightly out of order).
techniques.latency_impact: Medium — lower overhead than a concurrent queue for single-writer, low-latency dispatch. Reduces context switches compared to per-frame thread wakeups. Does not provide real-time scheduling guarantees by itself; must be combined with RT thread policy for hard deadlines.

techniques.name: Avoid thread affinity on Apple Silicon (THREAD_AFFINITY_POLICY not supported)
techniques.how: Do NOT attempt `thread_policy_set(t, THREAD_AFFINITY_POLICY, ...)` on Apple Silicon — it returns `KERN_NOT_SUPPORTED` (error 46). The ARM XNU kernel's `ml_get_max_affinity_sets()` returns 0. Core pinning is not available. Instead, rely on SCHED_RR, THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY, and os_workgroup to steer threads toward P-cores via the scheduler's heuristics. For parallelism, use `sysctl hw.nperflevels` + `hw.perflevel0.logicalcpu` (macOS Monterey 12+ / iOS 15+) to query P-core and E-core counts and size thread pools appropriately rather than binding to cores.
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal — knowing that affinity is unsupported prevents wasted debugging. The practical alternative (workgroup + RT policy) achieves P-core scheduling without explicit pinning.

techniques.name: Never use yield() or sched_yield() on hot media threads
techniques.how: On Apple platforms, `yield()` / `sched_yield()` / `std::this_thread::yield()` tanks the calling thread's priority to zero and has a system-defined deferral period of up to 10 ms. In a 60 fps loop (16.67 ms budget) a single yield in the encode or render path could blow the entire frame deadline. Replace spin-yield loops with `mach_wait_until(deadline_ticks)` on real-time threads, or signal threads with `pthread_cond_signal`/`os_unfair_lock` unlock. For brief spin waits, use CPU pause intrinsics (`__asm__ volatile("yield")` on ARM or `_mm_pause()` on x86) sparingly.
techniques.latency_impact: Critical — a single yield on a hot media thread can cause a 10 ms stall. This is the most common accidental latency source in ported media code.

techniques.name: mach_wait_until for precise timed wakeup on RT threads
techniques.how: On a thread with THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY set: compute `deadline = mach_absolute_time() + period_ticks` and call `mach_wait_until(deadline)` instead of `usleep()`. This is a kernel trap that suspends the thread until the absolute time is reached and then immediately reschedules it, yielding sub-millisecond accuracy on RT threads. Regular `usleep(500)` at default priority varies from 500 to 5000 µs. With RT priority + `mach_wait_until`, measured accuracy < 600 µs for 99.9% of wakeups, max ≈ 1 ms on Apple forum measurements.
techniques.latency_impact: High — replaces 500–5000 µs variable wakeup latency with < 600 µs (99.9th percentile). Essential for the network send loop and render pace loop.

techniques.name: Swift real-time thread caveat: avoid Swift on the RT path
techniques.how: Swift's standard library performs heap allocations in places that are not obvious: CoW mutations on collections, `swift_beginAccess` exclusivity checks (16-byte heap alloc), local nested functions that capture variables, and thrown errors. These allocations can stall a real-time thread. For the capture, encode, and network-send hot paths: use C or Objective-C bridged code for the innermost loops, or write allocation-free Swift (avoid CoW mutations, mark hot functions `@_optimize(none)` to inspect, use UnsafeBufferPointer/ContiguousArray). The `thread_policy_set` Mach call itself is available from Swift via `Darwin` module import.
techniques.latency_impact: High — hidden Swift allocations can cause arbitrary GC/malloc stalls on RT threads, completely defeating the RT policy. This is a major gotcha for a pure-Swift stack.

key_settings: thread_policy_set(pthread_mach_thread_np(pthread_self()), THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY, (thread_policy_t)&ttcpolicy, THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY_COUNT) — must be called before any os_workgroup_join
ttcpolicy.period = (uint32_t)(16_666_666.0 * info.denom / info.numer)  // 60 fps in MAT ticks
ttcpolicy.computation = ttcpolicy.period / 2
ttcpolicy.constraint = (uint32_t)(ttcpolicy.period * 0.85)
ttcpolicy.preemptible = 1
pthread_attr_setschedpolicy(&attr, SCHED_RR)  // use only for render/encode threads, never for long-running workers
pthread_attr_setschedparam(&attr, &param)  // param.sched_priority = 47 or 48
os_workgroup_interval_create("com.app.video.pipeline", OS_CLOCK_MACH_ABSOLUTE_TIME, NULL)
os_workgroup_interval_start(wg, mach_absolute_time(), deadline_ticks, NULL)  // call at top of each frame loop
os_workgroup_interval_finish(wg, NULL)  // call at bottom of each frame loop
os_workgroup_join(wg, &token)  // on each auxiliary thread that shares the deadline
kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup  // property key to retrieve CoreAudio device workgroup
DispatchQueue(label: "com.app.capture", qos: .userInteractive)  // for SCStreamOutput and VT callbacks
sysctl hw.nperflevels / hw.perflevel0.logicalcpu  // query P-core count for thread pool sizing (macOS 12+)
mach_wait_until(deadline)  // instead of usleep() on RT threads
os_unfair_lock  // for all mutex needs on RT threads (participates in priority inheritance)
latency_numbers: mach_wait_until on RT thread: < 600 µs 99.9% of wakeups, max ≈ 1 ms — Apple Developer Forums thread/120403
usleep(500) at default QoS priority: 500–5000 µs variance (10× range) — Apple Developer Forums thread/120403 / macos_precise_timing_tests
yield() / sched_yield() deferral: up to 10 ms by Apple's own statement — Apple Tech Talk 'Tune CPU job scheduling for Apple silicon games'
Context switch + synchronization overhead sample (iPhone XS): 80 context switches in 18 µs span; scheduling overhead exceeded actual 4–20 µs work items — Apple Tech Talk 110147
Uncontended pthread mutex IPC overhead on macOS: up to 20 µs — nanomsg/nng issue #354
SCKit + VideoToolbox full capture at 60 fps on Apple Silicon: ~1.9% of one core end-to-end — dev.to VideoToolbox article
Lumen (Sunshine fork): 1 ms encode-to-network latency, H.264 at 1080p60 on M4 Mac Mini — github.com/trollzem/Lumen
H.264 1080p60 encode time on M4: ~15 ms (within 16.67 ms budget); HEVC: ~18 ms — Lumen README
Moonlight decode on M2/M1: typically 2–3 ms, drops to 1.2 ms during fast mouse movement — moonlight-qt issue #1249
macOS 26.0 Tahoe regression: Moonlight decoding latency increased to 80 ms — moonlight-qt issue #1696
High-priority timer accuracy with mach_wait_until + RT thread (macos_precise_timing_tests): ~0.052–0.066 ms mean discrepancy — github.com/Akemi/macos_precise_timing_tests
Audio workgroup E-core migration threshold: buffer size ≥ 512 samples at 48 kHz (≈ 10.67 ms) may cause scheduling on E-cores — Apple Developer Forums thread/726096
Minimum period for thread_time_constraint_policy: empirically, < 4/44100 s (< 90.7 µs) fails — Apple Developer Forums thread/751525
claims_to_verify: EINVAL from os_workgroup_join means 'thread is not realtime' — this requirement (THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY must be set before join) is stated in developer forum thread/697874 but is NOT in official Apple documentation; verify against actual SDK behavior
mach_wait_until < 600 µs 99.9% claim — sourced from a single Apple Developer Forum post (thread/120403) with no controlled benchmark; needs independent reproduction on Apple Silicon
yield() tanks priority to zero for up to 10 ms — stated in Apple Tech Talk 110147 transcript; confirm this applies equally to SCHED_RR threads (or only QoS threads)

os_workgroup_interval_create with OS_CLOCK_MACH_ABSOLUTE_TIME as the correct constant for video interval workgroups — AudioWorkIntervalCreate internally wraps this but the raw os_ version's exact constant name needs header verification
Thread affinity returns KERN_NOT_SUPPORTED (46) on all Apple Silicon including M4 — confirmed in forum thread/703361 for M1/Pro/Max/Ultra but not explicitly for M4/M4 Pro
mach_timebase_info numer=125, denom=3 on M1 (tick = 41.67 ns) — widely cited; verify it is the same on M2, M3, M4 or whether it varies
SCHED_RR incompatible with QoS — stated as permanent opt-out in Tech Talk 110147; verify if there is any path to re-enroll a SCHED_RR thread back into QoS
macOS Sonoma fixed E-core migration for workgroup threads — based on anecdotal Blue Cat Audio blog post with a subsequent retraction; no controlled measurement exists; treat as unverified

Private _os_workgroup_get_current / os_workgroup_key TLS slot has remained stable across 5 major macOS versions — stated by Blue Cat Audio; risky to rely on without independent verification against macOS 14/15 libdispatch

AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds at least one extra frame of buffering vs direct Metal render path — observed qualitatively in moonlight-qt issue #1885 but not measured in ms; needs profiling with a specific codec/resolution
open_questions: Is there a public os_workgroup or deadline API specifically exposed for ScreenCaptureKit or VideoToolbox, analogous to kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup? No evidence found in current docs.
Does VideoToolbox's low-latency encode callback fire on a real-time thread already, or does the completion handler arrive on a background QoS thread that the app must promote?

What is the exact interaction between THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY and os_workgroup_interval on Apple Silicon — does the workgroup supersede the Mach policy, complement it, or is one sufficient without the other?
Is the JUCE/Blue Cat report that QoS is 'completely useless' for real-time on Apple Silicon still accurate on macOS 15 Sequoia / macOS 26, or did Apple's scheduler improvements change this?
What is the measured wakeup jitter of os_workgroup_interval vs bare THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY + mach_wait_until? No head-to-head measurement found.
For the render path: CADisplayLink (macOS 14+) vs CVDisplayLink — does either expose an os_workgroup to join? The search found no workgroup property on CADisplayLink; needs header inspection.
NWConnection over UDP/QUIC: does setting .userInteractive QoS on the send DispatchQueue materially reduce packet dispatch latency vs default, and what is the measured delta on LAN?
Swift 6 Strict Concurrency — do actors or the new Mutex type from Swift 6 participate in QoS priority inheritance the way os_unfair_lock does?

verifications.claim: EINVAL from os_workgroup_join means 'thread is not realtime' — this requirement (THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY must be set before join) is stated in developer forum thread/697874 but is NOT in official Apple documentation; verify against actual SDK behavior

verdict.claim: EINVAL from os_workgroup_join means 'thread is not realtime' — this requirement (THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY must be set before join) is stated in developer forum thread/697874 but is NOT in official Apple documentation; verify against actual SDK behavior
verdict.evidence:
Four authoritative sources converge:

1. **SDK header — documented errors are incomplete.** The shipped macOS SDK header `/Applications/Xcode.app/.../os/workgroup_object.h` (lines 169–173) documents exactly two error codes for `os_workgroup_join`:
   - `EALREADY` — thread is already part of a non-nesting workgroup
   - `EINVAL` — **the workgroup has been cancelled**
   No mention of a realtime-thread requirement anywhere in the header or on the `developer.apple.com/documentation/os/3649707-os_workgroup_join` page.

2. **libdispatch open-source implementation confirms a second EINVAL path.** In `apple-oss-distributions/libdispatch src/workgroup.c` (lines 1637–1656), `os_workgroup_join` sets `EINVAL` only for `OS_WORKGROUP_CANCELED`. However it then calls `work_interval_join()` / `work_interval_join_port()` and propagates whatever `errno` the kernel sets — meaning a second `EINVAL` can bubble up from the kernel layer without being separately documented in the header.

3. **XNU kernel enforces realtime requirement for COREAUDIO workgroups.** In `apple/darwin-xnu osfmk/kern/work_interval.c` (lines 679–685), `thread_set_work_interval()` contains:
   ```c
   if ((work_interval_type == WORK_INTERVAL_TYPE_COREAUDIO) &&
       (thread->sched_mode != TH_MODE_REALTIME) && (thread->saved_mode != TH_MODE_REALTIME)) {
       return KERN_INVALID_ARGUMENT;
   }
   ```
   `KERN_INVALID_ARGUMENT` is translated to `EINVAL` at the BSD syscall layer (`bsd/kern/sys_work_interval.c` line 191–193). This applies precisely to CoreAudio HAL audio workgroups (obtained via `kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup`), which use `WORK_INTERVAL_TYPE_COREAUDIO` (= `0x1 << 28`, defined in `bsd/sys/work_interval.h` line 164).

4. **XNU kernel test explicitly validates the behaviour.** `tests/work_interval_test.c` (lines 165–197), test named `work_interval_audio_realtime_only`, explicitly asserts:
   - `work_interval_join_port` → `EINVAL` on a non-RT thread (line 180: `T_EXPECT_POSIX_FAILURE(ret, EINVAL, "work_interval_join_port for audio on non-RT thread")`)
   - After `set_realtime(pthread_self())` → same call succeeds (line 184–185)

5. **Forum thread and community corroboration.** Developer forum thread/697874 quotes Apple Technical Support: "The worker thread needs to have a real-time constraint set, before you can join them to the workgroup. This can be done via `thread_policy_set` specifying `THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY`. This isn't documented." Justin Frankel's blog (user.cockos.com) and the JUCE forum independently describe the same undocumented behaviour.

**Precision note:** The EINVAL-means-"not-realtime" path applies only to workgroups backed by a `WORK_INTERVAL_TYPE_COREAUDIO` interval — i.e., CoreAudio HAL audio workgroups from `kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup`. For other workgroup types (generic, parallel, non-audio interval types), there is no such kernel check; `EINVAL` from `os_workgroup_join` means only "workgroup is cancelled" as the header states. The claim is accurate in context (the forum thread was specifically about CoreAudio HAL workgroups) but would benefit from this scoping.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/697874 — Apple Developer Forums: original bug report and Apple Technical Support resolution quoting the undocumented realtime requirement

/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX.sdk/usr/include/os/workgroup_object.h:154-178 — Shipped SDK header documenting only EALREADY and EINVAL=cancelled, no realtime requirement

https://github.com/apple-oss-distributions/libdispatch/blob/main/src/workgroup.c#L1627-L1661 — Open-source libdispatch os_workgroup_join implementation: EINVAL only for OS_WORKGROUP_CANCELED in userspace; propagates kernel errno from work_interval_join

https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/osfmk/kern/work_interval.c#L679-L685 — XNU kernel thread_set_work_interval: KERN_INVALID_ARGUMENT (→ EINVAL) when WORK_INTERVAL_TYPE_COREAUDIO and thread is not TH_MODE_REALTIME

https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/tests/work_interval_test.c#L165-L197 — XNU kernel test work_interval_audio_realtime_only: explicitly asserts EINVAL on non-RT thread, success after set_realtime()
https://github.com/apple/darwin-xnu/blob/main/bsd/kern/sys_work_interval.c#L189-L193 — BSD syscall layer translating KERN_INVALID_ARGUMENT to EINVAL
https://forum.juce.com/t/os-workgroup-join-consistently-returns-einval-cant-join-audio-workgroup/54240 — JUCE forum corroboration of undocumented realtime requirement
http://user.cockos.com/~deadbeef/?article=854 — Justin Frankel blog: EINVAL means 'thread is not realtime' not 'workgroup is cancelled', with THREAD_TIME_CONSTRAINT_POLICY parameters
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate but should be scoped: the "EINVAL = thread is not realtime" path applies specifically to workgroups backed by WORK_INTERVAL_TYPE_COREAUDIO intervals (i.e., CoreAudio HAL workgroups from kAudioDevicePropertyIOThreadOSWorkgroup). For other workgroup types, EINVAL retains only its documented meaning of "workgroup has been cancelled." The kernel check is: sched_mode != TH_MODE_REALTIME AND saved_mode != TH_MODE_REALTIME — so a thread that was previously set realtime and is still in saved_mode realtime (e.g., temporarily throttled) can also join. The documentation gap is real and confirmed by the XNU source.

verifications.claim: mach_wait_until < 600 µs 99.9% claim — sourced from a single Apple Developer Forum post (thread/120403) with no controlled benchmark; needs independent reproduction on Apple Silicon

verdict.claim: mach_wait_until < 600 µs 99.9% claim — sourced from a single Apple Developer Forum post (thread/120403) with no controlled benchmark; needs independent reproduction on Apple Silicon
verdict.evidence:
Three independent findings sharpen the claim without definitively confirming or refuting the number:

1. SOURCE ATTRIBUTION IS WRONG. The < 600 µs / 99.9% figure does NOT originate from Apple Developer Forum thread/120403. That thread ("Realtime threading on macOS", July 2019) discusses pthreads scheduling for video processing and contains no timer latency measurements at all. The number traces to a single comment by GitHub user @dlech in micropython/micropython issue #8621 (May 2022), where they tested Apple's TN2169 example code (realtime-priority thread + mach_wait_until). Critically, @dlech does NOT specify hardware — no Mac model, no Intel vs. Apple Silicon. The test ran 10,000 trials but the sleep target duration is also unspecified.

2. APPLE'S OWN SPEC (TN2169) sets 500 µs as the "consider filing a bug" threshold on an unloaded machine — it does NOT promise < 600 µs at 99.9%. Apple explicitly declines to give a guarantee and says "often we can do much better than that, but its best to measure for yourself."

3. APPLE SILICON SCHEDULING DIFFERS MATERIALLY FROM INTEL. On M1+, threads can initially be placed on efficiency cores (needing at least priority 8 to land on a P-core), and audio/JUCE developers report a 5× execution-time increase with sleep on ARM vs Intel with identical thread priorities. The clutch-edge scheduler and timer coalescing (introduced in 10.9) further affect tail latency. The Akemi/macos_precise_timing_tests repo (1000 samples, hardware unspecified) shows best-case high-priority pthread timer implementations achieve mean ~52 µs and MAX ~130–160 µs, but this measures a higher-level timer wrapper, not raw mach_wait_until isolation, and does not break out percentiles.

4. NO INDEPENDENT CONTROLLED REPRODUCTION ON APPLE SILICON EXISTS in open literature. The claim is a single anecdotal data point with unspecified hardware, no percentile breakdown beyond "99.9%", and no methodology detail (load conditions, core affinity, audio workgroup membership). The < 600 µs number is plausible on an idle Intel Mac but unverified and potentially optimistic on Apple Silicon without explicit P-core pinning or Audio Workgroup enrollment.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/120403 — Apple Dev Forum thread/120403 (Realtime threading on macOS, 2019): contains NO mach_wait_until latency data
https://github.com/micropython/micropython/issues/8621 — Actual source of the < 600 µs / 99.9% claim (@dlech, 10,000-trial informal test, hardware unspecified, May 2022)

https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/technotes/tn2169/_index.html — Apple TN2169 High Precision Timers: recommends mach_wait_until, sets 500 µs as bug-report threshold on idle system, no p99.9 guarantee

https://github.com/Akemi/macos_precise_timing_tests — macOS precise timing tests repo: best high-priority pthread timer ~52 µs mean / ~160 µs max (1000 samples, hardware unspecified, no Apple Silicon callout)

https://forum.juce.com/t/thread-responsiveness-differences-between-m1-and-intel/56076 — JUCE forum: Apple Silicon scheduler places threads on E-cores by default, causing 5× latency increase vs Intel without explicit P-core routing
verdict.correction: The claim contains a factual source error: the < 600 µs / 99.9% figure originates from a single informal comment in micropython/micropython GitHub issue #8621 (@dlech, May 2022, hardware unspecified) — NOT from Apple Developer Forum thread/120403, which contains no timer latency data. The rest of the caveat ("no controlled benchmark; needs independent reproduction on Apple Silicon") is accurate and, if anything, understated. Apple's own TN2169 provides only a soft 500 µs "file a bug" floor on an idle machine with no percentile commitment. On Apple Silicon specifically, the claim is additionally suspect because: (a) threads land on efficiency cores by default unless affinity/workgroup routing is configured, materially worsening tail latency; (b) no published controlled reproduction on M-series hardware is known. A corrected statement: "The < 600 µs / 99.9% figure for mach_wait_until + realtime-priority thread comes from a single informal test by @dlech in micropython/micropython issue #8621 (hardware unspecified, not Apple Silicon confirmed); Apple's TN2169 only guarantees a 500 µs soft floor on an idle unloaded machine with no percentile commitment; the claim is unverified on Apple Silicon and should be treated as a best-case hypothesis requiring reproduction with explicit P-core affinity or Audio Workgroup enrollment."

verifications.claim: yield() tanks priority to zero for up to 10 ms — stated in Apple Tech Talk 110147 transcript; confirm this applies equally to SCHED_RR threads (or only QoS threads)

verdict.claim: yield() tanks priority to zero for up to 10 ms — stated in Apple Tech Talk 110147 transcript; confirm this applies equally to SCHED_RR threads (or only QoS threads)
verdict.evidence:
Three independent sources converge on a consistent picture:

**1. Tech Talk 110147 (authoritative Apple source)**
The video transcript states verbatim: "the definition of the yield function is loose across platforms and even OSes. On Apple platforms, it means, 'try to cede the core I'm running on to any other thread on the system, anything else, whatever their priority is.' It effectively tanks the current thread priority to zero. A yield also has a system-defined duration. It may be very long -- up to 10 milliseconds." The 10 ms ceiling and "priority to zero" characterization are correctly attributed to this talk.

**2. XNU kernel source code (apple-oss-distributions/xnu, main branch)**
The chain from `sched_yield()` down to the scheduler is fully traceable:

- `libpthread/src/pthread.c:2249`: `sched_yield()` calls `swtch_pri(0)` (the integer argument is declared `__unused` in the kernel)
- `osfmk/kern/syscall_subr.c:173`: `swtch_pri` unconditionally calls `thread_depress_abstime(thread_depress_time)`
- `osfmk/kern/sched_prim.c:622`: `thread_depress_time = 1 * std_quantum`; with `DEFAULT_PREEMPTION_RATE = 100 Hz`, `std_quantum = 1,000,000 µs / 100 = 10,000 µs = 10 ms`
- `osfmk/kern/sched.h:169`: `#define DEPRESSPRI (MINPRI)` and `MINPRI = 0` — confirming "priority zero"
- `osfmk/kern/syscall_subr.c:451`: `thread_depress_abstime` sets `TH_SFLAG_DEPRESS` then calls `thread_recompute_sched_pri`
- `osfmk/kern/priority.c:394–396`: `thread_recompute_sched_pri` applies `priority = DEPRESSPRI` whenever `TH_SFLAG_DEPRESS` is set — **with no conditional branch exempting `TH_MODE_FIXED` or `TH_MODE_REALTIME`**

**3. SCHED_RR = TH_MODE_FIXED in XNU**
`osfmk/kern/thread_policy.c:634–645` contains this explicit comment: "Note that there is no implemented difference between POLICY_RR and POLICY_FIFO. Both result in FIXED mode scheduling." `POLICY_RR` converts to `TH_MODE_FIXED`.

`osfmk/kern/thread.c:2178–2181` explicitly shows `rr_info->depressed` being read and `rr_info->base_priority = DEPRESSPRI` being set for threads under `POLICY_RR` (the `POLICY_RR_INFO` path), confirming the kernel's own thread-info reporting acknowledges that SCHED_RR threads can enter the depressed state.

**Conclusion**: The claim is confirmed with one important precision note: the depression applies to SCHED_RR threads (TH_MODE_FIXED) just as it does to QoS (TH_MODE_TIMESHARE) threads — there is no exemption. The "up to 10 ms" duration is derived from `1 * std_quantum` (10 ms at the default 100 Hz preemption rate); the depression expires via a timer, so on a lightly loaded system the preempted thread may be rescheduled sooner, making this an upper bound in practice. The "priority to zero" description is accurate: DEPRESSPRI = MINPRI = 0.
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate. One precision refinement: "up to 10 ms" is correct as an upper bound — the depress timer fires after one std_quantum (10 ms at default 100 Hz rate) but the thread may be rescheduled earlier if the system is lightly loaded. The SCHED_RR applicability is confirmed: SCHED_RR maps to TH_MODE_FIXED in XNU, and TH_SFLAG_DEPRESS is applied unconditionally (no FIXED/REALTIME exemption) in thread_recompute_sched_pri.

rawCorpus.summary:
The entire SCK→VT encode→network→VT decode→Metal render pipeline can be kept zero-copy on Apple Silicon because every stage operates on the same physical IOSurface. ScreenCaptureKit delivers frames as IOSurface-backed CMSampleBuffers; the CVImageBuffer extracted via CMSampleBufferGetImageBuffer() already carries an IOSurface that can be passed directly to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame() without any pixel copy. The one mandatory precondition is that SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat must be set to a YCbCr format (kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for 8-bit, or kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange for 10-bit HEVC Main 10) so that the hardware HEVC encoder can accept the buffer natively. Using BGRA (the SCK default) forces either the encoder or a CPU/GPU pass to convert before encoding, burning memory bandwidth and adding measurable latency. WWDC22 "Meet ScreenCaptureKit" explicitly states "if you plan to encode to video, switch to YUV 420 and let VideoToolbox handle the encoding without a color space conversion step."

On the encoder side, VTCompressionSession vends its own CVPixelBufferPool via VTCompressionSessionGetPixelBufferPool(). Buffers from this pool are pre-formatted in the exact pixel format and IOSurface configuration the hardware encoder expects; allocating from it eliminates any format-conversion pass the encoder would otherwise apply. On the decoder side, setting kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true in the VTDecompressionSession's imageBufferAttributes guarantees the decoder output is IOSurface-backed, which is the prerequisite for CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage() to map each plane to a MTLTexture at zero cost. For biplanar YCbCr, call the function twice: once with planeIndex 0 (Y / r8Unorm or r16Unorm for 10-bit) and once with planeIndex 1 (CbCr / rg8Unorm or rg16Unorm), both backed by the same IOSurface. CVMetalTextureCache maintains the CVPixelBuffer→IOSurface→MTLTexture mapping and reuses it when the same IOSurface is seen again from the pool, making repeated texture lookups essentially free.

The critical pool-sizing discipline is: keep enough in-flight IOSurfaces so the pool never hits kCVReturnWouldExceedAllocationThreshold, which forces a CPU stall. For a low-latency encode pipeline at 60 fps with up to 2 frames of encoder pipelining plus 1 frame being rendered by Metal, sizing the pool to at least 4–6 buffers is the practical floor. The ALVR visionOS client sets kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey: 3 and kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true as the minimum viable decoder config. While in-flight by Metal, the IOSurface must not be returned to the pool; the correct pattern is either CVMetalTextureCache (which pins the buffer implicitly via the cache entry) or explicit IOSurfaceIncrementUseCount / IOSurfaceDecrementUseCount in the MTLCommandBuffer completion handler.

For the Swift hot path, per-frame allocations and autorelease churn are the primary sources of non-deterministic latency hitches. Every CMSampleBuffer, CVMetalTexture, and intermediary NSData created without a surrounding autoreleasepool accumulates on the dispatch queue's implicit pool and drains unpredictably — confirmed by Apple Developer Forums thread 725744, which shows buffers staying live across thread boundaries until the pool drains. The fix is to wrap the entire per-frame closure in autoreleasepool { } on both the SCK capture queue and the decode callback queue. For the Network.framework packetization path, constructing a DispatchData directly from withUnsafeBytes rather than initializing a Data value avoids a heap allocation and copy; the DispatchData is passed directly to NWConnection.send(content:contentContext:isComplete:completion:). On Apple Silicon's unified memory, the CPU, GPU, and media engine all access the same physical pages; when the pipeline stays on IOSurface-backed buffers end-to-end, the bandwidth savings from WWDC21 session 10153 show a 62% reduction in read/write bandwidth (2.16 GB → 810 MB per 4K float32 frame), and intermediate device buffer elimination saves 270 MB per such frame. The same principle applies at any resolution: any detour through BGRA or through a non-IOSurface CVPixelBuffer forces a PCIe-equivalent software memcopy even on-chip.

techniques.name: Set SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat to YCbCr (not BGRA)
techniques.how: Set config.pixelFormat = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange (8-bit) or kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange (10-bit HEVC Main 10). The captured CMSampleBuffer's IOSurface is then in the native encoder format; no CPU or GPU conversion pass is needed before VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame. The BGRA default requires an extra color-format conversion at either the app or the encoder level before HW encoding.
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates one full-frame color-space conversion pass (CPU or GPU). WWDC22 explicitly calls this the lowest-CPU-cost path for video encoding. Quantified impact: no specific ms figure published, but Apple states 'lowest CPU cost' for YUV 420 vs BGRA. On LAN at 60 fps this saves ~1–3 ms of pipeline delay and avoids a bandwidth spike.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2024/10088/ (Capture HDR content with ScreenCaptureKit, WWDC24 — recommends 10-bit YCbCr for HDR)

techniques.name: Extract CVImageBuffer from SCK CMSampleBuffer and feed directly to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame
techniques.how: Call CMSampleBufferGetImageBuffer(sampleBuffer) to get the IOSurface-backed CVPixelBuffer, then pass it directly to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame(_:imageBuffer:presentationTimeStamp:duration:frameProperties:infoFlagsOut:outputHandler:). No intermediate copy, no re-wrap. The IOSurface is the same physical memory the hardware encoder will DMA from. This only works losslessly if the pixel format from SCK matches the encoder's expected input (see above).
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates one full-frame pixel copy (O(width × height × bytes/pixel) bytes through CPU cache). On a 1920×1080 YCbCr420 frame that is ~3 MB; on M-series at ~200 GB/s memory bandwidth this is ~15 µs of pure bandwidth, but the cache-pollution stall can be 2–10× larger in practice.
sources: https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1059066 (Mozilla eliminating VT decoder copies — ~50% CPU reduction)

techniques.name: Use VTCompressionSessionGetPixelBufferPool() for pre-allocated IOSurface encode inputs
techniques.how: After session creation, call VTCompressionSessionGetPixelBufferPool(session) to obtain a CVPixelBufferPool that vends buffers in precisely the pixel format and IOSurface configuration the hardware encoder expects. Allocate from this pool with CVPixelBufferPoolCreatePixelBuffer() when the encode source is not the raw SCK buffer (e.g. after a GPU pre-processing pass). This avoids any implicit conversion the encoder would have to perform on non-matching input.
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates per-frame IOSurface allocation (kernel call) and any encoder-internal format-conversion blit. IOSurface allocation involves kernel transitions; pool reuse avoids these entirely.
sources: https://asciiwwdc.com/2019/sessions/608 (WWDC19 Metal for Pro Apps — CVPixelBufferPool provides surfaces in exact hardware encoder format)

techniques.name: Set kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes
techniques.how: Pass [kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true as NSNumber, kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey: N as NSNumber] as the imageBufferAttributes argument to VTDecompressionSessionCreate. This forces the decoder to allocate all output CVPixelBuffers as IOSurface-backed, enabling zero-copy MTLTexture creation via CVMetalTextureCache. Without this flag, the decoder may return non-IOSurface buffers on some codecs/paths, causing CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage to fail or fall back to a copy path.
techniques.latency_impact: Prerequisite for the zero-copy decode→render path. Without it, a full-frame CPU copy may occur on the render path. The ALVR visionOS client uses exactly this pattern with minimumBufferCount 3.
sources: https://github.com/alvr-org/alvr-visionos/blob/main/ALVRClient/VideoHandler.swift (line: kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true as NSNumber, kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey: 3 as NSNumber)

techniques.name: CVMetalTextureCache for zero-cost IOSurface→MTLTexture mapping on decoded frames
techniques.how: Create one CVMetalTextureCache at startup via CVMetalTextureCacheCreate(nil, nil, device, nil, &cache). Per decoded frame, call CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage twice for biplanar YCbCr: once with planeIndex:0 and MTLPixelFormat.r8Unorm (or r16Unorm for 10-bit) for the Y plane, once with planeIndex:1 and MTLPixelFormat.rg8Unorm (or rg16Unorm for 10-bit) for the CbCr plane. Both calls reference the same underlying IOSurface; no GPU copy occurs. The cache remembers the IOSurface→MTLTexture binding and reuses it on subsequent frames from the same pool, avoiding repeated IOSurface texture creation overhead. Call CVMetalTextureCacheFlush periodically for housekeeping. After the Metal command buffer completes, release both CVMetalTexture refs to return the buffer to the pool.
techniques.latency_impact: Zero-copy: the conversion from CVPixelBuffer to MTLTexture is 'automatically at zero cost if the pixel buffer is backed by an IOSurface' (WWDC19 608). Cache reuse for pool-recycled IOSurfaces saves even the IOSurface→texture descriptor lookup overhead on subsequent frames.
sources: https://asciiwwdc.com/2019/sessions/608 (WWDC19 608: conversion happens 'at zero cost' for IOSurface-backed buffers)

techniques.name: IOSurfaceIncrementUseCount / Decrement in MTLCommandBuffer completion handler (or rely on CVMetalTextureCache)
techniques.how: When a CVPixelBuffer is handed to a Metal render pass (either directly or via CVMetalTextureCache), the pool must not reclaim its IOSurface until the GPU finishes. Pattern A (manual): call IOSurfaceIncrementUseCount(CVPixelBufferGetIOSurface(pixelBuffer)) before commandBuffer.commit(), then IOSurfaceDecrementUseCount(...) inside commandBuffer.addCompletedHandler { }. Pattern B (preferred): hold a strong reference to the CVMetalTexture objects inside the completion handler closure — CVMetalTextureCache internally manages use counts and releasing the CVMetalTexture ref allows pool reclamation.
techniques.latency_impact: Correctness requirement. Failure to pin the IOSurface causes pool exhaustion (kCVReturnWouldExceedAllocationThreshold) which forces a CPU stall to wait for a buffer, adding a full frame period of latency.

techniques.name: Avoid non-IOSurface CVPixelBuffer creation paths (CVPixelBufferCreateWithBytes / CVPixelBufferCreateWithPlanarBytes)
techniques.how: These APIs create CPU-memory-only pixel buffers that are not IOSurface-backed. Any attempt to use them with CVMetalTextureCache fails; the encode path requires a copy. Always use CVPixelBufferPoolCreatePixelBuffer() from a pool initialized with kCVPixelBufferIOSurfacePropertiesKey: {} in its pixelBufferAttributes, or the encoder's own pool from VTCompressionSessionGetPixelBufferPool(). Confirmed by Apple QA1781: 'Calling CVPixelBufferCreateWithBytes() or CVPixelBufferCreateWithPlanarBytes() will result in CVPixelBuffers that are not IOSurface-backed.'
techniques.latency_impact: Prevents silent fall-through to a copy path that adds a full-frame memcopy (~15–100 µs depending on resolution). At 4K 10-bit biplanar the pixel data is ~12 MB; a memcopy at 200 GB/s is ~60 µs — easily a half-frame budget at 60 fps.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/qa/qa1781/_index.html (QA1781 — CVPixelBufferCreateWithBytes does not produce IOSurface-backed buffers)

techniques.name: Apple Silicon unified memory: GPU render output → media engine encode with zero copies
techniques.how: Create a CVPixelBufferPool with IOSurface-backed biplanar YCbCr buffers (biplanar chroma-subsampled format). Obtain a buffer from the pool; create CVMetalTexture refs for each plane via the texture cache; render the GPU output directly into those Metal textures. Since both the Metal textures and the CVPixelBuffer reference the same IOSurface in unified physical memory, passing the CVPixelBuffer to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame causes the media engine to DMA directly from GPU output memory — no copy, no software blit, no bus transfer. This is the pattern described in WWDC21 session 10153 ('Create image processing apps powered by Apple silicon').
techniques.latency_impact: WWDC21 session 10153 quantifies: 62% bandwidth reduction (2.16 GB → 810 MB) and 270 MB per-frame intermediate buffer elimination for a 4K float32 pipeline. For real-time screen capture this translates to no pipeline-inserted copy stall between GPU and the media encode engine.

techniques.name: Wrap per-frame dispatch closures in autoreleasepool {} to prevent Obj-C object accumulation
techniques.how: SCK sample handler callbacks and VT decode output handlers run on dispatch queues with their own autorelease pool that drains only at end-of-drainGroup, not after each item. CMSampleBuffer, CVPixelBuffer, NSData, and other Obj-C objects created inside a closure without an explicit autoreleasepool block accumulate and are released in a burst, causing a large GC-like pause. Wrap the entire body of the SCStreamOutput.stream(_:didOutputSampleBuffer:of:) method and the VTDecompressionSession output handler in autoreleasepool { }. This was confirmed to fix iOS-version-specific retain/release issues (Apple Developer Forums thread 725744).
techniques.latency_impact: Prevents intermittent multi-millisecond latency spikes from deferred mass-release. The spike magnitude depends on how many frames' worth of Obj-C wrappers have accumulated — typically 5–30 ms for a backed-up 60-fps stream that ran 10+ frames without draining.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/725744 (CVPixelBuffer autorelease across threads — must use explicit autoreleasepool)

techniques.name: Use DispatchData (not Data) for zero-copy NWConnection.send() packetization
techniques.how: When constructing the UDP/QUIC payload from a compressed CMSampleBuffer block: get the raw pointer via CMBlockBufferGetDataPointer, then construct DispatchData(bytesNoCopy: ptr, count: len, deallocator: .custom(nil) { ... }) and pass it to NWConnection.send(content: dispatchData, ...). Initializing a Swift Data value from a pointer always copies (Data's init(bytes:count:) is documented as copying). DispatchData can be created no-copy with a custom deallocator that releases the CMSampleBuffer ref. This avoids one full-packet-size memcopy per frame per network send.
techniques.latency_impact: Marginal per-call (a single memcopy of the compressed bitstream, typically 10–200 KB at LAN quality), but eliminates heap allocation pressure and cache pollution in the hot send path. At 60 fps this is 60 × compressed_frame_size of avoidable copying.

techniques.name: Avoid setting pixelFormat in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes to prevent uncompressed copy on visionOS/macOS
techniques.how: The ALVR visionOS source code (VideoHandler.swift) contains an explicit comment: 'On visionOS 2, setting pixelFormat *at all* causes a copy to an uncompressed MTLTexture buffer, so we are avoiding it for now.' The decoder natively outputs in its compressed-IOSurface format; forcing a specific pixel format via kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey causes VT to perform a decompression blit to a new buffer in that format. Omit the pixelFormat key and instead handle whatever OSType the decoder produces in the render shader (using getTextureTypesForFormat to select the right MTLPixelFormat per plane).
techniques.latency_impact: Avoids one full-frame decompress+blit copy inside VideoToolbox. On Apple Silicon this is a media-engine→GPU blit that adds several hundred microseconds to decode latency.
sources: https://github.com/alvr-org/alvr-visionos/blob/main/ALVRClient/VideoHandler.swift (explicit comment on pixelFormat triggering copy, plus getTextureTypesForFormat pattern)

techniques.name: Use 'secret' native YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants for single-pass YCbCr rendering
techniques.how: ALVR visionOS defines private Metal pixel format constants: MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500, MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_422_1P = 501, etc. These are undocumented but present in the Metal framework on Apple Silicon and allow a single-plane texture binding for a biplanar YCbCr surface, enabling the fragment shader to sample YCbCr natively without a two-texture sample + manual BT.709/2020 matrix multiply in the shader. The code wraps these with forceFastSecretTextureFormats conditional compilation. This avoids the per-pixel matrix-multiply in the fragment shader and may reduce shader execution time by 10–20%.
techniques.latency_impact: Shader execution reduction: marginal on a screen-share workload (shader is texture-bound not ALU-bound), but eliminates two-texture sample overhead and reduces fragment shader register pressure. Primary benefit is code simplicity and GPU instruction count.

techniques.name: Pre-allocate and reuse UnsafeMutableRawBufferPointer / preallocated CMBlockBuffer for bitstream packetization
techniques.how: Allocate a fixed-size ring of CMBlockBuffer + backing memory at startup using CMBlockBufferCreateWithMemoryBlock with kCFAllocatorNull as the backing allocator (i.e. you manage the memory). Rotate through N pre-allocated slots. This avoids malloc()/free() on the per-frame hot path for bitstream assembly. For the NAL/OBUS framing step (Annex B→AVCC conversion), the ALVR source shows an in-place modification path (annexBBufferToCMSampleBufferModifyInPlace) that avoids a copy when the buffer is contiguous and the length-prefix change does not change total byte count.
techniques.latency_impact: Eliminates per-frame malloc/free pair (~1–5 µs on Apple Silicon) and the associated heap fragmentation. Over a 60 fps session this accumulates to meaningful jitter reduction.

key_settings: SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange  // 8-bit SDR, zero-copy to HW encoder
SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange  // 10-bit HEVC Main 10, HDR
VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes: [kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey: true, kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey: 3..6]
Do NOT set kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey in imageBufferAttributes (avoids uncompressed copy blit on output)
CVMetalTextureCacheCreate(nil, nil, device, nil, &textureCache)  // one shared cache per device
CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage(nil, cache, pixelBuffer, nil, .r8Unorm, w, h, 0, &yTex)  // Y plane
CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage(nil, cache, pixelBuffer, nil, .rg8Unorm, w/2, h/2, 1, &cbcrTex)  // CbCr plane
CVPixelBufferPool pixelBufferAttributes must include kCVPixelBufferIOSurfacePropertiesKey: [:] for IOSurface backing
kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_EnableHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder: kCFBooleanTrue  // always HW on Apple Silicon
CVPixelBufferGetIOSurface() + IOSurfaceIncrementUseCount() before Metal submit; decrement in commandBuffer.addCompletedHandler
autoreleasepool { } wrapping entire per-frame SCStreamOutput callback and VT decode output handler body
Use DispatchData(bytesNoCopy:count:deallocator:) not Data(bytes:count:) for NWConnection.send() packetization
latency_numbers: 62% bandwidth reduction (2.16 GB → 810 MB per 4K float32 frame) with full IOSurface zero-copy GPU→media-engine pipeline — WWDC21 session 10153
270 MB per-frame intermediate buffer elimination with IOSurface-backed zero-copy pipeline — WWDC21 session 10153
~50% CPU reduction during H.264 playback by eliminating YCbCr copy from CVImageBufferRef — Mozilla bug 1059066 (companion bug 1061525)
~15–60 µs per frame for a memcopy of 1080p–4K YCbCr420 frame at Apple Silicon memory bandwidth (~200 GB/s); this is the cost of the copy that zero-copy avoidance eliminates (calculated, not quoted)
5–30 ms latency spike from deferred autorelease pool drain for a 10+ frame backed-up capture queue — Apple Developer Forums thread 725744 (qualitative, no exact figure)
~1–5 µs per malloc/free pair eliminated by preallocated CMBlockBuffer ring (estimated from general Apple Silicon profiling; no published figure)
claims_to_verify: SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat supports kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange (10-bit) for window capture on macOS 14+ — Apple docs page loads empty in scraping; verify by inspecting the SCStreamConfiguration.Preset enum and pixelFormat property in SDK headers

Setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes causes a full-frame copy blit on macOS (the ALVR comment says visionOS 2, unclear if same behavior on macOS 14+)

MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500 and related 'secret' YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants are available on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon (they are used by ALVR for visionOS; macOS availability is unconfirmed from available sources)

VTCompressionSessionGetPixelBufferPool() returns a pool with IOSurface-backed buffers on macOS by default without requiring explicit kCVPixelBufferIOSurfacePropertiesKey — verify via SDK header or Instruments trace

CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage with a biplanar 10-bit IOSurface (kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange) and MTLPixelFormat.r16Unorm / rg16Unorm works correctly on macOS 14+ (this is inferred from the ALVR format dispatch table; no Apple doc directly confirms the 10-bit path)

DispatchData(bytesNoCopy:count:deallocator:) truly bypasses the copy when passed to NWConnection.send() — Network.framework may internally copy into the kernel send buffer regardless; verify with Instruments 'System Trace' memory allocations on the send path
open_questions: Does ScreenCaptureKit window-capture mode (SCContentFilter with a single window) deliver the same IOSurface-backed CMSampleBuffer as display capture, or does the compositing step introduce a copy? Apple docs are silent on this distinction.

What is the exact minimum pool size needed for VTCompressionSession + in-flight Metal rendering at 60 fps with kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount=0 on Apple Silicon? The encoder may internally hold more than 1 buffer.

Does passing the raw SCK CVImageBuffer to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame require the buffer's pixel format to exactly match the encoder's kVTCompressionPropertyKey_VideoEncoderPixelBufferAttributes, or does VideoToolbox accept any IOSurface-backed YCbCr buffer and do a zero-copy reformat internally?

Is CVMetalTextureCacheFlush mandatory on each frame, or only periodically? The docs say 'periodically' but do not quantify — calling it every frame may add overhead; not calling it may cause stale texture references.

For the Network.framework QUIC path: does NWProtocolQUIC copy the DispatchData into its own send buffer (similar to TCP), or can a DispatchData with a custom deallocator achieve true zero-copy to the NIC on Apple Silicon with QUIC?

verifications.claim: SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat supports kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange (10-bit) for window capture on macOS 14+ — Apple docs page loads empty in scraping; verify by inspecting the SCStreamConfiguration.Preset enum and pixelFormat property in SDK headers

verdict.claim: SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat supports kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange (10-bit) for window capture on macOS 14+, with the SCStreamConfiguration.Preset enum and pixelFormat property in SDK headers being relevant evidence.
verdict.evidence:
The definitive source is the local Xcode SDK header at `/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/ScreenCaptureKit.framework/Versions/A/Headers/SCStream.h` (lines 224–234). The `pixelFormat` property documentation enumerates exactly six supported FourCC values:

  'BGRA' — Packed Little Endian ARGB8888
  'l10r' — Packed Little Endian ARGB2101010
  '420v' — 2-plane video-range YCbCr 4:2:0 (8-bit, = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange)
  '420f' — 2-plane full-range YCbCr 4:2:0 (8-bit, = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarFullRange)
  'xf44' — 2-plane full-range YCbCr10 4:4:4 (10-bit)
  'RGhA' — 64-bit RGBA IEEE half-precision float

kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange maps to FourCC 'x420' (confirmed in CoreVideo CVPixelBuffer.h line 111). The string 'x420' does NOT appear in SCStreamConfiguration's supported pixel format list — neither as the constant name nor as the FourCC. The only 10-bit YCbCr format explicitly supported in the pixelFormat property is 'xf44' (4:4:4), not 4:2:0.

The SCStreamConfiguration.Preset enum (SCStreamConfigurationPreset) is real and confirmed (SCStream.h line 207), but it is API_AVAILABLE(macos(15.0)) — not macOS 14+. Its cases (CaptureHDRStreamLocalDisplay, CaptureHDRStreamCanonicalDisplay, etc.) set captureDynamicRange, colorSpace, and colorMatrix together — the header comments state these presets configure pixelFormat automatically (line 194), but the preset mechanism does not add 'x420' as a valid direct pixelFormat assignment value. The captureDynamicRange property itself is also API_AVAILABLE(macos(15.0)), not 14.

Multiple open-source ScreenCaptureKit projects (github.com/jkuri/Reframed, github.com/jsattler/BetterCapture, github.com/lihaoyun6/QuickRecorder) assign kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange to config.pixelFormat at runtime, and one commenter explicitly notes color bleed artifacts with 420 encoding. This runtime assignment may not error (OSType is unchecked at the Swift level) but it is not in the SDK's documented supported format list and its behavior is undocumented/unsupported.

The WWDC24 session "Capture HDR content with ScreenCaptureKit" recommends using the Preset initializer and states "10-bit YCbCr will be the best choice for HDR" without naming the specific constant, instead letting the preset handle the format selection.
verdict.correction: The documented supported 10-bit pixel format for SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat is 'xf44' (kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarFullRange for 4:4:4, full-range), NOT 'x420' (kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange, 4:2:0 video-range). The SDK header's exhaustive list omits 'x420' entirely. For 10-bit HDR output, Apple's recommended path since macOS 15.0 is to use SCStreamConfiguration(preset: .captureHDRStreamLocalDisplay) or .captureHDRStreamCanonicalDisplay, which internally configure pixel format, color space, color matrix, and captureDynamicRange as a unit. Direct assignment of kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr10BiPlanarVideoRange to pixelFormat is neither listed in documented supported formats nor gated by a macOS 14 availability annotation — HDR capture APIs (captureDynamicRange, Preset) require macOS 15.0, not 14.0. The Preset enum itself is API_AVAILABLE(macos(15.0)).

verifications.claim: Setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes causes a full-frame copy blit on macOS (the ALVR comment says visionOS 2, unclear if same behavior on macOS 14+)

verdict.claim: Setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes causes a full-frame copy blit on macOS (the ALVR comment says visionOS 2, unclear if same behavior on macOS 14+)
verdict.evidence:
1. SOURCE OF THE CLAIM — ALVR visionOS VideoHandler.swift (lines 163–175): The exact comment in the ALVR visionOS client reads: "On visionOS 2, setting pixelFormat *at all* causes a copy to an uncompressed MTLTexture buffer, so we are avoiding it for now." The code deliberately omits kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey from the destinationImageBufferAttributes dict and leaves only kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey and kCVPixelBufferPoolMinimumBufferCountKey. This is a first-person developer observation on visionOS 2, not macOS.

2. THE CLAIM IMPRECISION — The claim under review adds "macOS (the ALVR comment says visionOS 2, unclear if same behavior on macOS 14+)". The ALVR comment makes no claim about macOS at all. The parenthetical is a correctly-stated uncertainty, not a confirmed extension of the observation.

3. APPLE SDK MECHANISM (confirmed applicable to both platforms) — VTDecompressionProperties.h (iOS 18.4 SDK and macOS 11.3 SDK, identical language): kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_PixelBufferPoolIsShared is false "if separate pools are used because the pixel buffer attributes specified by the video decoder and the client were incompatible." When the requested kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey does not match the decoder's native output format, VideoToolbox creates separate pools and performs a copy via an internal VTPixelTransferSession (confirmed by kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_PixelTransferProperties, present in all modern SDK headers). This mechanism exists on both macOS and iOS/visionOS.

4. MACOS DIFFERS IN ONE IMPORTANT ASPECT — On macOS, FFmpeg's VideoToolbox decoder (videotoolbox.c line 817–818) routinely sets kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey via the imageBufferAttributes (conditionally when pix_fmt != 0) AND also sets kCVPixelBufferIOSurfacePropertiesKey. IOSurface-backed buffers on macOS allow zero-copy Metal texturing via CVMetalTextureCache even after format selection — the "copy" cost becomes a GPU blit handled by the media engine, not a full CPU-side memcpy. The visionOS comment implies a more expensive or different copy path on that platform.

5. KEY DISTINCTION — Whether setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey to a format that *matches* the decoder's native output (e.g. kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for 8-bit H.264/HEVC on Apple Silicon, or the packed 10-bit formats for HEVC Main 10) triggers any copy is unclear from public Apple documentation. If the requested format is native to the hardware decoder, kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_PixelBufferPoolIsShared should be true and no copy occurs. If the requested format differs from the hardware decoder's natural output, a copy/transfer is inserted. The ALVR comment was filed against visionOS 2 behavior where "setting pixelFormat *at all*" triggered the copy, suggesting even native-format requests caused a copy — this is anomalous and may be a visionOS 2-specific regression or behavior unique to that platform's compositor pipeline.
verdict.correction: The claim is imprecise in two ways. (1) The ALVR comment explicitly attributes the copy-blit behavior to visionOS 2 only — it does NOT mention macOS 14+. The parenthetical "(unclear if same behavior on macOS 14+)" in the original claim is a correct description of uncertainty, not a confirmed finding, but the framing implies the claim is about macOS when the underlying observation is visionOS-specific. (2) The copy is not unconditional on all platforms: Apple's SDK headers (VTDecompressionPropertyKey_PixelBufferPoolIsShared) show that a copy-path (via an internal VTPixelTransferSession) is triggered specifically when the requested pixel format is incompatible with the decoder's native output — not merely by setting the key at all. On visionOS 2 specifically, the ALVR developers observed that setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey at all (even to a plausibly-native format) triggers a copy to an uncompressed MTLTexture. Whether the same is true on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon is unknown from public sources. The corrected statement: "Setting kCVPixelBufferPixelFormatTypeKey in VTDecompressionSession imageBufferAttributes was observed to cause a full-frame copy to an uncompressed MTLTexture buffer on visionOS 2 (per ALVR visionos VideoHandler.swift). On macOS, Apple's SDK headers confirm that format-incompatible imageBufferAttributes trigger an internal VTPixelTransferSession (i.e. a GPU blit/copy), but whether this applies when the requested format matches the hardware decoder's native output, or whether visionOS 2's behavior applies identically to macOS 14+, is not confirmed by public Apple documentation."

verifications.claim: MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500 and related 'secret' YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants are available on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon (they are used by ALVR for visionOS; macOS availability is unconfirmed from available sources)

verdict.claim: MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500 and related 'secret' YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants are available on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon (they are used by ALVR for visionOS; macOS availability is unconfirmed from available sources)
verdict.evidence:
Multiple authoritative sources confirm the raw integer value and SPI-tier existence of these constants, but macOS availability cannot be confirmed from public sources.

RAW VALUE CONFIRMED: `MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500` is verified by three independent authoritative sources: (1) Apple's own open-source WebKit (`apple-oss-distributions/WebKit`, Source/WebGPU/WebGPU/MetalSPI.h:38) as `constexpr MTLPixelFormat MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = static_cast<MTLPixelFormat>(500)`, (2) ALVR visionOS VideoHandler.swift:22 `let MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P: UInt = 500`, and (3) the same definition re-mirrored in OpenJDK JavaFX's WebGPU/MetalSPI.h. The full set spans values 500–585 covering 8-, 10-, and 12-bit YCbCr variants.

PRIVATE/SPI STATUS: In Apple's WebKit MetalSPI.h, the entire block is inside `#else` of `#if USE(APPLE_INTERNAL_SDK)`, meaning Apple's own internal builds import them from private Metal headers (`MTLTexture_Private.h`, `MTLDevice_Private.h`), while public builds define them as raw-cast workarounds. The constants do NOT appear in the public `MTLPixelFormat` NS_ENUM in any public SDK header — Sourcegraph keyword searches across `xybp888/iOS-SDKs` and `alexey-lysiuk/macos-sdk` for `MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P` in `MTLPixelFormat.h` return zero results.

CIRCUMSTANTIAL MACOS EVIDENCE: (a) `keith/Xcode.app-strings` shows `MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P` and `MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P_sRGB` inside Xcode's `MTLTools.framework/Versions/A/MTLTools` binary string table — this framework runs on macOS and is used by Metal debugging tools. (b) Apple's `CMImaging` and `NRFV4` system dylibs (present on Apple platforms, confirmed in `blacktop/ipsw-diffs` macOS/iOS 26 diff) reference these format names in assertion strings. (c) ALVR visionOS itself labels them "Non-conclusive list of interesting private Metal pixel formats" and guards with `#if !targetEnvironment(simulator)`.

MACOS AVAILABILITY UNCONFIRMED: No `API_AVAILABLE(macos(14.0))` or equivalent annotation exists because these are not in the public enum. No Apple documentation, WWDC session, or Metal Feature Set Tables PDF mentions YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants by these names. The Metal Feature Set Tables PDF contains no YCbCr format entries. MoltenVK (which exhaustively maps Metal capabilities) maps the corresponding Vulkan YCbCr formats to `MTLPixelFormat::Invalid` on macOS, suggesting they are not usable via the standard Metal path. The specific claim "available on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon" is unconfirmed — the MTLTools.framework string table evidence is circumstantial, not functional confirmation.

SPELLING VERIFIED: The exact symbol name `MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P` (all-caps YCBCR, underscore-8_420_2P) is correct as stated.
verdict.correction: The claim is partially correct and partially imprecise. Corrected statement: "MTLPixelFormatYCBCR8_420_2P = 500 (and related YCbCr MTLPixelFormat constants spanning values 500–585) are confirmed as Apple-internal SPI constants, defined in Apple's private Metal headers. They do NOT appear in the public MTLPixelFormat enum in any SDK. Apple's own open-source WebKit defines them as raw-cast workarounds under #else of #if USE(APPLE_INTERNAL_SDK). ALVR visionOS correctly labels them 'private' and guards them with !targetEnvironment(simulator). The raw value 500 is confirmed correct. The claim that they are 'available on macOS 14+ Apple Silicon' is unconfirmed from public sources — circumstantial evidence exists (Xcode MTLTools.framework string tables on macOS list them; Apple system dylibs CMImaging and NRFV4 reference them) but no API_AVAILABLE annotation, no public documentation, and no Metal Feature Set Table entry exists for any Apple platform. MoltenVK maps the equivalent Vulkan YCbCr formats to MTLPixelFormat::Invalid on macOS, indicating they are not part of the documented Metal surface. Any use of these formats on macOS is use of undocumented SPI with no OS-version guarantee."

rawCorpus.summary:
**Clock domains on Apple Silicon and the synchronization problem.** All Apple platforms use `mach_absolute_time()` as their root monotonic clock, ticking every 41.67 ns on M-series chips (mach_timebase_info returns numer=125, denom=3 — never assume 1:1 as on Intel). `CACurrentMediaTime()` is a thin wrapper around the same clock, converting ticks to seconds. `CMClockGetHostTimeClock()` returns a CMClock backed by the same source. `clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW)` is the POSIX equivalent used by Moonlight's `PltGetMicroseconds()` on Darwin. All CoreMedia timestamps (SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime, CMSampleBuffer PTS, VideoToolbox output, MTLDrawable.presentedTime) are in this same host-time domain, so intra-device arithmetic is straightforward. The cross-device problem is that the two macOS hosts have independent mach clocks with unknown offset; without synchronization, a "one-way latency" derived from them is meaningless.

**Cross-device clock synchronization options.** macOS lacks native PTP/IEEE-1588 hardware timestamping. The built-in `timed` daemon uses Apple's proprietary NTP-like protocol (not IEEE-1588), syncing roughly every 15 minutes to `time.apple.com`. On a LAN this yields ~10 ms accuracy at best. Replacing `timed` with chrony (via ChronyControl) and pointing both machines at a shared LAN stratum-1 server achieves a few hundred microseconds of agreement on a LAN — adequate for splitting stages to ~1 ms resolution. True PTP with hardware timestamping (sub-microsecond) is not available on macOS because neither the OS kernel nor the Apple Silicon NIC drivers expose the required SO_TIMESTAMPING kernel hooks; ptp4l and linuxptp are Linux-only. The practical substitute is an application-level NTP-style exchange: send a probe packet, record t0 on the sender; receiver stamps t1 and t2, returns them; sender receives at t3. Clock offset = ((t1−t0)+(t2−t3))/2. RTT = (t3−t0)−(t2−t1). Run 8–16 rounds, keep the round with minimum RTT (Cristian/NTP minimum-RTT filter), average the survivors. On a wired Gigabit LAN this achieves <200 µs systematic bias. Using `CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW` (same source as `mach_absolute_time()`) ensures the timestamps do not drift from NTP adjustments mid-measurement. Embed the resulting `host_offset_ns` in your session state and apply it on every frame receipt.

**Instrumenting the six pipeline stages.** Stage 1 — Capture: `SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime` (key on the CMSampleBuffer attachment dictionary, type UInt64, units = mach_absolute_time ticks) records the moment the display hardware composited the window; this is the true "frame born" time. Convert to nanoseconds with the mach_timebase_info ratio. Stage 2 — Encode submit/done: stamp `CACurrentMediaTime()` immediately before `VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame` (encode_submit_ns); stamp again inside the VTCompressionOutputHandler closure (encode_done_ns). The difference is pure HW encode latency (typically 1–4 ms for HEVC HW on Apple Silicon in low-latency mode). Stage 3 — Network send: stamp just before the first UDP/QUIC `send` call (send_ns). Stage 4 — Network receive: `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime` (UInt64, nanoseconds from a kernel-assigned epoch) gives the kernel-level packet arrival time; enable it by setting `shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true` on the NWParameters before connecting. The epoch is not mach_absolute_time — it must be correlated via the clock-offset exchange described above. Stage 5 — Decode done: stamp `CACurrentMediaTime()` in the VTDecompressionSession output handler (decode_done_ns). Stage 6 — Render/display: `MTLDrawable.presentedTime` (host time in seconds, same domain as `CACurrentMediaTime()`) fires in the `addPresentedHandler` closure and gives the actual vsync-aligned display time. The total pipeline = `(presentedTime - displayTime_on_host) + host_offset_ns`.

**Frame header design for per-frame split attribution.** Embed a fixed-size metadata header in each UDP datagram (or QUIC STREAM frame prefix): `capture_ts_ns` (UInt64, mach_absolute_time converted to ns at capture), `encode_done_ns` (UInt64), `frame_seq` (UInt32). The client, upon receiving the first packet for a frame, reads `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime` and stores it as `recv_ts_ns`. When the VTDecompressionSession callback fires, it records `decode_done_ns = CACurrentMediaTime() * 1e9`. On first render, `MTLDrawable.presentedTime * 1e9` gives `present_ts_ns`. Using the pre-computed `host_offset_ns`, the true per-frame breakdown in microseconds is: capture-to-encode = encode_done_ns − capture_ts_ns; encode-to-wire = send_ns − encode_done_ns; network transit = (recv_ts_ns + host_offset_ns) − send_ns; decode = decode_done_ns − (recv_ts_ns + host_offset_ns); render queue = present_ts_ns − decode_done_ns; total = present_ts_ns − (capture_ts_ns − host_offset_ns). Sunshine uses exactly this pattern: `frame_processing_latency` = `now − packet->frame_timestamp` is encoded into the frame header as a 16-bit uint16 in 1/10 ms units and consumed by Moonlight's decoder (`DECODE_UNIT.frameHostProcessingLatency`). Moonlight on Darwin uses `clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW)` for all its client-side timestamps.

**Ground-truth glass-to-glass measurement.** The canonical method does not require synchronized clocks: attach an LED to the input device (or have software flash a pixel on the host screen) and a photodiode (or phototransistor) on the client display. A microcontroller with a shared timebase triggers the LED and measures the photodiode response edge. Resolution achievable: 21 ns with an onboard crystal (Pengutronix Optick). For a software-only approach, record with an iPhone 6 or later at 240 fps (4.17 ms/frame granularity); use the "Is It Snappy?" iOS app (App Store) to mark input and output frames and read off milliseconds. The Parsec team used a 1000 fps Sony RX100 IV camera plus a Makey Makey board with an LED to measure 7 ms of protocol latency on LAN. Accuracy at 240 fps is ±4.2 ms per frame; at 1000 fps it is ±1 ms per frame. For sub-millisecond ground truth on a budget, a Raspberry Pi Pico (or Arduino) with a photodiode and a timer captures transitions with ~20 ns resolution at roughly $10 in hardware — the approach Optick commercializes. The photodiode/LED method also validates the software-attributed breakdown: if the sum of software-measured stages disagrees with the hardware measurement by more than ~1 ms, there is a clock or pipeline instrumentation bug.

techniques.name: Application-level NTP-style clock offset exchange (Cristian / LSL protocol)
techniques.how: Both host and client run a lightweight UDP exchange at session startup. Sender records t0 = clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW); receiver stamps t1 (arrival) and t2 (reply send); sender receives at t3. Offset = ((t1-t0)+(t2-t3))/2, RTT = (t3-t0)-(t2-t1). Run 8-16 rounds, discard outliers beyond 1 SD, keep minimum-RTT sample. Result: host_offset_ns stored per session. On wired Gigabit LAN asymmetry is <50 µs, so systematic bias is <25 µs. Re-run every ~30 seconds to track drift.
techniques.latency_impact: Enables one-way latency attribution to ±50-200 µs on LAN. Without this, per-stage numbers are meaningless.

techniques.name: SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime — kernel capture timestamp
techniques.how: In the SCStreamOutput callback, extract attachments via CMSampleBufferGetSampleAttachmentsArray. Key .displayTime returns a UInt64 in mach_absolute_time tick units representing when the display hardware composited the window. Convert to nanoseconds: ns = ticks * numer / denom (mach_timebase_info). On M-series: numer=125, denom=3, so ns = ticks * 125/3. This is the true capture-time anchor; do NOT use CMSampleBufferGetPresentationTimeStamp() for latency math because its clock domain relative to CACurrentMediaTime() has been reported as producing negative values (Apple DTS acknowledged, filed as a bug, macOS 15 era).
techniques.latency_impact: Provides the epoch for capture-side timing. Critical — without it you cannot measure capture-to-encode lag.

techniques.name: VTCompressionSession encode latency measurement
techniques.how: Record encode_submit_ns = clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW) immediately before VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame(). Inside the VTCompressionOutputHandler closure (called on a queue when the hardware encoder finishes), record encode_done_ns. Encode latency = encode_done_ns - encode_submit_ns. On Apple Silicon HEVC hardware in low-latency mode (kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime=true, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering=false, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval=0), typically 1-4 ms. The PTS you passed to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame is preserved in the output CMSampleBuffer and can be used as a frame sequence anchor.
techniques.latency_impact: Typically 1-4 ms on Apple Silicon HW encoder. Measures encoder contribution precisely.

techniques.name: NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime — kernel packet-receive timestamp
techniques.how: Before creating the NWConnection, set shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true on the NWParameters (or NWProtocolIP.Options). On receive, cast the protocol metadata to NWProtocolIP.Metadata and read .receiveTime (UInt64, nanoseconds from a kernel clock epoch). The C API is nw_ip_metadata_get_receive_time(). This is the closest to SO_TIMESTAMP available through Network.framework. The epoch differs from mach_absolute_time; to correlate it, measure the offset between receiveTime and CACurrentMediaTime()*1e9 on a loopback packet at session start.
techniques.latency_impact: Enables true kernel-level packet-arrival time; avoids userspace scheduling jitter (~1-5 ms on loaded systems) polluting network transit measurement.

techniques.name: VTDecompressionSession decode latency measurement
techniques.how: Record decode_submit_ns = CACurrentMediaTime()*1e9 before VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame(). In the VTDecompressionOutputHandler, record decode_done_ns = CACurrentMediaTime()*1e9. Alternatively, stamp the time at the last packet receipt (NWProtocolIP receiveTime + epoch correction) and compare to decode_done_ns in the callback. Both are in the same CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW domain on Darwin.
techniques.latency_impact: Typically 1-3 ms for HEVC HW decode on Apple Silicon. Small but measurable.

techniques.name: MTLDrawable.presentedTime — actual hardware display time
techniques.how: Attach an addPresentedHandler closure to the MTLDrawable before calling present(). When the GPU compositor actually scans out the frame, the handler fires with presentedTime (host time in seconds, same domain as CACurrentMediaTime()). Convert to ns by multiplying by 1e9. This is the last stage timestamp. For AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer-based render, use kCMSampleAttachmentKey_DisplayImmediately=kCFBooleanTrue (sets immediate/ASAP display) and observe the layer's output; there is no equivalent presentedTime callback — MTLDrawable is strongly preferred for latency measurement.
