techniques.latency_impact: Measures actual scanout time vs decode-done; captures render-queue and vsync wait latency.

techniques.name: Frame header metadata — per-frame host timestamps embedded in the bitstream
techniques.how: In the UDP datagram header (or QUIC STREAM prefix, before the HEVC NAL data), embed: frame_seq (uint32), capture_ts_ns (uint64, mach_absolute_time converted to ns), encode_done_ns (uint64). On client: read capture_ts_ns + encode_done_ns from header when first packet arrives, combine with receiveTime and decode/present timestamps. This is exactly what Sunshine does (frame_processing_latency field = encode_done - capture_ts in 1/10 ms, 16-bit) and what Moonlight reads as DECODE_UNIT.frameHostProcessingLatency. Adding full 64-bit ns timestamps costs 16 bytes/frame — negligible.
techniques.latency_impact: Enables per-frame split of host vs network vs client latency without any clock sync requirement for host-side stages.

techniques.name: Chrony as NTP client on both macOS machines (replacing timed)
techniques.how: Install ChronyControl (macOS GUI) or build chrony from source. ChronyControl disables timed during installation. Configure both host and client to sync from the same local LAN stratum-1 source (a Raspberry Pi running chrony with GPS PPS, or an off-the-shelf GPS NTP appliance). With a shared LAN NTP server, inter-machine offset is a few hundred microseconds. Re-enables the application-level offset exchange as a cross-check. Accuracy improves further with GPS source to ~1 µs.
techniques.latency_impact: Achieves ~200-500 µs LAN clock sync without any hardware PTP support. Sufficient for 1 ms stage resolution.

techniques.name: PTP / IEEE-1588 on macOS LAN
techniques.how: No native macOS support. ptp4l (linuxptp) is Linux-only and requires kernel SO_TIMESTAMPING hooks absent from macOS. ptpd is available as a Homebrew formula and runs on macOS but uses software timestamping only (no NIC hardware assist), reducing accuracy to ~100 µs - 1 ms depending on system load. For the target (single-window sharing, two macOS machines on LAN), the NTP/chrony approach plus the application-level exchange is more practical and achieves similar accuracy without the ptpd maintenance burden.
techniques.latency_impact: Hardware PTP would give <1 µs accuracy; software PTP on macOS gives ~100 µs - 1 ms. Not recommended as primary path.

techniques.name: High-speed camera method (240 fps iPhone / 1000 fps Sony RX100)
techniques.how: Point the camera to simultaneously capture (a) input action or a flashing LED on the host screen, and (b) the client display. Use iPhone rear camera at 240 fps (4.17 ms/frame) or Sony RX100 IV at 1000 fps (1 ms/frame). Analyze frame-by-frame in slow motion. For input: connect a Makey Makey board to the client; the physical press triggers an LED and the cursor/animation appears on both host and client screens. Count frames between LED illumination and client-display response. Software tool: 'Is It Snappy?' iOS app for 240 fps analysis, or MPC-HC frame-by-frame for high-fps footage. This method requires NO clock synchronization and measures true glass-to-glass.
techniques.latency_impact: Ground-truth measurement. Parsec measured 7 ms of protocol latency this way. Resolution: ±4.2 ms at 240 fps, ±1 ms at 1000 fps.

techniques.name: LED + photodiode microcontroller rig (Optick / DIY)
techniques.how: Attach a bright LED in front of the capture camera (or drive a pixel on the host window). Place a phototransistor/photodiode directly on the client display surface. Connect both to a microcontroller (Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32, or Arduino with hardware timer capture). The MCU timestamps both the LED trigger edge and the photodiode rising edge using the same hardware timebase. Resolution: 21 ns on a crystal oscillator (Pengutronix Optick). Cost: ~$10-30 DIY, or the commercial Optick unit from Linux Automation GmbH. Output: a histogram of glass-to-glass latency across many frames, surfacing jitter as well as mean.
techniques.latency_impact: Highest accuracy ground truth: 21 ns resolution, no clock sync required. Validates all software-attributed numbers.

techniques.name: Metal GPU timing (MTLCommandBuffer.gpuStartTime / gpuEndTime)
techniques.how: After the MTLCommandBuffer is committed, wait for completion, then read .gpuStartTime and .gpuEndTime (both in host time seconds, same as CACurrentMediaTime()). The difference is the true GPU execution time for the render pass. gpuStartTime - commitTime measures scheduling latency in the GPU command queue. Available since macOS 10.15 / iOS 10.3. For a Metal-based renderer, this gives precise GPU render time separate from vsync wait.
techniques.latency_impact: Isolates GPU render time (typically 0.5-3 ms) from vsync queue delay (0 to one full frame period).

techniques.name: mach_absolute_time / CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW as the canonical measurement clock
techniques.how: Use clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW) (Swift: equivalent via POSIX bridge) for all latency measurements on Apple Silicon. This returns nanoseconds, is monotonic, does not stop during sleep, is unaffected by NTP adjustments (unlike CLOCK_MONOTONIC on macOS which IS affected by timed), and maps to the same hardware timer as mach_absolute_time(). To convert mach_absolute_time() ticks to ns: call mach_timebase_info(&info) once at startup, then ns = ticks * info.numer / info.denom. On M1/M2/M3/M4: numer=125, denom=3. CACurrentMediaTime() returns seconds from the same source. Avoid CLOCK_REALTIME (wall-clock, can jump) and CLOCK_MONOTONIC (NTP-adjusted on macOS).
techniques.latency_impact: Foundational — all stage timestamps must use this clock for consistent arithmetic. Using mixed clocks invalidates all measurements.

key_settings: SCStreamFrameInfo attachment key `.displayTime` (UInt64, mach_absolute_time ticks) — true capture epoch
mach_timebase_info: numer=125, denom=3 on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) — always call at startup, never hardcode
CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW via clock_gettime_nsec_np() — use for all latency stamps; immune to NTP adjustments
NWProtocolIP.Options.shouldCalculateReceiveTime = true — enable kernel packet-receive timestamps
NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime (UInt64 nanoseconds) — read in NWConnection.receive callback via protocolMetadata
MTLDrawable.addPresentedHandler — closure fires with presentedTime (seconds, host time) when frame actually scans out
MTLCommandBuffer.gpuStartTime / .gpuEndTime (seconds, host time) — GPU execution interval for render pass
VTCompressionSession: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime=true, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering=false — required for low-latency encode to make encode_done_ns meaningful
CMSampleBufferGetSampleAttachmentsArray() — extract SCStreamFrameInfo dictionary from capture buffer
kCMSampleAttachmentKey_DisplayImmediately = kCFBooleanTrue — for AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer ASAP render (no presentedTime callback)
Per-frame header: embed capture_ts_ns (uint64) + encode_done_ns (uint64) + frame_seq (uint32) = 20 bytes overhead
Sunshine pattern: frame_processing_latency field in RTP/frame header = uint16 in 1/10 ms units (capture-to-send)
latency_numbers: NTP (macOS timed daemon, LAN): ~10 ms accuracy — Eclectic Light Company / Apple timed documentation
NTP (chrony, LAN, shared local server): 200-500 µs accuracy — scottstuff.net NTP limits article (2025)
NTP (chrony + GPS PPS, LAN): ~1 µs accuracy — scottstuff.net, chrony-project.org examples
Application-level NTP-style offset exchange (LAN wired, min-RTT filter): <200 µs systematic bias — LSL time_synchronization docs
PTP hardware (IEEE-1588, Linux with hw-timestamping NIC): 20-100 ns — IEEE PTP spec / Pengutronix
Software PTP on macOS (ptpd, no hw timestamps): ~100 µs - 1 ms — inferred from Linux software PTP baselines
Optick microcontroller photodiode rig resolution: 21 ns — Pengutronix blog (May 2024)
iPhone 240 fps camera latency measurement: ±4.17 ms per-frame resolution — isitsnappy.com
1000 fps camera (Sony RX100 IV) latency measurement: ±1 ms per-frame resolution — Parsec blog
Parsec total pipeline latency on Gigabit LAN: 4-8 ms at 240 fps — Parsec Medium blog
Parsec DIY measured protocol overhead on LAN: 7 ms — Parsec DIY measurement blog
VideoToolbox HEVC HW encode latency (low-latency mode, Apple Silicon): ~1-4 ms — WWDC21 session 10158 (100 ms reduction for 720p30 claim implies encode is a small fraction)
mach_absolute_time tick on Apple Silicon M1: 41.67 ns (numer=125, denom=3) — Eclectic Light (2020), confirmed in multiple sources
Moonlight frameHostProcessingLatency granularity: 0.1 ms (1/10 ms units) — Limelight.h source code
macOS timed NTP sync interval: approximately every 15 minutes — Apple developer forums / timed documentation
claims_to_verify: NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime epoch: the documentation says 'nanoseconds based on a clock epoch' but does not specify which epoch — verify whether it is mach_absolute_time epoch, CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW, or a separate kernel monotonic clock, and whether a calibration loopback is needed

SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime clock domain: an Apple DTS engineer confirmed it differs from CACurrentMediaTime() but did not specify the conversion path — verify the exact relationship or conversion API on macOS 14/15

MTLDrawable.presentedTime same clock as CACurrentMediaTime(): multiple secondary sources confirm this but Apple's official docs only say 'host time in seconds' — verify this is in fact the mach_absolute_time domain
mach_timebase_info numer=125, denom=3 on all M-series chips: verified for M1 but confirm M2/M3/M4 share the same ratio (the value can vary per model per Apple's own documentation caveat)

CLOCK_MONOTONIC on macOS is NTP-adjusted (unlike CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW): the Apple developer forums thread implies this, but verify via Darwin source or official documentation that CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW is the correct immune alternative
ptpd availability and software-only accuracy on macOS 14+: Homebrew formula exists but accuracy without kernel SO_TIMESTAMPING is unverified on Apple Silicon — measure empirically

NWProtocolIP.Options.shouldCalculateReceiveTime availability for QUIC connections (not just UDP): verify this property works when the NWParameters uses QUIC not raw UDP, since QUIC adds a framing layer above IP
open_questions: What is the exact clock-domain relationship between SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime and mach_absolute_time() — are they the same ticks or different epoch? Apple has not documented this formally.

Does NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime work for QUIC datagrams (QUIC DATAGRAM extension) or only for raw UDP sockets? The API is on NWProtocolIP (IP layer) so it should be independent, but needs verification.

Is there any Apple-private or SPI API that exposes PTP-style hardware timestamps from the Apple Silicon Ethernet or Wi-Fi controller? Apple uses precise time internally (e.g., for AirPlay sync) — whether this is accessible to third-party apps is unknown.

CAMetalDisplayLink.Update.presentationTime vs MTLDrawable.presentedTime: CAMetalDisplayLink is preferred for frame pacing; verify whether its presentationTime is a prediction (like targetTimestamp) or the actual hardware scanout confirmation.

For QUIC streams (not datagrams), the receive time of individual QUIC packets is hidden inside the QUIC layer — the app sees stream bytes, not packets. Is there a Network.framework API or QUIC extension that surfaces per-STREAM-FRAME arrival time for latency measurement?

verifications.claim: NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime epoch: the documentation says 'nanoseconds based on a clock epoch' but does not specify which epoch — verify whether it is mach_absolute_time epoch, CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW, or a separate kernel monotonic clock, and whether a calibration loopback is needed

verdict.claim: NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime documentation says 'nanoseconds based on a clock epoch' but does not specify which epoch — verify whether it is mach_absolute_time epoch, CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW, or a separate kernel monotonic clock, and whether a calibration loopback is needed
verdict.evidence:
The claim that the documentation "does not specify which epoch" is directly refuted by multiple independent sources all drawing from Apple's own documentation corpus.

1. The Apple developer docs (mirrored in the apple-docs-for-rag repository on GitHub, which indexes Apple's official documentation markdown) state unambiguously for both the Swift property and the C function:
   - Swift: `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime` — "The time at which a packet was received, in nanoseconds, based on `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW`." (zhangyu1818/apple-docs-for-rag: Network/md/network-nwprotocolip-nwprotocolip.metadata-receivetime.md, line 12)
   - C: `nw_ip_metadata_get_receive_time(_:)` — "Access the time at which a packet was received, in nanoseconds, based on `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW`." (same repo, Network/md/network-nw_ip_metadata_get_receive_time(_:).md, line 10)

   This wording is repeated consistently across every related Network framework doc page in that repo (8+ pages all say `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW`).

2. The clock identity is CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW — NOT mach_absolute_time and NOT CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW. These are meaningfully different:
   - `mach_absolute_time()` and `CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW` are equivalent (the macOS man page for clock_gettime(3) explicitly states "The returned value is identical to the result of mach_absolute_time() after the appropriate mach_timebase conversion is applied" for CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW). Both do NOT increment during system sleep.
   - `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW` DOES increment during system sleep (it tracks elapsed real time since an arbitrary boot-like point, unaffected by NTP/frequency adjustments). The LLVM libc++ patch D74341 confirms: CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW "is monotonic, increased during sleep."
   - Because CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW and CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW diverge whenever the machine has been asleep, their numeric values are NOT interchangeable without calibration.

3. Calibration implication: For a LAN-only latency measurement scenario, if the local timestamp is taken with mach_absolute_time() or clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW), and receiveTime is in CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW, a one-time offset calibration IS required between the two clocks (take both readings simultaneously at startup to get the delta; the delta is stable between reboots but diverges if the machine sleeps between capture of receiveTime and the reference timestamp). The "epoch" for both is an arbitrary point near system boot, but they diverge post-sleep.
sources: https://www.manpagez.com/man/3/clock_gettime/ (macOS clock_gettime man page, CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW = mach_absolute_time after timebase conversion)
https://reviews.llvm.org/D74341 (LLVM D74341: CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW is monotonic, increased during sleep, nanosecond precision on Apple)
verdict.correction: The documentation DOES specify the clock: both `NWProtocolIP.Metadata.receiveTime` (Swift) and `nw_ip_metadata_get_receive_time()` (C) are documented as returning nanoseconds based on `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW` — not mach_absolute_time, not CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW. CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW differs from mach_absolute_time/CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW in one critical way: it DOES increment during system sleep, whereas mach_absolute_time and CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW do not. Both have an arbitrary epoch near system boot, but their values diverge after any sleep event. A calibration offset IS needed if your local timestamps use mach_absolute_time or CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW — read both clocks simultaneously at startup (e.g., `clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW) - clock_gettime_nsec_np(CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW)`) to get the current sleep-accumulated offset; this delta changes every time the machine wakes from sleep, so the calibration must be refreshed on wake notifications.

verifications.claim: SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime clock domain: an Apple DTS engineer confirmed it differs from CACurrentMediaTime() but did not specify the conversion path — verify the exact relationship or conversion API on macOS 14/15

verdict.claim: SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime clock domain: an Apple DTS engineer confirmed it differs from CACurrentMediaTime() but did not specify the conversion path — verify the exact relationship or conversion API on macOS 14/15
verdict.evidence:
Three authoritative SDK headers in the macOS 26.5 SDK (applicable to macOS 12.3+) establish the relationship conclusively:

1. SCStream.h (ScreenCaptureKit), line 399 — the official Objective-C header comment for SCStreamFrameInfoDisplayTime reads verbatim: "The key for the CFDictionary attached to the CMSampleBuffer for the mach absolute time when the event occurred. For a frame event, this is when the frame was displayed by the window server." Path: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX26.5.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/ScreenCaptureKit.framework/Versions/A/Headers/SCStream.h:399

2. CABase.h (QuartzCore), line 124–125 — the official comment for CACurrentMediaTime reads: "Returns the current CoreAnimation absolute time. This is the result of calling mach_absolute_time() and converting the units to seconds." Path: ...QuartzCore.framework/.../CABase.h:124

3. CMSync.h (CoreMedia), line 127–128 — CMClockGetHostTimeClock() comment: "On Mac OS X, the host time clock uses mach_absolute_time but returns a value with a large integer timescale (eg. nanoseconds)." Path: ...CoreMedia.framework/.../CMSync.h:127

All three share the same underlying clock source: mach_absolute_time(), which is a monotonic tick counter that starts at boot and pauses during sleep. They are therefore in THE SAME clock domain. No "conversion path" across different domains is needed — only a units conversion (raw ticks → seconds).

Regarding the Apple Developer Forums thread (thread/785046, May 2025) where a developer reported negative latency values and an Apple DTS engineer escalated to engineering: the DTS engineer's response was that engineering needed to investigate — they never stated the clocks differ. The forum report of numerically larger timestamps is a runtime anomaly (possibly a bug in how PTS is set on the CMSampleBuffer), not evidence of a different clock domain.

Correct conversion from displayTime (raw mach ticks, UInt64) to seconds for comparison with CACurrentMediaTime():
  var tb = mach_timebase_info_data_t()
  mach_timebase_info(&tb)
  let displaySeconds = Double(displayTime) * Double(tb.numer) / Double(tb.denom) / 1_000_000_000.0
  // now comparable to CACurrentMediaTime()

Alternatively use CMClockMakeHostTimeFromSystemUnits(displayTime) to get a CMTime, then CMTimeGetSeconds().
verdict.correction: SCStreamFrameInfo.displayTime IS in the same clock domain as CACurrentMediaTime(). Both are rooted in mach_absolute_time(). The SDK header for SCStreamFrameInfoDisplayTime explicitly states it is a "mach absolute time" value (raw UInt64 ticks). CACurrentMediaTime() is defined in CABase.h as "the result of calling mach_absolute_time() and converting the units to seconds." No cross-domain conversion is needed — only a units conversion from raw mach ticks to seconds using mach_timebase_info (multiply by numer/denom, divide by 1e9). The Apple DTS engineer in the referenced forum thread (May 2025) did NOT say the clocks differ; they escalated a runtime anomaly (negative latency values) to engineering for investigation, which may reflect a separate SCKit bug in how PTS or displayTime is stamped — not a clock domain mismatch.

verifications.claim: MTLDrawable.presentedTime same clock as CACurrentMediaTime(): multiple secondary sources confirm this but Apple's official docs only say 'host time in seconds' — verify this is in fact the mach_absolute_time domain

verdict.claim: MTLDrawable.presentedTime same clock as CACurrentMediaTime(): multiple secondary sources confirm this but Apple's official docs only say 'host time in seconds' — verify this is in fact the mach_absolute_time domain
verdict.evidence:
Two Apple SDK headers settle the question definitively:

1. **`CABase.h`** (QuartzCore, iPhoneOS 17.5 SDK, line 140–144):
   ```c
   /* Returns the current CoreAnimation absolute time. This is the result of
    * calling mach_absolute_time () and converting the units to seconds. */
   CA_EXTERN CFTimeInterval CACurrentMediaTime (void)
   ```
   This is Apple's own header comment, not secondary commentary.

2. **`CAMediaTiming.h`** (QuartzCore, line 12–14):
   ```
   /* Absolute time is defined as mach time converted to seconds. The
    * CACurrentMediaTime function is provided as a convenience for querying
    * the current absolute time. */
   ```

3. **`MTLDrawable.h`** (Metal, iPhoneOS 17.5 SDK, line 48–52):
   ```objc
   /*!
    @property presentedTime
    @abstract The host time that this drawable was presented on screen.
    */
   @property(nonatomic, readonly) CFTimeInterval presentedTime
   ```

4. **`MTLCommandBuffer.h`** (Metal, line 279–280):
   ```objc
   /*!
    @property GPUStartTime
    @abstract The host time in seconds that GPU starts executing this command buffer.
    */
   @property (readonly) CFTimeInterval GPUStartTime
   ```
   All Metal timing properties (`GPUStartTime`, `GPUEndTime`, `kernelStartTime`, `kernelEndTime`, `presentedTime`) are typed `CFTimeInterval` and described as "host time in seconds."

5. The `presentDrawable:atTime:` method (MTLCommandBuffer.h line 317) takes `CFTimeInterval presentationTime` and its doc comment says "specific host time" — Apple's own sample code passes `CACurrentMediaTime() + offset` to this parameter, confirming they share the same domain.

**Chain of identity:** `MTLDrawable.presentedTime` (CFTimeInterval, "host time in seconds") == `CACurrentMediaTime()` return type and definition (CFTimeInterval, "mach_absolute_time() converted to seconds") == `mach_absolute_time()` domain in seconds.

No conflicting evidence was found across Apple docs, SDK headers, QA1643 (Audio Host Time), or community sources. QA1643 confirms "iOS developers may simply call mach_absolute_time to retrieve the current host time," and that 'host time' IS mach_absolute_time.

rawCorpus.summary:
The best real-world achieved latency for low-latency LAN streamers forms a clear hierarchy. Parsec holds the published record at 4–8 ms total pipeline latency at 240 fps on wired GigE LAN — but this is deliberately an unrealistic best-case demo; their practical recommendation is 60 fps where the pipeline adds ~7 ms over the local connection. Moonlight + Sunshine on wired GigE achieves community-measured totals of roughly 10–20 ms end-to-end at 60 fps, with NVENC encode latency around 5–6 ms (median 5.8 ms per Parsec's own H.264 encoder benchmark), VideoToolbox decode on Apple Silicon around 1–3 ms, and wired network round-trip under 1 ms (GigE serialization is ~12 µs for a 1500-byte frame, sub-millisecond total one-way). ALVR and VR streamers are the most latency-obsessed domain: ALVR targets <50 ms motion-to-photon total, with typical observed totals of 70–140 ms wireless; Virtual Desktop achieves 70–85 ms on Wi-Fi 6E for Quest 3. Wired USB streaming shows a pipeline of ~3.9 ms encode + ~4.6 ms transmission + ~9.3 ms decode = ~19 ms excluding display; motion-to-photon including display is typically 40–85 ms wireless. Apple Vision Pro is measured to be "not far behind" Meta Quest in angular motion-to-photon testing (specific ms withheld in the source graph). The absolute theoretical minimum for a glass-to-glass pipeline at 120 fps on Apple Silicon wired LAN is approximately 10–16 ms: ~1 ms ScreenCaptureKit callback overhead (hardware-accelerated, CPU cost is only 1.9% of one core), ~2–4 ms VideoToolbox HEVC HW encode with low-latency rate control, <0.1 ms wired GigE one-way transmission, ~1–3 ms VideoToolbox HW decode, and ~4–8 ms for display scan-out at 120 Hz (half-frame average = 4.2 ms, worst-case = 8.3 ms). At 60 fps the display contribution doubles (8.3 ms average, up to 16.7 ms worst-case), pushing the realistic floor to 14–26 ms on wired LAN. Wi-Fi adds 1–3 ms on Wi-Fi 6/6E under ideal conditions, with 2–5 ms typical in real environments.

The WWDC 2021 VideoToolbox low-latency mode announcement documented a 100 ms reduction for 720p 30fps — which represents eliminating frame reordering and buffering overhead, not the raw encode time itself. The key Apple Silicon breakthrough is that `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` supports HEVC (H.265) on Apple Silicon (unlike Intel Macs where only H.264 was supported), confirmed via FFmpeg patch PR #20453. The mode enforces one-in-one-out encoding with no frame reordering. However, a critical caveat: querying `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` after setting this flag returns `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` (-12900), suggesting the property query itself is unsupported in low-latency mode (not necessarily that hardware acceleration is absent — Apple's VideoToolbox documentation is acknowledged as "often incomplete or wrong"). The Apple Media Engine on M-series chips is documented to encode HEVC at ~300 fps at 1080p, meaning per-frame encode time averages ~3.3 ms ceiling at 1080p, but actual low-latency single-frame encode latency is unquantified by Apple publicly.

On the display side, AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds measurable extra latency vs. a direct Metal renderer path on macOS. Moonlight issue #1885 documents that `AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer` adds "one or more extra frames" of buffering vs `VT_FORCE_METAL=1` on macOS with discrete GPUs. This is a critical finding for the target stack: the Apple-recommended `AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer` path incurs compositor buffering overhead that the Metal-direct path avoids. For the lowest display latency, decoded frames should be presented via `CAMetalLayer` / direct Metal rendering rather than routing through `AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer`. Display scanout geometry: at 120 Hz each scanout row takes 1/120th of the frame time ÷ display height; the average scanout position contribution is half-frame = 4.2 ms at 120 Hz, 8.3 ms at 60 Hz. OLED pixel response (0.1 ms) is negligible vs. LCD (up to 5 ms GtG). The realistic best-case glass-to-glass on Apple Silicon at 120 fps wired is ~14 ms; at 60 fps wired ~22 ms; Wi-Fi adds 2–5 ms in both cases.

A key cross-cutting finding from the VR domain: measured total motion-to-photon latencies from the Greendayle measurement project show Virtual Desktop underreports its own latency by ~40 ms — the built-in latency indicators miss the full motion-to-photon chain. This is a general warning that system-reported latency numbers from streaming overlays (including Moonlight's Ctrl+Alt+Shift+S stats) capture network+decode but miss capture delay, compositor buffering, and display scanout — the true glass-to-glass total is always higher than the overlay shows. Slice-based encoding can shave 10–12 ms from encode latency by transmitting the first slice before the full frame is complete. The NeSt-VR paper measured VF-RTT (video frame round-trip time) of 4–5 ms as standard over Wi-Fi, confirming sub-5 ms network round-trips are achievable on LAN Wi-Fi for the transmission stage alone.

techniques.name: 240 fps streaming mode (Parsec demo)
techniques.how: At 240 fps each frame interval is 4.17 ms, so even a 2-frame encode+decode pipeline is only 8.3 ms. Parsec demonstrated total encode+transmit+decode of 4–8 ms on wired GigE LAN using NVENC H.264 with 100 Mbps cap and GTX 1070/1050Ti.
techniques.latency_impact: Total pipeline 4–8 ms at 240 fps on wired LAN. Not practical for 60 fps: at 240 fps one frame = 4.17 ms so 2 frames = 8.3 ms; at 60 fps the budget is 16.7 ms/frame. Higher frame rates compress the pipeline budget into fewer absolute ms.

techniques.name: NVENC median 5.8 ms H.264 hardware encode
techniques.how: Parsec measured median NVENC H.264 encoding latency at 5.8 ms across co-play sessions. AMD VCE was 15.06 ms (2.59× slower). Intel Quick Sync was intermediate (1.89× slower than NVENC). Hardware encoder uses NVENC low-latency or ultra-low-latency tuning mode.
techniques.latency_impact: 5.8 ms median encode for NVENC H.264. This is the best published hardware encoder number for a real streaming system. Apple Silicon Media Engine encodes HEVC at ~300 fps at 1080p (ceiling ~3.3 ms/frame) — potentially matching or beating NVENC but unconfirmed in latency-specific benchmarks.

techniques.name: VideoToolbox kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl with HEVC on Apple Silicon
techniques.how: Set `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue` in the encoderSpecification dict passed to VTCompressionSessionCreate. On Apple Silicon this works for HEVC (H.265) unlike Intel Macs (H.264 only). Enforces one-in-one-out encoding, eliminates frame reordering, adapts rate controller faster. FFmpeg patch PR #20453 confirmed support. Caveat: querying `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` afterward returns kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr (-12900) — query unsupported in low-latency mode, not proof HW acceleration is absent.
techniques.latency_impact: Apple's WWDC 2021 demo showed up to 100 ms reduction for 720p 30fps — this reflects eliminating buffering overhead. Actual per-frame encode time unmeasured publicly for Apple Silicon but ceiling is ~3.3 ms at 1080p based on 300 fps throughput figure. Critical enabler for the target stack.

techniques.name: Metal renderer instead of AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on macOS
techniques.how: Moonlight issue #1885 documents that the default AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer renderer adds 'one or more extra frames' of display latency vs. forcing Metal with VT_FORCE_METAL=1 on macOS with discrete/eGPUs. The fix is to render decoded frames directly via CAMetalLayer/Metal rather than routing through AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's compositor buffer queue. On Apple Silicon (unified memory) this is especially impactful since there is no GPU copy penalty.
techniques.latency_impact: Saves 1–2 full frame times (16.7–33.4 ms at 60 fps, 8.3–16.7 ms at 120 fps) vs AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on affected hardware. Critical finding for macOS client.

techniques.name: Sliced encoding (parallel slice transmission)
techniques.how: Split each frame into horizontal slices (2–8 typical). Each slice is encoded independently in parallel and transmitted immediately after completion without waiting for the full frame. Decoder can start decoding slice N+1 while slice N is in flight. Reduces encode+transmit pipelining latency. Supported in both H.264 and HEVC. ALVR implements this. VideoToolbox supports `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_NumberOfPendingFrames` control but direct slice control API availability is unclear.
techniques.latency_impact: Up to 25% end-to-end latency reduction, approximately 10–12 ms savings according to Haivision benchmarks. Most impactful at high resolutions where single-frame encode takes longer.

techniques.name: ALVR sub-50ms motion-to-photon pipeline design
techniques.how: ALVR targets <50 ms total motion-to-photon with a pipeline of: capture+encode 5–10 ms, network 5–15 ms, decode 5–15 ms, compositor+display 5–15 ms. Uses BitrateManager to reduce bitrate when decoder latency exceeds max_decoder_latency_ms for consecutive frames. Tracks `total_pipeline_latency`, `video_decode`, `video_decoder_queue`, `rendering`, `vsync_queue` per frame via ClientStatistics.
techniques.latency_impact: Target <50 ms total. Real-world wireless VR shows 70–140 ms (Greendayle measurements on Pico 4 Pro at 90Hz). Wired or optimal Wi-Fi 6E achieves the 50 ms target. Stage budgets: each of the 4 stages gets 5–15 ms.

techniques.name: 120 Hz display scanout to minimize scanout contribution
techniques.how: Display scanout adds on average half a frame period to photon latency (frames are scanned top-to-bottom; average pixel is displayed mid-scan). At 60 Hz average = 8.3 ms, worst-case = 16.7 ms. At 120 Hz average = 4.2 ms, worst-case = 8.3 ms. ProMotion displays with VRR can be driven at 120 Hz. Presenting frames without V-sync (immediate mode) eliminates the wait-for-next-refresh overhead but causes tearing. For a streaming client, targeting the next display refresh deadline precisely minimizes both tearing and extra wait latency.
techniques.latency_impact: Moving from 60 Hz to 120 Hz saves 4.1 ms average scanout contribution. VSync-off saves up to another full frame (16.7 ms at 60 Hz / 8.3 ms at 120 Hz) at cost of tearing. Net gain of 4–8 ms by upgrading to 120 Hz display alone.

techniques.name: Wired GigE for sub-millisecond network contribution
techniques.how: On gigabit Ethernet, a 1500-byte frame takes ~12 µs to serialize. Propagation on Cat5/6 is 0.556 µs/100m. NIC processing ~1 µs. Total one-way network latency on wired LAN is effectively <0.1 ms even accounting for switch hops. Contrast with Wi-Fi 6/6E which achieves 1–3 ms RTT (0.5–1.5 ms one-way) under ideal conditions, but can spike to 4–6 ms under load.
techniques.latency_impact: Wired LAN: <0.1 ms one-way (effectively 0 contribution to glass-to-glass budget). Wi-Fi 6/6E: 0.5–2 ms one-way typical. Wi-Fi 5 or congested: 2–10 ms. Delta between wired and Wi-Fi is 1–3 ms under good conditions, up to 10–20 ms under interference.

techniques.name: VR streaming overlay latency underreporting warning
techniques.how: The Greendayle measurement project found that Virtual Desktop's built-in latency indicator underreports total motion-to-photon by ~40 ms. System-reported latency (including Moonlight's stats overlay and ALVR dashboard) captures network round-trip + decode time but systematically misses: (1) capture pipeline delay from display compositor to CMSampleBuffer callback, (2) compositor buffering in AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer, (3) display scanout time to average pixel. True glass-to-glass is always higher than overlay numbers.
techniques.latency_impact: Awareness issue — not a technique but a calibration requirement. Overlay numbers can underreport by 20–40 ms. Use a high-speed camera against both screens, or a hardware timestamp injector, for true glass-to-glass measurement.

techniques.name: VideoToolbox HW decode with Metal rendering path (iOS/macOS)
techniques.how: Apple Silicon HW decoder via VideoToolbox achieves ~1 ms decode latency for H.264 on M2 iPad (Moonlight issue #1087 notes M2 iPad decode time is comparable to 1 ms based on responsiveness parity with gaming laptops reporting ~1 ms decode). On macOS the default AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer path adds buffering. Using `kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue` and routing to Metal via `kCVPixelBufferMetalCompatibilityKey` for zero-copy GPU access avoids extra copies.
techniques.latency_impact: HW decode: ~1–3 ms on Apple Silicon (M2+). SW decode via FFmpeg: 8–20 ms. Savings of 5–17 ms. Plus up to 2 frame savings by avoiding AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on macOS.

key_settings: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue (VTCompressionSessionCreate encoderSpecification dict) — enables low-latency HEVC encode on Apple Silicon

claims_to_verify: Parsec 4–8 ms total pipeline at 240fps — this is explicitly described as a stress-test demo and may not reflect true glass-to-glass (input+render+encode+transmit+decode+display); the blog post focuses on frame delivery consistency, not a full end-to-end measurement with a high-speed camera.

NVENC median 5.8 ms encode — this is a 2017-era measurement (GTX 1070/1050Ti, Pascal architecture); modern Ampere/Ada Lovelace NVENC is faster. Apple Silicon Media Engine encode latency remains unmeasured in any published latency-specific benchmark.

kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl works with HEVC on Apple Silicon — confirmed in an FFmpeg patch PR discussion but not in Apple's official documentation. The property query (kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) creates ambiguity about whether the hardware encoder is actually engaged.

Apple Media Engine HEVC ~300 fps at 1080p → 3.3 ms/frame ceiling — this is a throughput figure from a batch encoding context (Softron), not a latency-specific single-frame measurement. Throughput != latency; hardware encoder pipelines introduce internal buffering even in low-latency mode.

Virtual Desktop underreports by ~40 ms — from Greendayle community measurement project, not peer-reviewed. Methodology uses high-speed video comparison which is sound but replication would strengthen confidence.

AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds '1+ extra frames' on macOS — reported in Moonlight user issue #1885, not an Apple-documented characteristic. May be hardware/driver specific (observed on Radeon Pro 580 specifically). Apple Silicon behavior may differ.

macOS 26 Moonlight decode latency regression to ~80 ms — from issue #1696, a very recent report (macOS 26 = macOS Tahoe beta). This is likely a regression Apple will address; verify against stable release.
Wi-Fi 6E local RTT as low as 2 ms — best-case figure from one access point manufacturer; real-world depends heavily on driver, AP hardware, airtime utilization.

Sliced encoding 10–12 ms savings claim — from a Haivision marketing page, not a peer-reviewed measurement. The 25% figure depends heavily on baseline latency; for a 16 ms encode the savings would be ~4 ms, not 10–12 ms.
open_questions: What is the actual per-frame encode latency (not throughput) for VideoToolbox HEVC low-latency mode on M2/M3/M4 Apple Silicon? The ~3.3 ms ceiling derived from throughput is an upper bound, not a measured latency number.

Does kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl actually engage the dedicated Media Engine hardware on Apple Silicon for HEVC, or does it fall back to a software path? The kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr on the HW-acceleration query needs resolution.

What is the ScreenCaptureKit callback latency from display compositor to CMSampleBuffer delivery on Apple Silicon? The clock-domain mismatch issue (forum thread 785046) means this has not been reliably measured by any published source.
Does VideoToolbox HEVC low-latency mode support slice-based encoding? No Apple documentation confirms slice control APIs are exposed for the Media Engine hardware path.

What is the display pipeline latency for Metal CAMetalLayer presentation on Apple Silicon (M-series) — specifically from MTLCommandBuffer commit to first photon on a 120 Hz ProMotion display? This is the final missing number for the theoretical floor calculation.
On iOS/iPadOS client: does the Metal rendering path (avoiding AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer) result in measurably lower display latency vs the default path, as observed on macOS?

What is the VRR / ProMotion interaction with a streaming client at non-native frame rates (e.g., streaming at 60 fps to a 120 Hz display)? Does the display hold frames for exactly 16.7 ms or interpolate?
Parsec's 4–8 ms claim — does it include the display scanout contribution, or is it pipeline-only (encode+transmit+decode)? If pipeline-only, true glass-to-glass would add 4–8 ms display at 120 Hz.

verifications.claim: Parsec 4–8 ms total pipeline at 240fps — this is explicitly described as a stress-test demo and may not reflect true glass-to-glass (input+render+encode+transmit+decode+display); the blog post focuses on frame delivery consistency, not a full end-to-end measurement with a high-speed camera.

verdict.claim: Parsec 4–8 ms total pipeline at 240fps — this is explicitly described as a stress-test demo and may not reflect true glass-to-glass (input+render+encode+transmit+decode+display); the blog post focuses on frame delivery consistency, not a full end-to-end measurement with a high-speed camera.
verdict.evidence:
The Parsec blog post "Pushing It To The Limit — Parsec At 240 Frames Per Second With Approximately 4–8 Milliseconds Of Lag" (July 2017, authored by James Stringer) confirms two sub-claims and refutes one:

SUB-CLAIM 1 — "explicitly described as a stress-test demo": CONFIRMED. The post states verbatim: "We do not recommend using Parsec at 240 FPS. We only test the software at 60 FPS, but the test we did demonstrates the capability of our software." The extreme hardware (GTX 1070 host, GTX 1050ti client, 1 Gbps Ethernet, 100 Mbps bandwidth cap, 240 Hz monitors) is clearly a pushed-limit demonstration, not a typical deployment.

SUB-CLAIM 2 — "may not reflect true glass-to-glass (input+render+encode+transmit+decode+display)": CONFIRMED. The methodology was: two 240 Hz monitors filmed simultaneously with a 1000 FPS camera using Test UFO's flicker test; FRAPS confirmed frame rates on both machines. The 4–8 ms figure is derived arithmetically from "client is ~2 frames behind at 240fps" (1/240 s × ~2 ≈ 8.3 ms). This measures display-to-display frame delivery latency (capture → encode → network → decode → render on client display). It does NOT include input-injection latency (the time from a peripheral event on the client side being transmitted to the host, processed by the application, and rendered back). Parsec's separate input-latency test ("Testing Game Streaming Input Latency On Parsec With DIY Instructions") uses a Makey Makey board + 1000 FPS camera to measure input-to-pixel latency independently, confirming these are two distinct measurements.

SUB-CLAIM 3 — "not a full end-to-end measurement with a high-speed camera": PARTIALLY WRONG. A 1000 FPS high-speed camera WAS explicitly used (the post states "we bought two 240hz monitors and a 1000 FPS camera to record the video"). The second part — that it is not a full true-glass-to-glass measurement including input — is accurate, but the claim that no high-speed camera was used is factually incorrect.

Therefore the overall claim is partially accurate (stress-test characterization correct; not full input+pipeline latency correct) but contains a specific factual error (denying that a high-speed camera was used).
verdict.correction: The claim is mostly accurate but contains one factual error: a 1000 FPS high-speed camera WAS used in the measurement. The corrected statement should read: "Parsec 4–8 ms at 240fps is explicitly a stress-test demo (Parsec themselves say they only test at 60fps in production), and the measurement is display-to-display frame delivery latency (how many frames the client display lags behind the server display), not full glass-to-glass latency that also includes input-injection round-trip. The 4–8 ms figure derives arithmetically from '~2 frames behind at 240fps' captured via a 1000 FPS camera filming both monitors simultaneously, so the figure reflects encode+network+decode+render pipeline latency but excludes peripheral-input-to-application processing time."

verifications.claim: NVENC median 5.8 ms encode — this is a 2017-era measurement (GTX 1070/1050Ti, Pascal architecture); modern Ampere/Ada Lovelace NVENC is faster. Apple Silicon Media Engine encode latency remains unmeasured in any published latency-specific benchmark.

verdict.claim: NVENC median 5.8 ms encode — this is a 2017-era measurement (GTX 1070/1050Ti, Pascal architecture); modern Ampere/Ada Lovelace NVENC is faster. Apple Silicon Media Engine encode latency remains unmeasured in any published latency-specific benchmark.
verdict.evidence:
The claim has two sub-parts, each with different verification status:

SUB-CLAIM 1 — "5.8ms is a Pascal/GTX 1070 measurement from 2017"
PARTIALLY REFUTED. The 5.8ms figure does come from a Parsec blog post (parsec.app/blog/nvidia-nvenc-outperforms-amd-vce…), confirmed published May 1 2018, NOT 2017. More critically, the 5.8ms figure is a fleet-wide MEDIAN across all Nvidia NVENC GPUs in Parsec's real user sessions (described as "more than a quarter million recent Co-Play sessions"), not a controlled benchmark of the GTX 1070 and GTX 1050 Ti specifically. The blog post explicitly acknowledges generational differences exist but does not attribute the 5.8ms median to Pascal specifically — it is a blended average across whatever Nvidia GPUs were in the Parsec user population at the time. The GTX 1070/1050Ti were used in a separate Parsec test article (the 240fps article) to demonstrate overall pipeline latency (4–8ms total), not encode latency specifically.

The claim that "Ampere/Ada NVENC is faster" is plausible from throughput data but no published latency-specific per-frame encode-time benchmark for Ampere or Ada was found. The arXiv papers (2511.18688, 2605.01187, 2511.18686) measure end-to-end latency in frame counts (~7 frames = ~117ms at 60fps), not per-frame encode time in ms. NVIDIA's own Video Codec SDK documentation and Ada benchmark PDF (July 2023) do not publish per-frame encode latency figures.

SUB-CLAIM 2 — "Apple Silicon Media Engine encode latency remains unmeasured in any published latency-specific benchmark"
LARGELY CONFIRMED with one notable caveat. No peer-reviewed or Apple-published per-frame encode latency figure for the Media Engine exists. Apple's WWDC 2021 session (developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2021/10158/) gives only a relative improvement ("reduces delay by up to 100ms for 720p 30fps") without absolute per-frame timing. The Lumen project (github.com/trollzem/Lumen, a Moonlight fork) reports ~15ms for H.264 and ~18ms for HEVC at 1080p on M4 in its README, but this is community-measured from a streaming application, not a controlled academic or Apple-published benchmark. These are real measurements but not from a "published latency-specific benchmark" in the academic sense.

SUMMARY: The 5.8ms figure is a real Parsec-published May 2018 number, but it is a fleet median over all Nvidia GPU generations, not a controlled GTX 1070/Pascal-only measurement. The "2017-era" year is off by one year (2018). Calling it "Pascal architecture" overstates the precision — the methodology does not isolate Pascal. The statement that modern Ampere/Ada is faster per-frame remains without a published per-frame ms figure to confirm it quantitatively. The Apple Silicon Media Engine sub-claim is largely correct: no published latency-specific benchmark exists, though the Lumen project provides a community-measured ~15–18ms encode time at 1080p on M4.
verdict.correction: The 5.8ms figure is a May 2018 Parsec blog measurement, not 2017, and it is a fleet-wide median across all Nvidia GPU models in their user population at the time — not a GTX 1070/1050Ti-specific or Pascal-only controlled benchmark. The methodology explicitly pools across GPU generations. The claim that "modern Ampere/Ada is faster" is directionally reasonable but no published source provides a per-frame encode latency in milliseconds for Ampere or Ada to verify this quantitatively; all recent academic papers measure end-to-end system latency in frame counts. The Apple Silicon Media Engine sub-claim is largely accurate: no Apple-published or peer-reviewed per-frame encode latency figure exists, though the Lumen open-source streaming project reports community-measured ~15ms (H.264) and ~18ms (HEVC) encode times at 1080p60 on the M4, which are real-world but not formally benchmarked figures.

verifications.claim: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl works with HEVC on Apple Silicon — confirmed in an FFmpeg patch PR discussion but not in Apple's official documentation. The property query (kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) creates ambiguity about whether the hardware encoder is actually engaged.

verdict.claim: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl works with HEVC on Apple Silicon — confirmed in an FFmpeg patch PR discussion but not in Apple's official documentation. The property query (kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) creates ambiguity about whether the hardware encoder is actually engaged.
verdict.evidence:
The claim has three sub-assertions that need to be assessed separately:

**1. "Works with HEVC on Apple Silicon"**
This part is partially confirmed but overstated as settled. The FFmpeg patch (commit by Cameron Gutman, merged to FFmpeg master on September 10, 2025) adds HEVC support under the compile-time guard `TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC`. The commit message states: "It is supported only for H.264 on Intel Macs, but it can be used with both H.264 and HEVC on Apple Silicon." This is an empirical claim with no Apple documentation citation.

Apple's official sources contradict or are silent on HEVC support:
- WWDC 2021 session 10158 ("Explore low-latency video encoding with VideoToolbox") explicitly states: "Note, the supported video codec type in this mode is H.264." HEVC is not mentioned.
- The SDK header `VTCompressionProperties.h` (iOS 18.3, macOS 14 era) for `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` says in its @discussion: "Only High profiles. Levels are left for the encoder to automatically set." "High" is an H.264 profile concept — HEVC uses "Main"/"Main 10"/etc. The header makes no reference to HEVC.
- Apple's developer documentation page for the symbol says only "Requires that an encoder which supports low-latency operation be selected, and enables low-latency mode" with no codec specification.

So the characterization "confirmed... but not in Apple's official documentation" correctly states the evidence asymmetry, but the word "confirmed" is too strong — the FFmpeg patch is empirical engineering code that works in practice on Apple Silicon, not an Apple-confirmed API guarantee. The behavior may be hardware-dependent and could silently regress across OS versions.

**2. "The property query (kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) creates ambiguity about whether the hardware encoder is actually engaged"**
This sub-claim conflates two distinct things and is therefore imprecise.

The -12900 (`kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr`) error occurs when querying `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` on a session configured with `EnableLowLatencyRateControl`. The SDK header explicitly documents: "This is not supported in all encoders or in all encoder operating modes. kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr will be returned when this option is not supported." The Apple Developer Forums thread 751291 confirms exactly this behavior — galad87 (a community expert) confirms H.264 and HEVC are always hardware-accelerated on iOS/Apple Silicon regardless of what the property query returns. The property being unqueryable in low-latency mode is an API incompleteness, not evidence of software fallback. A separate -12902 error ("Low latency RC mode requires hardware encoder") is the actual gating error for hardware absence.

**3. API symbol spelling**
`kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` — confirmed correct in SDK headers (iOS 14.5+, macOS 11.3+, tvOS 14.5+, visionOS 1.0+). Available since macOS 11.3 Big Sur.

**Bottom line**: The claim is uncertain rather than confirmed or refuted because: (a) HEVC support via this key is empirically functional on Apple Silicon based on the FFmpeg patch and real-world use, but is explicitly undocumented and the official Apple sources only mention H.264; (b) the `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` ambiguity claim is imprecise — the error signals that the "is hardware accelerated?" property is unqueryable in low-latency mode, not that hardware is disengaged, and this distinction matters for building a reliable low-latency pipeline.
sources: https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2021/10158/ — WWDC 2021 session 10158: explicitly states only H.264 is supported in low-latency mode

https://github.com/xybp888/iOS-SDKs/blob/main/iPhoneOS18.3.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/VideoToolbox.framework/Headers/VTCompressionProperties.h#L1118-L1134 — SDK header: @discussion says 'Only High profiles' (H.264 concept), no HEVC mention
https://www.mail-archive.com/ffmpeg-cvslog@ffmpeg.org/msg68397.html — FFmpeg commit merged 2025-09-10: adds TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC condition, empirical claim with no Apple doc citation
https://www.mail-archive.com/ffmpeg-devel@ffmpeg.org/msg185545.html — FFmpeg patch PR discussion by Cameron Gutman

https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/751291 — Apple Developer Forums: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder returns -12900 in low-latency mode; confirmed H.264/HEVC always HW-accelerated on Apple Silicon
https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/684262 — Apple Developer Forums: -12902 error 'Low latency RC mode requires hardware encoder' is the actual gating error for HW absence
verdict.correction: The claim should be rephrased as: "kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is officially documented for H.264 only (WWDC 2021 + SDK headers); empirical evidence from the FFmpeg project (patch merged September 2025) shows it also works with HEVC on Apple Silicon under the compile-time condition TARGET_CPU_ARM64, but this is not documented or guaranteed by Apple. The kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr (-12900) that arises when querying kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder in low-latency mode does NOT indicate hardware is disengaged — the SDK explicitly warns this property is unsupported in certain encoder operating modes, and Apple forums confirm HEVC is always hardware-accelerated on Apple Silicon. The actual hardware-absence gating error is -12902 ('Low latency RC mode requires hardware encoder'). For a production low-latency screen-sharing stack targeting macOS 14+ Apple Silicon, setting this key for HEVC is likely to work in practice but carries risk of silent regression across OS updates, since it relies on undocumented behavior."

rawCorpus.summary:
**The low-latency encoder API is H.264-only — HEVC must be configured manually.** Apple's `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` (available macOS 11.3+, introduced WWDC21) is explicitly documented for H.264 only. The SDK header for `kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing` (macOS/iOS 26+) also requires that flag. For HEVC, you must manually replicate the low-latency regime: set `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = true`, `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering = false` (disables B-frames), `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP = false` (HEVC defaults to Open-GOP which the SDK header warns may introduce cross-GOP dependencies), `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval = 0` (encoder chooses — set to a very large number or rely on `kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceKeyFrame` on-demand for infinite GOP), `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount = 0` (default kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount = -1 allows buffering; 0 forces immediate output), `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality = true` (macOS 11+, supported by H.264 and HEVC HW encoders per the header), and `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ExpectedFrameRate` set to the target FPS. The Apple header explicitly lists the ultra-low-latency conferencing recipe as the combination of all five of these settings. Note that `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel` must be disabled when low-latency rate control is active.

**Tiles, slices, and WPP are irrelevant for Apple's hardware encoder.** VideoToolbox exposes `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxH264SliceBytes` for H.264 (byte-size limit per slice, for MTU alignment) but there is no equivalent tile or WPP control for HEVC in the public VideoToolbox API. Apple's fixed-function Media Engine does not expose tile/WPP configuration — these are software-encoder constructs (x265, NVENC) used to parallelize CTU-row processing. The hardware encoder's pipeline is already internally parallel and presents a single output NAL. Tiles and WPP therefore have no actionable surface in this stack and should not be a design factor. The only relevant parallelism knob is `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RecommendedParallelizationLimit` (macOS 14+), which is for offline multi-session encode parallelism, not real-time streaming.

**H.264 vs HEVC decode latency on Apple clients.** Evidence from Moonlight issue #1087 shows M2 iPad achieves ~1ms decode time while M2 Mac achieves ~4-6ms with the same codec, both using Apple hardware. Moonlight issue #1885 shows `AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer` (the indirect path Moonlight uses on Mac) adds noticeable display latency versus a direct Metal/CAMetalLayer render path with `VT_FORCE_METAL=1`. This implicates the render path, not the codec choice, as the dominant Mac decode-side latency factor. For a custom all-native stack that renders directly via Metal with `CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage` into a `CAMetalLayer` (as Moonlight's `VTMetalRenderer` does), this ~5ms penalty is avoided. On that renderer, H.264 and HEVC hardware decode latency on Apple Silicon are both sub-1ms on the Media Engine itself — the codec choice is not the dominant factor. Moonlight community notes that some older non-Apple-Silicon devices decoded H.264 faster than HEVC because they lacked a dedicated HEVC Media Engine and fell back to GPU-shader decode, but this is irrelevant for Apple Silicon targets (M1+). HEVC Main 10 carries no meaningful additional decode latency versus HEVC Main 8 on Apple Silicon because the same hardware block handles both.

**Frame rate strategy is a high-leverage latency knob.** At 60 fps each frame interval is 16.7ms; at 120 fps it is 8.3ms. This halves the worst-case frame-arrival latency floor. ScreenCaptureKit's `SCStreamConfiguration.minimumFrameInterval` can be set to `1/120` on ProMotion displays (M1 Max/Pro and later MacBook Pro, M3 iMac, all M-series Mac Pro/Studio driving 120Hz monitors) and confirmed working on macOS 15.3.1 with M1 Max at 120 fps. The key constraint is that the frame must be fully processed within the interval or ScreenCaptureKit drops it and the latency advantage is lost. Capturing above the display's physical refresh rate (e.g., 240fps on a 120Hz display) yields no additional frames — ScreenCaptureKit gates delivery at the actual refresh rate. Encoding at 120fps with a fixed QP or constrained bitrate is feasible on Apple Silicon given the Media Engine can sustain 300+ fps for HEVC 1080p. Intra-refresh versus IDR: for a LAN-only stack where packet loss is negligible, IDR-on-demand (infinite GOP) is strictly superior — it provides instantaneous clean recovery at any point the client requests, whereas periodic intra-refresh smears recovery across multiple frames and degrades quality during the refresh window. Entropy coding: `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_H264EntropyMode` allows choosing CAVLC vs CABAC for H.264. CAVLC decodes with less computational overhead (no arithmetic state machine), but on Apple's fixed-function hardware decoder the difference is immeasurably small and CABAC delivers ~7% better compression at no practical latency cost. HEVC mandates CABAC, and there is no entropy mode selection knob for HEVC in VideoToolbox.

techniques.name: Set kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl (H.264 only)
techniques.how: Pass in the encoderSpecification dict to VTCompressionSessionCreate: set kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue. This automatically enforces: infinite GOP (all-P after IDR), no frame reordering, High profile, temporal layer support. Combine with kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = true and kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality = true per the Apple SDK header recipe. Not applicable to HEVC — returns kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr on HEVC sessions.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — eliminates frame reordering (100ms reduction at 720p30 per WWDC21), eliminates look-ahead buffering, enables instant rate-control adaptation

techniques.name: Manual HEVC low-latency config (replaces EnableLowLatencyRateControl for HEVC)
techniques.how: Since EnableLowLatencyRateControl is H.264-only, set these on a HEVC VTCompressionSession: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue; kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering = kCFBooleanFalse; kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP = kCFBooleanFalse (macOS 10.14+; HEVC defaults to true — must be explicitly disabled); kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval = INT_MAX (infinite GOP); kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount = 0 (forces immediate output — default kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount=-1 allows encoder buffering); kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality = kCFBooleanTrue (macOS 11+); kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ExpectedFrameRate = target fps. Use kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceKeyFrame on demand for IDR recovery.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — without AllowOpenGOP=false and AllowFrameReordering=false, HEVC encoder may buffer multiple frames for B-frame lookahead and inter-GOP references, adding tens of ms

techniques.name: Set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount = 0
techniques.how: VTSessionSetProperty(session, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount, CFNumberCreate(0)). Default is kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount (-1), meaning the encoder may hold arbitrary frames before emitting output. Setting to 0 forces 'before the call to encode frame N returns, frame N must have been emitted.' This is the single most direct API for eliminating encoder-side buffering latency.
techniques.latency_impact: high — eliminates encoder output buffering delay; the default -1 value can add multiple frames of latency at the encoder's discretion

techniques.name: Render via Metal/CAMetalLayer with CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage, NOT AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer
techniques.how: On the decoder (client) side, use VTDecompressionSession → CVPixelBuffer → CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage → bind as MTLTexture → encode Metal render command → present via CAMetalLayer. Avoid AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on macOS, which routes through an internal compositor and adds a full vsync cycle of latency. On macOS, AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer's indirect rendering path adds several ms vs the direct Metal path. The Moonlight VTMetalRenderer (vt_metal.mm) demonstrates this exact pattern using CAMetalLayer + CVMetalTextureCache.
techniques.latency_impact: high — Moonlight reports AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds noticeable cursor lag vs VT_FORCE_METAL=1 on the same hardware (issue #1885). Metal direct path avoids compositor queuing.

techniques.name: Capture at 120fps via SCStreamConfiguration.minimumFrameInterval = 1/120
techniques.how: Set SCStreamConfiguration.minimumFrameInterval = CMTimeMake(1, 120) on ProMotion-capable Mac hosts. This halves per-frame interval from 16.7ms to 8.3ms, directly cutting worst-case frame delivery latency by ~8ms. Requires: (a) host display supports 120Hz (M1 Max/Pro MacBook Pro, M3 iMac, external 120Hz display), (b) the encoding pipeline completes within 8.3ms or ScreenCaptureKit drops the frame. Confirmed working on M1 Max macOS 15.3.1 at 120fps. Capturing beyond the display's physical refresh rate yields no additional frames.
techniques.latency_impact: high — reduces frame-interval latency contribution from ~16.7ms to ~8.3ms (~8ms saving); also reduces average capture-to-encode delay by half a frame interval (~4ms)

techniques.name: Enable Long-Term Reference (LTR) frames instead of periodic IDR
techniques.how: Set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR = kCFBooleanTrue (macOS 12+). Encoder emits a token via kVTSampleAttachmentKey_RequireLTRAcknowledgementToken when it creates an LTR frame. Client ACKs receipt via kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_AcknowledgedLTRTokens. On corruption or packet loss, trigger kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh instead of a full IDR. LTR-P frames are smaller than IDR frames (no intra-coded penalty), so recovery events cost less bitrate and time. Combined with infinite GOP (MaxKeyFrameInterval=INT_MAX), this means no automatic IDR events interrupt the stream.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — eliminates periodic IDR overhead (IDR frames are 3-10x larger than P-frames, causing bitrate spikes and encoder-side buffering); on LAN with near-zero loss, the main benefit is eliminating any accidental periodic IDR the encoder might insert

techniques.name: Prefer IDR-on-demand over periodic intra-refresh for LAN
techniques.how: For a LAN-only stack, use infinite GOP (MaxKeyFrameInterval=INT_MAX) + ForceKeyFrame on demand rather than periodic intra-refresh. Periodic intra-refresh (PIR) smears recovery across N frames (e.g., a 1-second intra-refresh at 60fps takes 60 frames to complete a full refresh), during which the stream contains mixed intra/inter blocks producing visible quality degradation. On LAN where packet loss is <0.01%, the recovery-latency benefit of PIR is irrelevant and the quality cost is real. IDR-on-demand provides instantaneous clean recovery at the moment it is needed.
techniques.latency_impact: medium — for LAN, PIR only adds complexity and quality artifacts; IDR-on-demand provides the same recovery semantics with lower average bitrate and no multi-frame degradation window

techniques.name: HEVC profile: kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main10_AutoLevel for 10-bit, kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main_AutoLevel for 8-bit
techniques.how: Set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ProfileLevel. Use kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main10_AutoLevel for Main 10 / 4:2:0 10-bit (macOS 10.13+). Use kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main_AutoLevel for 8-bit. The _AutoLevel suffix delegates level selection to the encoder, which is correct for streaming since level constrains max luma/chroma sample rate and bitrate. Do NOT pin a specific level — let the encoder choose. For 1080p/120fps or 4K/60fps, the encoder will auto-select Level 5.1 or 5.2. Lower levels (4.1/4.2) would constrain throughput and could cause the encoder to drop frames.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — profile choice doesn't directly affect encode latency; incorrect level can cause encoder to throttle; AutoLevel avoids this

techniques.name: H.264 entropy mode: CAVLC for marginal decode speed, CABAC for compression efficiency
techniques.how: Set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_H264EntropyMode = kVTH264EntropyMode_CAVLC or kVTH264EntropyMode_CABAC. CABAC offers ~7% better compression than CAVLC at higher computational cost. On Apple Silicon's fixed-function hardware decoder, both paths are handled in dedicated silicon with negligible latency difference. CAVLC is incompatible with H.264 High Profile (requires Main or Baseline). For this project (HEVC primary codec, H.264 fallback), the practical guidance is: use CABAC with H.264 High Profile for better bitrate efficiency; only consider CAVLC if you observe decode CPU load issues on legacy iOS clients that lack an HEVC Media Engine.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — on Apple Silicon HW decoder, CAVLC vs CABAC decode latency difference is sub-millisecond; the compression efficiency benefit of CABAC (~7% bitrate) is the more significant factor

techniques.name: HEVC tiles and WPP: not applicable to VideoToolbox
techniques.how: VideoToolbox exposes no tile or WPP configuration for HEVC. Apple's Media Engine implements these internally in fixed-function silicon. There is no kVTCompressionPropertyKey_HEVCTiles or equivalent. Attempting to control parallelism at this level via VideoToolbox is not possible. This is a non-issue: the hardware encoder and decoder already operate at maximum internal throughput regardless of tile/WPP settings. For reference, software HEVC encoders show WPP achieving 8.7x speedup and tiles 9.3x on 12-core systems, but these benefits are already baked into Apple's hardware pipeline.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — no API surface exists; hardware encoder/decoder already internally parallel

techniques.name: Use kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaximumRealTimeFrameRate for burst-rate encoder readiness
techniques.how: Set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaximumRealTimeFrameRate to the maximum possible input rate (e.g., 120 for 120fps capture). Available macOS 15+/iOS 18+. This informs the encoder of the peak rate at which frames could arrive, allowing it to pre-configure internal buffers for that throughput. Distinct from ExpectedFrameRate (the average). For a 120fps capture pipeline this ensures the encoder does not under-configure its pipeline for burst scenarios.
techniques.latency_impact: marginal — prevents encoder from being under-configured for burst-rate frames, which could cause queuing; primarily a readiness hint

techniques.name: New macOS/iOS 26 presets: kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing
techniques.how: Available macOS 26+ / iOS 26+. Query kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SupportedPresetDictionaries and apply the kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing preset. Per the SDK header, this preset requires EnableLowLatencyRateControl=true and encapsulates the recommended low-latency settings bundle. This is the official Apple-blessed low-latency encoder configuration path going forward. Combine with kVTCompressionPreset_HighSpeed for maximum throughput.
techniques.latency_impact: critical — official Apple preset for ultra-low-latency conferencing/gaming; bundles all the optimal low-latency settings automatically

key_settings: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue (H.264 encoder spec dict, macOS 11.3+)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering = kCFBooleanFalse
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP = kCFBooleanFalse (HEVC-critical: defaults to true; must be explicitly disabled, macOS 10.14+)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount = 0 (kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount=-1 is the default and allows buffering)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality = kCFBooleanTrue (macOS 11+, supported by H.264 and HEVC HW encoders)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval = INT_MAX (infinite GOP; use kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceKeyFrame on demand)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR = kCFBooleanTrue (macOS 12+; use kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh for recovery)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ExpectedFrameRate = 60 or 120
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaximumRealTimeFrameRate = 120 (macOS 15+/iOS 18+; peak burst rate hint)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ProfileLevel = kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main10_AutoLevel (for 10-bit HEVC)
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_H264EntropyMode = kVTH264EntropyMode_CABAC (H.264 fallback; use with High profile)
SCStreamConfiguration.minimumFrameInterval = CMTimeMake(1, 120) (120fps capture on ProMotion host displays)
Render path: CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage into CAMetalLayer (NOT AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer on macOS)
kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing (macOS 26+ / iOS 26+; new unified preset for ultra-low-latency)
latency_numbers: 100ms: reduction in encode latency at 720p30 from eliminating frame reordering (WWDC21 session 10158)
16.7ms → 8.3ms: frame interval at 60fps vs 120fps; upgrading to 120fps saves ~8ms worst-case frame delivery latency (blur busters / mathematical identity)
~1ms: HEVC hardware decode time on M2 iPad (Moonlight issue #1087, iOS using AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer direct path)
~4-6ms: HEVC hardware decode time on M2 Mac with Moonlight default AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer indirect path (Moonlight issue #1087)
~80ms: pathological macOS 26.0 decoding latency regression reported on Intel Iris Plus with Moonlight 6.1.0 (Moonlight issue #1696 — macOS 26 regression, not Apple Silicon)
Several ms: latency added by AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer vs direct Metal render on macOS (Moonlight issues #1885; qualitative, no exact number published)
~7%: CABAC bitrate advantage over CAVLC at same quality (standard reference, multiple papers on ResearchGate)
8.7x encode speedup from WPP vs single-thread on 12-core system (Fraunhofer HHI / ResearchGate — software encoder only, not applicable to VideoToolbox HW)
claims_to_verify: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is documented as H.264-only in WWDC21 — verify whether macOS 14/15/26 SDK has extended it to HEVC (the SDK header text as of MacOSX26.5 still says 'supported video codec type: H.264' but does not explicitly say HEVC is excluded in the newer revision)

kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP defaults to kCFBooleanTrue for HEVC — the SDK header says 'For HEVC encoding the property value is kCFBooleanTrue by default' at line 190-191; verify this is still the default behavior on macOS 14+ and iOS 17+ hardware encoders

kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount = 0 semantics: SDK says 'A value of zero implies default behavior' in one source but the header at line 570-574 says it 'limits the number of frames that may be held in the compression window. If M=0, before encode frame N returns, frame N-0=N must have been emitted.' — verify whether 0 means 'no limit' (default) or 'immediate emit'

M2 iPad ~1ms decode time vs M2 Mac ~4-6ms decode time for HEVC: these numbers come from Moonlight community reports (issue #1087), not Apple-published benchmarks — verify whether the gap persists with a direct Metal render path on Mac or if it is entirely attributable to AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer

kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing requires EnableLowLatencyRateControl=true per the macOS 26.5 SDK header — verify whether this means it is also H.264-only or whether macOS 26 extends the preset/flag to HEVC

ScreenCaptureKit at 120fps confirmed on M1 Max macOS 15.3.1: sourced from an Apple Developer Forums thread (thread/785046); verify whether the 120fps delivery rate is only achievable when the host display runs at 120Hz or if it can be achieved with a 60Hz display and minimumFrameInterval=1/120
open_questions: Does macOS 26 extend kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl to HEVC? The new kVTCompressionPreset_VideoConferencing (macOS 26) requires this flag — if it now supports HEVC, the manual HEVC low-latency config would be superseded.
What is the actual encode latency of the Apple Silicon Media Engine for HEVC Main 10 1080p at 60fps and 120fps in the low-latency configuration? No authoritative Apple-published number exists.

Is kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SuggestedLookAheadFrameCount (macOS 15+, macOS-only) relevant? The header says it is ignored when EnableLowLatencyRateControl=true. For the HEVC manual path without that flag, setting this to 0 might be needed to prevent look-ahead buffering.

For the iOS/iPadOS client, does the Moonlight-observed ~1ms decode time for HEVC replicate when using VTDecompressionSession directly (as opposed to AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer), and does the same hold for the macOS client with a Metal render path?

Does kVTCompressionPropertyKey_OutputBitDepth (macOS 12.3+) need to be set explicitly to 10 for HEVC Main 10 or does kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main10_AutoLevel imply it? The header says 'absence assumes highest bit depth allowable by profile', which would be 10-bit for Main10, but explicit setting avoids ambiguity.

Is there a way to signal tile entry points or dependent slices in HEVC via VideoToolbox for lower decoder-side pipeline latency? Current evidence suggests no public API exists, but Apple may expose internal flags through VTSessionCopySupportedPropertyDictionary that are undocumented.

verifications.claim: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is documented as H.264-only in WWDC21 — verify whether macOS 14/15/26 SDK has extended it to HEVC (the SDK header text as of MacOSX26.5 still says 'supported video codec type: H.264' but does not explicitly say HEVC is excluded in the newer revision)

verdict.claim: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is documented as H.264-only in WWDC21 — verify whether macOS 14/15/26 SDK has extended it to HEVC (the SDK header text as of MacOSX26.5 still says 'supported video codec type: H.264' but does not explicitly say HEVC is excluded in the newer revision)
verdict.evidence:
Three independent authoritative sources were checked:

1. SDK headers (primary, authoritative): The `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` docblock in `/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/SDKs/MacOSX26.5.sdk/.../VTCompressionProperties.h` lines 1187–1202 contains NO mention of "H.264" or "supported video codec type" anywhere. The identical comment text appears verbatim in the macOS 15.4 SDK at lines 1119–1134. The phrase "supported video codec type: H.264" does NOT appear in the SDK header at all — in either version. This directly refutes the sub-claim that "the SDK header text as of MacOSX26.5 still says 'supported video codec type: H.264'".

2. WWDC21 session 10158 (origin of H.264-only claim): The session transcript does state verbatim: "Note, the supported video codec type in this mode is H.264, and we're bringing this feature on both iOS and macOS." This is the correct origin of the H.264-only claim — a spoken statement in a 2021 WWDC video, not the SDK header.

3. FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c (empirical implementation evidence): A committed patch (commit d87210745e, landed in FFmpeg master) explicitly enables low-latency rate control for HEVC on Apple Silicon with the condition `(TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC)`. The commit message states: "It is supported only for H.264 on Intel Macs, but it can be used with both H.264 and HEVC on Apple Silicon." The current FFmpeg master (github.com/FFmpeg/FFmpeg libavcodec/videotoolboxenc.c line 1715) confirms this is the live codepath: `((avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_H264) || (TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC))`.

The net result: the SDK header (in all versions checked: macOS 15.4 and 26.5) says nothing about codec restrictions — no "H.264" and no "HEVC" restriction — making the quoted header text in the claim a fabrication. HEVC support via this key is practically confirmed on Apple Silicon by both FFmpeg's empirical evidence and the absence of any SDK-level prohibition.
sources: https://www.mail-archive.com/ffmpeg-cvslog@ffmpeg.org/msg68397.html (FFmpeg commit d87210745e: 'avcodec/videotoolboxenc: allow low latency RC with HEVC')
https://www.mail-archive.com/ffmpeg-devel@ffmpeg.org/msg185545.html (FFmpeg patch review: allow low latency RC with HEVC on Apple Silicon)
verdict.correction:
The claim contains two distinct errors:

(a) The SDK header does NOT say 'supported video codec type: H.264'. That phrase appears only in the WWDC21 video transcript (session 10158), not in VTCompressionProperties.h. Both macOS 15.4 and macOS 26.5 SDK headers for this constant are codec-agnostic — the docblock says nothing about H.264 or HEVC restrictions.

(b) HEVC support for kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl IS functionally available on Apple Silicon (ARM64), not just H.264. This is confirmed by FFmpeg's shipping implementation: the key is applied for HEVC when TARGET_CPU_ARM64 is true (commit d87210745e). On Intel Macs, the WWDC21 claim of H.264-only still holds.

Corrected statement: 'kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl was announced as H.264-only at WWDC21 (transcript: "the supported video codec type in this mode is H.264"). As of macOS 15.4 and macOS 26.5 SDKs, the header docblock contains no codec restriction text at all. Empirical evidence from FFmpeg (commit d87210745e) demonstrates that on Apple Silicon (ARM64), the key works for both H.264 and HEVC. Apple has not updated the SDK header to document this extended HEVC support officially, but the restriction is a hardware limitation (Intel only), not a permanent API restriction.'

verifications.claim: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP defaults to kCFBooleanTrue for HEVC — the SDK header says 'For HEVC encoding the property value is kCFBooleanTrue by default' at line 190-191; verify this is still the default behavior on macOS 14+ and iOS 17+ hardware encoders

verdict.claim: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP defaults to kCFBooleanTrue for HEVC — the SDK header says 'For HEVC encoding the property value is kCFBooleanTrue by default' at line 190-191; this is still the default behavior on macOS 14+ and iOS 17+ hardware encoders
verdict.evidence:
Three independent SDK headers on the local machine all carry the identical documentation comment confirming the default and the exact quoted text:
